(1) tests/test_sg_parity.py:63-71 asserts only 4 outputs (prices, cognitive_prices, num_substitutions, total_wealth) at one seed, no Null A/B, no S=1. (2) The YH005 suite (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/tests/test_parity.py) checks 5 seeds x 8 parity keys, 6 round_trips arrays, 3 order-size arrays, per-agent final_wealth, S=1, Null A/B, plus invariants. (3) pyproject.toml:41 testpaths=["tests","packages"] excludes imported/, and packages/ contains zero test files; CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:24) runs `pytest -m "not slow"`, so the weak single-seed test is the ONLY parity guard in CI (the statistical 5% test is slow-marked and skipped). (4) No mitigation exists: the canonical package (packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/reference.py, vectorized.py) implements all the unasserted surfaces (round_trips at reference.py:297-318, num_orders_by_size at :120/:288-321, final_wealth at :295, history_mode/decision_mode at :72-79/:138/:183), so a regression confined to e.g. the Null-B per-agent RNG loop or round-trip logging in vectorized.py would pass workspace CI. Aggravating fact the auditor missed: even if collected, the YH005 suite would not guard the canonical package — it sys.path-imports the experiment-local model.py/simulate.py (test_parity.py:26-29), so the richer assertions were never ported to cover packages/abm_models/sg at all. Severity: not a live wrong-result today (Null paths currently bit-match), so not critical; but the Stage-B go/no-go gate and docs/backbone_parity.md stake correctness of the actively-evolving canonical SG on bit-parity that CI does not actually enforce — major is calibrated.

findings_verified.title: No automated guard that canonical SG stays identical to the YH005 source of truth, unlike the classical models
findings_verified.detail: tests/test_classical_parity.py loads imported YH001-004 model.py files by path and asserts bit-identical outputs against the packages/ copies, but no equivalent guard exists for SG, the canonical model per ADR 0001. packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/{reference,vectorized,history}.py are currently identical to YH005/{model,simulate,history}.py except one import line each (verified by diff), yet nothing enforces this against YH005. The existing SG tests only partially cover: test_sg_reference_vectorized_bit_parity (fast) guards vectorized against the packaged reference, so a lone vectorized.py edit is caught — but an edit to reference.py itself, to both backends, or to YH005 breaks the documented byte-identity with no failing test. The findings-parity test is slow-marked (excluded from the default/CI suite per pyproject.toml) and its 5% tolerance is far too loose to catch e.g. a tie-break-scale behavioral change (ties occur at ~3.5% of close events). YH005's local 'from history import ...' dependency explains why it was skipped from the classical parity pattern, but a sys.path-based dynamic import (or a source-normalization/byte-comparison test) would close the gap.
findings_verified.evidence: tests/test_classical_parity.py:23-30 _load_original(yh) loads imported model.py by path and tests CB/LM/MG/GCMG only; grep for 'sg|speculation|YH005' in that file matches nothing but the imported/ path constant.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add test_sg_original_parity that _load_original('YH005') (model.py and simulate.py) and asserts np.array_equal on all output keys against packages run_reference/simulate at small scale, mirroring the classical pattern.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all factual claims. (1) tests/test_classical_parity.py dynamically imports imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH00{1..4}/model.py by path and asserts bit-identical arrays for CB/LM/MG/GCMG only; no SG test of that pattern exists anywhere in tests/ (grep for YH005/speculation in tests/ matches only comments and the path constant). (2) I re-diffed: packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/reference.py vs YH005/model.py and sg/vectorized.py vs YH005/simulate.py differ only in one import line each ('from .history' vs 'from history'); sg/history.py vs YH005/history.py is byte-identical. So the byte-identity claim in docs/backbone_parity.md ("実測は 0.00%、byte-identical 移植") currently holds but is unenforced against YH005. (3) The 5% findings test (test_sg_parity.py::test_sg_findings_parity_within_5pct) is @pytest.mark.slow and pyproject.toml line 43 documents slow as excluded in CI by default — so it never runs in the standard 74-test suite, and even when run, 5% relative tolerance would not catch a tie-break-scale change. One correction to the auditor's detail: it is not true that the slow test is "the only cross-implementation check" — test_sg_parity.py::test_sg_reference_vectorized_bit_parity (lines 63-71, not slow-marked) asserts bit-identity between the packaged reference and vectorized backends. This partially mitigates: an edit to vectorized.py alone would fail against reference.py. But it is intra-package only — an edit to reference.py (the YH005 copy itself), a coordinated edit to both backends, or an edit to YH005 in imported/ leaves no failing test while silently invalidating ADR 0001's canonicity claim and backbone_parity.md's 0.00% claim. Severity major is calibrated correctly: not critical (no wrong results today — files are verifiably identical), but a real gap around the highest-priority actively-developed research line (YH001..YH008) whose documented parity claims are load-bearing, and where the sibling classical models do get exactly this guard. Note the classical parity test docstring explains those models were testable this way because they have "no local dependencies"; YH005/model.py does a flat 'from history import ...', which makes path-based dynamic import slightly harder (needs sys.path insertion or package-style loading) but not impossible — so the gap is explainable but not justified.

findings_verified.title: FW implementation is not Franke-Westerhoff (2012) DCA/TPA: no discrete-choice/logit switching, no herding term, ad-hoc |return|*100 rule
findings_verified.detail: The FW adapter (extracted byte-identical from imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/fw.py) is not an FW-2012 DCA/TPA implementation: no switching index a_t, no logit fractions, no nu*exp(±a) transition probabilities, no alpha_n herding term (absent from the entire package), alpha_w repurposed from wealth to misalignment coefficient, ad-hoc |return|*100 scaling (model.py:196), hard [0.05,0.95] clipping (model.py:206), and level-price rather than log-price demands. Despite this, the module docstring (lines 3-7), describe_complexity() (lines 149-153), the wrapper __init__.py ('herding/sentiment'), and fingerprint_atlas/methods.py:145-154 all attribute it to Franke & Westerhoff 2012 — the atlas entry additionally mislabels alpha_o as 'herding'. Tests (tests/test_prism_family_parity.py) only guard bit-identity with the PRISM original, not paper fidelity. ADR 0001 (line 37) and backbone_parity.md (lines 47-49) already flag the FW-family lineage as separate/deferred, which is why this is major (mislabeled retired-lineage model actively fingerprinted under a false citation) rather than critical (canonical/active research code is unaffected).
findings_verified.evidence:
p_to_chart = p.alpha_o + p.alpha_p * abs(returns[t]) * 100
p_to_fund = p.alpha_o + p.alpha_w * abs_mispr
... n_c = max(0.05, min(0.95, n_c))
vs docstring lines 3-7: 'Based on Franke & Westerhoff (2012) ... endogenous sentiment-driven switching via transition probabilities' and describe_complexity(): '(Franke & Westerhoff 2012)'
findings_verified.suggestion: Either implement true DCA (logistic n_c with alpha_o + alpha_n herding + alpha_p misalignment-squared, log-price demands, FW's published parameter estimates) or rename/redescribe the model as an FW-inspired sentiment toy so the atlas provenance is honest.
findings_verified.verify_note: All specific claims verified against packages/abm_models/abm_models/franke_westerhoff/model.py. Lines 196-206 use ad-hoc flow probabilities p_to_chart = alpha_o + alpha_p*|r_t|*100 (hardcoded x100 not in any FW-2012 variant) and p_to_fund = alpha_o + alpha_w*|mispricing| (alpha_w, FW's wealth coefficient, repurposed as misalignment), with hard clipping of n_c to [0.05, 0.95]. There is no discrete-choice logit (DCA), no nu*exp(±a) transition-probability approach (TPA), no switching index a_t, and grep confirms no alpha_n herding term exists anywhere in the package — the mechanism FW-2012 identify as generating volatility clustering is absent. Demands are level-price (lines 168-181), not log-price. Yet the docstring (lines 3-7), describe_complexity() (lines 149-153), and __init__.py ("herding/sentiment") all attribute the model to Franke & Westerhoff (2012). The defect is not mitigated: tests/test_prism_family_parity.py only asserts bit-identity with the PRISM original (from which the code was extracted verbatim, byte-identical simulation core), not fidelity to the paper; no 'simplified' caveat exists in either file. The drift propagates downstream: packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/methods.py:145-154 fingerprints the model with reference 'Franke & Westerhoff 2012 (J. Econ. Dyn. Control)' and even mislabels the parameters (calls alpha_o 'herding' when alpha_o is FW's predisposition and herding is the absent alpha_n). docs/adr/0001 line 37 and docs/backbone_parity.md lines 47-49 acknowledge the PRISM FW-family lineage is separate and its treatment deferred, but do not correct the FW-2012 attribution. Severity major is calibrated: not critical because this is retired PRISM lineage (not the canonical SG/YH005 active research line) and outputs are internally consistent, but the misattribution is live in atlas fingerprint/complexity metadata, so it will bite any research artifact citing these results as FW-2012.

findings_verified.title: CI describe_complexity and docstring claim a continuous double auction with limit-order book; the code has no order book
findings_verified.detail: CONFIRMED with one impact-chain correction: the MDL-weighting consumer of ComplexitySpec exists only in the retired PRISM lineage (imported/PRISM/src/prism/pipeline.py:349,512); nothing in packages/, experiments/, or tests/ reads describe_complexity(), so description_length=9.0 currently feeds no active computation. The live harm channels are (1) the identical false 'continuous double auction with limit orders / order matching' mechanism description hard-coded in the active fingerprint atlas at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/methods.py:121-131, which is injected verbatim into LLM annotation/literature-matching prompts (methods_draft.py), and (2) the canonical REGISTRY entry name 'chiarella_iori' (packages/abm_models/abm_models/__init__.py:47) implying CIP-2009 fidelity for what is a reduced-form price-impact proxy (model.py lines 203, 221-225). A fix should update both the model docstring/describe_complexity and the methods.py mechanism text (or clearly relabel the model as a CI-inspired reduced form).
findings_verified.evidence: structural_description=("Continuous double-auction model with limit-order book, heterogeneous agents (fund/chart/noise), and order-matching price discovery (Chiarella, Iori & Perelló 2009)") vs _simulate_one_path(): `fill_price = best_ask + p.price_impact * net_order / (ask_depth + 1e-6)` and `best_bid = fill_price - spread / 2` (lines 203, 221)

findings_verified.verify_note: Direct read of packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py confirms the mismatch exactly as reported: docstring (lines 1-11) and describe_complexity() (lines 148-157) claim a continuous double auction with limit-order book and order-matching price discovery, but _simulate_one_path() (lines 159-241) is a reduced-form scalar model — one aggregate net_order per step pushed through a price-impact formula (line 203: fill_price = best_ask + p.price_impact * net_order / (ask_depth + 1e-6)), best bid/ask mechanically reset to fill ± fixed spread (lines 221-222), scalar bid/ask depth with ad-hoc 0.95/0.05 mean-reversion (lines 224-225), and three aggregate strategy weights evolving via a performance rule (lines 227-239) rather than a heterogeneous agent population. There are no orders, no book, no matching. One correction to the auditor's impact chain: within this workspace, describe_complexity()/description_length is consumed only by the retired PRISM pipeline (imported/PRISM/src/prism/pipeline.py:349,512); no code under packages/, experiments/, or tests/ reads ComplexitySpec, so the MDL-weighting channel is dormant. However, the finding's substance survives because the same false 'continuous double auction / limit order / order matching' mechanism text is duplicated in the ACTIVE fingerprint atlas (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/methods.py:121-131) and is fed verbatim into the LLM annotation and literature-matching prompts (methods_draft.py), and the model is the canonical 'chiarella_iori' REGISTRY entry (packages/abm_models/abm_models/__init__.py:47). So the misdescription actively contaminates research metadata, not just comments. Severity major is roughly calibrated given the active atlas-prompt consumption; it would drop to minor if the atlas mechanism text were corrected independently, since the simulation numerics themselves are internally consistent.

(2) run_aggregate_c0.py lines 33-36 do sys.path.insert(0, YH005) then sys.path.insert(0, HERE), so HERE=YH006 ends up first on sys.path; since YH006/analysis is a regular package it shadows YH005/analysis.py (which does exist and holds the real plot_* functions). (4) No mitigation exists: nothing else in YH006 imports run_aggregate_c0, and the YH006/tests/ suite (test_aggregate_parity, test_parity, test_roundtrip_invariants, test_wealth_conservation) never exercises this import path. (5) Consequences check out: outputs/ does not exist (and is .gitignore'd, line 6 of YH006/.gitignore ignores outputs/), so the documented c0u_metrics.json / c0p_metrics.json are absent, while the ten results_c0{u,p}_*.png figures ARE committed in YH006/ root — the committed figures cannot be regenerated by the committed script. SPEC.md ~line 251 indeed references analysis.stylized_facts (CCDF, moments) as functional, which is drift against the 0-byte stub. Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: this is the user's own active research line (highest priority) and a genuine reproducibility break, but it fails loudly with an ImportError rather than silently producing wrong results, so it does not rise to 'critical'.

findings_verified.title: plot_hold_ratio estimates N from max step-sum of action counts; recorded run shows N_est=983 for N=1000, biasing all published action ratios
findings_verified.detail: plot_hold_ratio (packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py:318 and byte-identical imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/analysis.py:318) estimates N as the max over steps of num_buy+num_sell+num_active_hold+num_passive_hold, which is exact only if some step has zero idle agents. In the recorded YH005_1 run (phase1_mechanism_figures.py, N=1000), outputs/phase1_metrics.json records N_est=983, so the four action ratios in the metrics JSON and Fig.10-equivalent figure are inflated by 1000/983 (~1.7%) and the idle fraction is underestimated (0.0876 recorded vs ~0.103 true, ~15% relative). Exact N is available in the same sim_result as len(sim_result["final_wealth"]) (returned by both sg/vectorized.py and sg/reference.py). The same estimator is independently re-implemented (not imported) in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/compare_figure.py:266, so YH006 comparison figures carry the same bias.
findings_verified.evidence:
N_est = int((num_buy + num_sell + num_act + num_pas).max())  # core.py:318
num_idle = np.clip(N_est - (num_buy + num_sell + num_act + num_pas), 0.0, None)
Recorded: YH005_1/outputs/phase1_metrics.json contains "N_est": 983 while the run used N=1000
findings_verified.suggestion: Use N = len(sim_result['final_wealth']) (or pass N explicitly) instead of the max-heuristic; regenerate the hold-ratio figures/metrics.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every element of the claim checks out. (1) packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py:318 computes N_est = int((num_buy + num_sell + num_act + num_pas).max()); the comment on lines 316-317 explicitly assumes "max 値の和が N", which is only true if at least one of the T steps has zero idle agents. (2) The counters are per-agent mask sums over exactly N agents (packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/vectorized.py:213-218), so the step-sum is <= N always, never > N; N_est can only underestimate. (3) The recorded artifact imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005_1/outputs/phase1_metrics.json shows params.N = 1000 and hold_ratio.N_est = 983 for the same run (driven by phase1_mechanism_figures.py, PARAMS N=1000, calling plot_hold_ratio at line 130), proving the underestimation actually occurred: every one of the 50000 steps had >=17 idle agents. Consequences in the recorded JSON/figure: buy/sell/active_hold/passive_hold fractions divide by 983 instead of 1000, inflating each by factor 1000/983 (~1.73%, e.g. recorded buy 0.21200 vs true 0.20840), and idle is underestimated (recorded 0.08757 vs true ~0.1031, ~15% relative error). (4) The exact N is indeed available in the same sim_result: both sg/vectorized.py (line 242) and sg/reference.py (line 311) return "final_wealth" of length N. (5) The byte-identical copy exists at imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/analysis.py:318, and YH006/compare_figure.py:266-275 re-implements the same flawed estimator independently (so it "propagates" by duplication rather than by import — a minor nuance, not a refutation). No test or caller mitigates this; nothing passes true N in. Severity: major is calibrated — it is a real defect that already biased recorded outputs of the active research line (YH005_1) and canonical package code, but it is a diagnostic/plotting helper with ~1.7% bias on the four action ratios (and the field is honestly labeled N_est), not corruption of simulation results themselves, so not critical.

findings_verified.title: No ground-truth tests for any estimator; the only test exercising the stylized-facts package is slow-marked (excluded from the default suite) and circular (same implementation on both sides)
findings_verified.detail: The stylized_facts package (Hill, ACF, kurtosis, CCDF, and all five figure/metric functions) has no unit tests against known distributions anywhere in the workspace (packages/stylized_facts/ has no tests dir; tests/ contains none). test_sg_findings_parity_within_5pct is @pytest.mark.slow, hence excluded from the passing 74-test suite, and even when run it compares stylized_facts_summary output against RECORDED numbers that were produced by the byte-identical YH005/analysis.py — so it can only detect copy drift, never estimator wrongness (the plot_hold_ratio N_est bias and the ACF normalization variant would both pass it forever). The fast bit-parity test (line 63) does not touch stylized-facts code at all. I verified externally that _acf recovers AR(1) phi^k and hill_mle_tail_index recovers alpha=3 on Pareto samples — such checks would be cheap deterministic unit tests.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add fast ground-truth unit tests: ACF of a seeded AR(1) vs phi^k within tolerance, Hill alpha on seeded Pareto samples, kurtosis_windowed on Gaussian ~0, plus NaN-handling and edge-case tests (max_lag >= len, all-equal values). These run in milliseconds and would guard the canonical estimators in the default suite.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every claim checks out. (1) packages/stylized_facts/ has no tests (only pyproject.toml + source); testpaths=["tests","packages"] and nothing in tests/ unit-tests the estimators against known distributions. (2) test_sg_findings_parity_within_5pct (tests/test_sg_parity.py:36) is @pytest.mark.slow and CI runs `uv run pytest -m "not slow"` (.github/workflows/ci.yml:24); collection with that marker selects only the bit-parity test from this file (73 tests total), which never imports stylized_facts. (3) Circularity verified by diff: the first 130 lines of packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py are byte-identical to imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/analysis.py, the code that generated the RECORDED values — so even when run, the test compares an implementation against its own historical output and cannot detect estimator wrongness (e.g. the _acf pairwise-NaN approximation at core.py:36-43 or the N_est heuristic at core.py:318 would pass forever). (4) No mitigation elsewhere: the only ground-truth estimator tests in the repo (imported/PRISM/tests/unit/test_facts.py, imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/tests/unit/test_sf_battery.py) test different implementations (prism.facts, toy.sf_battery) and live outside testpaths, so they never run in the workspace suite. Severity 'major' is calibrated: this is a coverage gap, not a demonstrated wrong result (so not critical), but the canonical parity claims (docs/backbone_parity.md, spec 001 AC2 go-no-go gate) rest entirely on these unverified estimators in active research code, and cheap deterministic ground-truth tests (AR(1) phi^k for ACF, Pareto alpha for Hill) would close it.

findings_verified.title: execute_proposal indexes NaN-filtered fingerprint matrix with an unfiltered run-list index
findings_verified.detail: execute_proposal (propose_cli.py:258-279) builds fps_std from only the all-finite fingerprints but indexes it with new_idx computed by enumerating the unfiltered all_runs list. Because load_runs orders by id and the new run is always the last row, any earlier run with a NaN fingerprint makes new_idx exceed len(fps_std), causing an IndexError at fps_std[new_idx] (line 272, when predicted_fingerprint is set) or D[new_idx] (line 279) — a guaranteed crash, not the silent wrong-run comparison originally claimed. The crash occurs after insert_run() but before update_proposal_status(), so the run row is committed while the proposal remains 'approved'; retrying inserts a duplicate run, and the DB is then permanently unable to execute any proposal until the NaN row is removed. Mitigating context: the CI-rebuilt DB (populate + populate_refs with the pinned seeds) was verified empirically to contain zero NaN-fingerprint rows among 126 runs, so the path is currently latent; but NaN fingerprints are an explicitly designed outcome (fingerprint() returns all-NaN for short/degenerate series; hill returns NaN for constant returns), collect_population() exists to filter them, and LLM-proposed extreme parameter sweeps are the likeliest source of such degenerate runs. Fix: use collect_population (or filter all_runs first) so the row list and fps_std stay aligned, and move insert_run + update_proposal_status closer together (or compute metrics before insert).
findings_verified.evidence:
fps_all = np.vstack([r["fingerprint"] for r in all_runs
                     if np.all(np.isfinite(r["fingerprint"]))])
fps_std, mu_feat, sd_feat = standardize(fps_all)
new_idx = next((i for i, r in enumerate(all_runs)
                if r["id"] == run_id and np.all(np.isfinite(r["fingerprint"]))), None)
...
actual_std = fps_std[new_idx] if new_idx is not None else ...
findings_verified.suggestion: Compute new_idx over the filtered list (e.g. build kept = [r for r in all_runs if finite]; new_idx = next(i for i,r in enumerate(kept) if r['id']==run_id)), or use db.collect_population() which returns aligned (fps, kept_rows). Wrap insert_run + update_proposal_status so a metric failure cannot leave the run/proposal pair inconsistent.
findings_verified.verify_note: The index misalignment is real. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/propose_cli.py lines 258-279: fps_all (line 259-260) stacks only all-finite fingerprints, but new_idx (lines 262-263) is the enumeration index into the UNFILTERED all_runs list; that index is then applied to the filtered arrays at fps_std[new_idx] (line 272) and D[new_idx] (line 279). Nothing re-maps the index (contrast db.py collect_population, lines 475-489, which exists precisely to keep fps and rows aligned — execute_proposal does not use it). NaN fingerprints are a designed possibility: fingerprint() returns all-NaN for <50 finite obs (fingerprint.py line 151), hill_tail_index returns NaN for degenerate series (lines 66-74), and insert_run/load_runs round-trip NaN via JSON null (db.py 395, 451). One refinement to the claim: load_runs orders by id (db.py line 441) and run_id is the freshly inserted max id, so the new run is always the LAST element of all_runs; with k>=1 prior NaN rows, new_idx = len(all_runs)-1 >= len(fps_std) = len(all_runs)-k, so the failure is always an IndexError, never the silent wrong-row computation the auditor also claimed. The state-inconsistency consequence is confirmed: insert_run (line 247) commits before the crash and update_proposal_status (line 281) is never reached; the only try/except (lines 235-243) ends before this block, so the proposal stays 'approved'/'proposed' and re-execution inserts a duplicate run (cmd_auto line 360 and cmd_execute line 307 just swallow/log the exception). Severity calibration: I empirically rebuilt the DB exactly as the CI workflow does (.github/workflows/propose.yml: populate --n-per-model 12 --seed-base 1000 --rng-seed 7, populate_refs synthetic + real) — all 126 runs have fully finite fingerprints, so the bug is currently latent, and when it fires it crashes loudly rather than silently corrupting research results. That fails the 'critical = wrong results/data corruption' bar; it is a real defect likely to bite (LLM-proposed extreme params are exactly the inputs that produce degenerate/NaN runs, and a single NaN row would permanently break every subsequent execute against that DB while leaking duplicate runs), hence major.

findings_verified.title: Preference/UCB loop is not NaN-safe: one NaN-fingerprint row poisons ridge fit and ranks NaN candidates first
findings_verified.detail: propose_next_k vstacks r['fingerprint'] from labeled/unlabeled rows without filtering non-finite rows (unlike atlas.py, which routes through collect_population). PreferenceModel.fit uses plain X.mean/X.std (not nan-aware), so a single NaN feature in any labeled row makes A = Xa.T@Xa contain NaN and np.linalg.solve returns all-NaN weights — every mu prediction becomes NaN, so score is all-NaN and the top-k ranking is meaningless. Even with clean labels, a NaN-fingerprint UNLABELED candidate gets score=NaN; np.argsort places NaN last, and the [::-1] reversal (line 181) then ranks NaN-scored candidates FIRST, so the labeling session is fed exactly the degenerate runs. NaN fingerprints are an expected data condition in this DB (fingerprint() returns NaN axes for short/degenerate series).
findings_verified.evidence:
X_lab = np.vstack([r["fingerprint"] for r in labeled_rows]) if labeled_rows else np.empty((0, 0))
...
self.mean_ = X.mean(axis=0)   # fit(): not nanmean
...
order = np.argsort(score)[::-1]   # NaN scores sort last, reversal puts them first
findings_verified.suggestion: Filter both pools through collect_population() (or np.all(np.isfinite(fp))) before fitting/scoring, keeping row alignment; alternatively treat NaN scores as -inf before argsort.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three claimed failure modes are real and reachable. (1) NaN fingerprints are an expected DB condition: fingerprint() returns NaN axes for degenerate series and db.py:395 stores non-finite values as JSON null (row is kept, not skipped); load_runs (db.py:451) round-trips null back to NaN. (2) The consuming path label_cli.label_next (label_cli.py:258-266) passes load_runs output to propose_next_k with no isfinite filter — unlike atlas.py (collect_population, db.py:484) and propose_cli.py:260-263, which do filter. (3) preference.py:152/154 vstacks raw fingerprints; standardize() uses nanmean/nanstd for column stats but leaves NaN entries in the matrix, so PreferenceModel.fit (preference.py:61-62, plain X.mean/X.std) produces NaN mean_, NaN A = Xa.T@Xa, and np.linalg.solve returns all-NaN w_ — all mu and all scores become NaN when any labeled row has a NaN feature. (4) With clean labels, an unlabeled NaN-fingerprint candidate still gets mu = NaN (novelty stays finite via nansum, but NaN + finite = NaN); np.argsort places NaN last and the [::-1] at preference.py:181 ranks NaN-scored rows FIRST, so the top-k proposals are exactly the degenerate runs. Partial mitigations only: the cold-start branch (n_lab < 2) uses nansum distances and is NaN-robust, and _verbal_summary (label_cli.py:58) prints a "(NaN in features)" warning so the human labeler can skip — but the ridge path (normal operation after 2 labels) misbehaves exactly as claimed and tests only cover finite fingerprints. Severity major is calibrated: it corrupts the active-learning proposal ordering (wasted labeling effort, biased label set) but does not directly corrupt canonical research results and a human is in the loop.

findings_verified.title: Replaying a 'real' run for labeling re-fetches Yahoo data anchored at now(), showing a different window than was fingerprinted
findings_verified.detail: _replay_series for origin='real' (label_cli.py:115) re-fetches Yahoo closes with cache_dir=None, so it always downloads a fresh window anchored at the current time (real_refs.fetch_yahoo_closes: end=int(time.time()), start=end-years), ignoring any cache used at populate time. The stored window_start_idx/window_len are then applied to this calendar-shifted return series, so the plotted PNG shows a window displaced by however many trading days elapsed since populate_refs ran (the _full rows drift too). The mismatch is silent: the plot title carries no dates. Correction to the original claim: the verbal summary and feature vector printed by _format_row come from the DB-stored fingerprint and remain consistent with the row — only the plot drifts. Since the auto-opened plot is the primary visual input for the snap preference judgment, labels written for real-market rows weeks/months after populate are attached to fingerprints of a different market window than the one shown, biasing the human-preference ground truth for the (small) set of real landmark rows. Impact scales with the populate-to-label delay; same-day labeling is essentially unaffected.
findings_verified.evidence:
ts, closes = real_refs.fetch_yahoo_closes(symbol, years=years, cache_dir=None)
rets = real_refs.log_returns(closes)
if "window_start_idx" in r["params"]:
    s = int(r["params"]["window_start_idx"])
    w = int(r["params"]["window_len"])
    rets = rets[s:s + w]
findings_verified.suggestion: The row's params already store first_ts/last_ts — slice by timestamp instead of by index, and/or reuse the populate-time cache_dir. As a guard, recompute fingerprint(series) on the replayed window and warn if it disagrees with the stored vector.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified end-to-end. label_cli._replay_series (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/label_cli.py:111-121) calls real_refs.fetch_yahoo_closes with cache_dir=None; in real_refs.py:45-51 that makes cache_path None, so the cache branch is skipped and a fresh download always occurs with end=int(time.time()), start=end-years — even if populate_refs was run with --cache-dir. populate_real stores window_start_idx/window_len (real_refs.iter_reference_runs, lines 137-150) as indices into the return series fetched at populate time; replaying later applies the same integer index to a series whose calendar anchor has advanced, so rets[s:s+w] (label_cli.py:117-120) covers a shifted calendar window (the _full rows drift too). The plot shows no dates (only id/model/seed), so the mismatch is silent. Path is reachable: cold_start/label_next call _replay_series whenever show_plot=True (default), and origin='real' rows exist via populate_real; _replay_series has no other callers and no tests guard it. One inaccuracy in the original detail: the verbal summary (読み) and printed feature vector come from the DB-stored fingerprint (_format_row, lines 179-189), NOT the fresh fetch — only the plotted PNG drifts. Since the PNG is the primary snap-judgment input per the module docstring, the corruption of real-row preference labels when labeling is delayed stands.

findings_verified.title: min_relevance_to_keep discards the completed extraction, losing the score and guaranteeing re-extraction cost on every future ingest
findings_verified.detail: When relevance_score < min_relevance_to_keep, ingest() skips update_literature_extraction entirely (arxiv_ingest.py:262-269), discarding the completed extraction including the score itself — despite the inline comment "(do NOT delete row; mark with low score)" stating the opposite intent. Because extracted_by_model stays NULL, the already_extracted check (lines 246-249) fails on later runs and the same paper is re-sent to Groq and re-dropped. This bites in production: the weekly scheduled workflow .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml passes --min-relevance-to-keep 0.3 and persists DB state via a JSON snapshot, so below-threshold papers are re-extracted weekly for as long as they remain in the newest-30 query window (bounded, not literally forever — and a run where the stochastic LLM score crosses 0.3 will then persist the paper, making outcomes seed-dependent). Consequences: recurring silent Groq spend/rate-limit pressure, and the DB cannot distinguish "known irrelevant" from "never extracted". Fix: write the extraction (with its low score) and let downstream consumers filter via load_literature(min_relevance=...), which already exists.
findings_verified.evidence:
if (ext["relevance_score"] is not None
        and ext["relevance_score"] < min_relevance_to_keep):
    n_dropped += 1
    ...
    continue   # extraction result never written; row stays extracted_by_model=NULL
findings_verified.suggestion: Always persist the extraction (the relevance filter belongs at query time — load_literature already supports min_relevance). If you must suppress the fields, at least record extracted_by_model + relevance_score so the skip check short-circuits future runs.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified in code: at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py:262-269, a successful extraction with relevance_score < min_relevance_to_keep hits `continue` before update_literature_extraction, so the paid extraction (including the low score) is discarded and extracted_by_model stays NULL. The inline comment on line 262 literally says "(do NOT delete row; mark with low score)" yet the code never marks the row — the implementation contradicts its own stated intent. The already-extracted skip (lines 246-249) tests extracted_by_model, so the same paper is re-extracted via Groq on every subsequent ingest that returns it. This is not a dormant opt-in path: .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml line 103 runs the weekly scheduled ingest with --min-relevance-to-keep 0.3 and round-trips DB state through data/literature_methods.json (preserving the NULL), so the recurring re-extraction happens in the actual production automation. No test covers the drop path and no caller mitigates it. Minor correction: "forever" is overstated — the weekly preset sorts by submitted date with max=30, so a dropped paper is only re-hit while it stays in the newest-N window (and a stochastic score crossing 0.3 on a later run would persist it), bounding the recurring cost to a few weeks per paper. The defect (lost score, DB unable to distinguish known-irrelevant from never-extracted, repeated API spend) stands.

findings_verified.title: cmd_auto formats prediction_error/novelty with :.2f, crashing on None and mislabeling a successfully executed proposal as FAILED
findings_verified.detail: cmd_auto (propose_cli.py:355-359) formats prediction_error/actual_novelty_distance with :.2f. Both are legitimately None: prediction_error when the proposal lacks predicted_fingerprint (validator check at propose.py:440 is conditional; enforcement is prompt-only), actual_novelty_distance when the new run is the only finite-fingerprint run (guaranteed for the first proposal executed against a fresh runs DB — summarize_corpus works with zero runs) or when the new fingerprint has NaN entries. The TypeError is swallowed by the broad except at propose_cli.py:360, so a successfully executed proposal (run row inserted at 247, status='executed' at 281) is printed as FAILED, appears in BOTH results and failures (double-counted in the summary at 364), forces exit code 1, and a retry is refused by the status guard at 222-225. DB state remains correct; the defect is in loop reporting/exit code, not data.
findings_verified.evidence:
r = execute_proposal(args.db, pid, seed=seed, verbose=False)
...
print(f"  ✓ [...] pred_err={err:.2f} nov={nov:.2f} ...")
except Exception as exc:
    failures.append({"proposal_id": pid, "error": str(exc)})
findings_verified.suggestion: Format defensively (e.g. f"{err:.2f}" if err is not None else "n/a") and narrow the except around execute_proposal only, not the reporting line.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every link in the claimed chain checks out in the code. (1) None is a legitimate return value: in execute_proposal (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/propose_cli.py:266-267) prediction_error stays None unless p["predicted_fingerprint"] is truthy, and the validator (propose.py:440, `if "predicted_fingerprint" in p and p["predicted_fingerprint"] is not None:`) explicitly tolerates its absence — enforcement is prompt-only (SYSTEM_PROMPT says MUST, but gpt-oss-120b JSON mode does not schema-enforce it), so accepted proposals without a prediction are possible. (2) actual_novelty_distance stays None (propose_cli.py:275-279) when the new run is the only finite-fingerprint run (`len(all_runs) > 1` fails) or when the new fingerprint contains NaN (new_idx is None). summarize_corpus (propose.py:316-356) works fine with zero runs (n_runs=0), so `atlas auto` on a fresh DB is reachable and its FIRST executed proposal deterministically has actual_novelty=None. (3) The crash: propose_cli.py:357-359 formats `pred_err={err:.2f} nov={nov:.2f}`; with None this raises `TypeError: unsupported format string passed to NoneType.__format__`, which the broad `except Exception` at line 360 catches, appending to failures and printing `✗ ... FAILED` — after execute_proposal has already inserted the run row (line 247) and set status='executed' (lines 281-286). The summary line (364) and exit code (367/385 return 1 if failures) therefore misreport a successful execution, and a retry via `execute` is refused by the status guard at lines 222-225 ("status is 'executed'; refusing to execute"). Note r is appended to results at line 354 BEFORE the format, so the same proposal is counted in both `executed` and `failed`, an additional inconsistency. No test covers cmd_auto or the None case (tests/test_propose.py only uses non-None values, lines 110/330). Severity 'major' is calibrated: it is a real crash that bites deterministically on a fresh atlas DB and stochastically when the LLM omits predicted_fingerprint, corrupting the loop's reporting and exit code in tooling (fingerprint_atlas pipeline) — but the DB state itself remains correct, so it is not 'critical'.

findings_verified.title: Propose workflow never restores the literature snapshot, so weekly proposals run with an empty literature corpus
findings_verified.detail: propose.yml (.github/workflows/propose.yml:48-61) rebuilds the DB with populate, populate_refs and methods_cli seed only; it never loads data/literature_methods.json into the literature_methods table, and no code path outside ingest_arxiv.yml reads that snapshot. Since the SQLite DB is deliberately not committed, every propose run sees an empty literature_methods table. propose.py:337-340 swallows this (bare except → literature_all = []) and classify_references' defensive ensure_literature_schema (propose.py:196) prevents any error, so _select_literature_for_context injects zero papers into the Groq context and the prompt rule 'references[] is MANDATORY when literature[] is non-empty' (propose.py:106) silently deactivates. This contradicts ingest_arxiv.yml's PR body (lines 136-138) which promises the snapshot is 'the source of truth for the literature DB — re-run any propose workflow and these papers will be available as LLM context.' Note: data/literature_methods.json does not yet exist in the repo, so the defect is currently latent; it manifests on the first propose run after an ingest PR is merged (both workflows are weekly-scheduled and sequenced ingest→propose). Fix: add a 'Materialise DB from snapshot' step (copy of ingest_arxiv.yml lines 63-96) to propose.yml after the rebuild step.
findings_verified.evidence: propose.yml 'Rebuild DB' step: populate / populate_refs / methods_cli seed only — no equivalent of ingest_arxiv.yml's 'Materialise DB from snapshot' step; propose.py:338 'literature_all = load_literature(db_path)' with defensive ensure_literature_schema at line 196 that hides the emptiness.
findings_verified.suggestion: Copy the 'Materialise DB from snapshot' step from ingest_arxiv.yml into propose.yml before the Groq step, or factor it into a shared script; add a CI assertion that n_literature_total > 0 when data/literature_methods.json exists.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all three legs of the claim. (1) propose.yml's 'Rebuild DB from scratch' step (lines 54-61) runs only fingerprint_atlas.populate, populate_refs, and methods_cli seed; grep confirms none of these modules writes to the literature_methods table, and a repo-wide grep for literature_methods.json shows the snapshot is referenced only in ingest_arxiv.yml — propose.yml has no equivalent of ingest_arxiv.yml's 'Materialise DB from snapshot' step (lines 63-96). (2) The DB is not committed (the workflow comment explicitly says it is regenerated to avoid committing SQLite; data/ is absent from git), so every propose run starts with an empty literature_methods table. (3) propose.py summarize_corpus (line 338) loads literature via load_literature inside a bare try/except returning [], and classify_references calls ensure_literature_schema defensively (line 196), so the emptiness is silent; _select_literature_for_context then injects zero papers and prompt rule 6 ('references[] MANDATORY when literature[] is non-empty', line 106) deactivates. propose_cli from-corpus adds no snapshot restore either. No test or caller mitigates this. One nuance: data/literature_methods.json does not yet exist in the repo, so the defect has zero observable effect until the first ingest PR is merged — but both workflows run on weekly cron (ingest Sunday 21:00 UTC, propose Monday 09:00 UTC, deliberately sequenced), so the handoff breaks as soon as the pipeline operates as designed, exactly contradicting ingest_arxiv.yml's PR-body claim (lines 136-138).

findings_verified.title: stylized_facts — the measurement instrument for every parity and findings claim — has no dedicated unit tests
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as reported, with one strengthening correction: the finding says the slow-marked parity test is the only functional exercise, but even that test is weaker than a validity check — docs/backbone_parity.md documents that packages/stylized_facts is a byte-identical port of YH005's analysis code, and the RECORDED reference values in test_sg_parity.py come from that same lineage (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/outputs/baseline_metrics.json). So the slow test can only catch regressions introduced during/after extraction, never an estimator bug inherited from YH005 (wrong Hill order-statistic k, biased ACF normalization, excess- vs raw-kurtosis confusion), which would reproduce at 0.00% relative error indefinitely. Combined with ci.yml:24 excluding slow tests, hill_mle_tail_index, _acf, volatility_acf, kurtosis_windowed and stylized_facts_summary in packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py have zero validation against analytic ground truth and zero execution in fast CI, despite producing every headline metric in the repo's recorded findings.
findings_verified.evidence: tests/ listing contains no stylized_facts test; the only imports of stylized_facts in tests are in test_sg_parity.py, whose functional use sits under @pytest.mark.slow (line 36) which ci.yml:24 excludes.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add fast unit tests with analytic references: Hill α on Pareto samples within tolerance, return_acf of iid noise ≈ 0 with correct CI band, kurtosis_windowed on N(0,1) ≈ 0, log_returns_from_prices on a geometric ramp exact.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every factual element of the finding checks out. (1) tests/ contains no test_stylized_facts.py (listing: test_arxiv_ingest, test_citation_and_draft, test_classical_parity, test_config, test_fingerprint_atlas, test_methods, test_preference, test_prism_family_parity, test_propose, test_provenance, test_registry_protocol, test_rng, test_sg_parity — nothing for stylized_facts). (2) packages/stylized_facts contains only pyproject.toml and stylized_facts/{__init__.py, core.py} — no package-local tests either. (3) The only test importing stylized_facts is tests/test_sg_parity.py (line 17); the sole function that actually executes stylized_facts_summary/log_returns_from_prices is test_sg_findings_parity_within_5pct, which carries @pytest.mark.slow (line 36), and .github/workflows/ci.yml line 24 runs `uv run pytest -m "not slow"` — so in CI the estimators are import-collected but never executed. (4) No analytic ground-truth tests exist anywhere (no Hill-on-Pareto, ACF-of-white-noise, or Gaussian-kurtosis checks). Crucially, the apparent mitigation is weaker than it looks: the slow parity test compares against RECORDED values from YH005's baseline_metrics.json, but docs/backbone_parity.md states the package is a "byte-identical 移植" of YH005's analysis code — the recorded numbers were produced by the same estimator lineage, so this test only guards the port against regression, not estimator correctness (a wrong Hill k or biased ACF normalization inherited from YH005 would pass at 0.00% rel error forever). And even that regression guard is excluded from CI. Severity: "major" is correctly calibrated — this is not itself a wrong-result defect (so not critical), but it is the highest-leverage coverage gap in a repo whose canonical claims (RECORDED, docs/backbone_parity.md, all experiment headline metrics) are numbers emitted by these unvalidated estimators, and the known tie-break discrepancy already shows this lineage can harbor silent divergences.

findings_verified.title: Partial-close fills contribute zero ΔG and zero wealth: the filled quantity's P&L is silently dropped
findings_verified.detail: When a close partially fills (e.g. 4 of 10), reconcile only shrinks entry_quantity to the residual (6) and counts num_partial_closes; no round trip is recorded, G is not credited, and sg_wealth is not updated for the 4 units actually closed. When the residual finally closes, dG = entry_action*(close_price_cog_final - entry_price_cog) is applied to the residual quantity ONLY, priced at the final close step. So the partially-closed units' cognitive P&L vanishes from both the strategy-selection signal (G) and the wealth dynamics (Fig A Pareto tail), and total realized ΔG×q is not conserved against executed volume. The module docstring acknowledges the bookkeeping ('部分 close は entry_quantity を actual fill 分だけ残す') but not the P&L leak; SPEC §3.2(c) only says to record the partial rate. Measured frequency in my instrumented run: 58 partial closes vs 4805 RTs (~1.2%), so the standalone bias is modest — but the same code path is also what finding #1 funnels corrupted states through, and the semantics should be fixed together.
findings_verified.evidence: speculation_agent.py:372-376: 'elif abs(actual_vol) < self.entry_quantity: self.position = 1 if actual_vol > 0 else -1; self.entry_quantity = abs(actual_vol); self.num_partial_closes += 1; self.close_partial_matches += 1' — no G/sg_wealth/round_trips update for the filled portion; full credit only at :345-348 'dG = ...; self.G[self.active_idx] += dG; self.sg_wealth += int(dG) * int(self.entry_quantity)' using the residual quantity.
findings_verified.suggestion: On partial close, book a round trip for the filled quantity at the current close_price_cog (w += dG_partial * q_filled, G += dG_partial per YH005 convention) and keep the residual open with the original entry price; or explicitly document the drop as a modeling choice and report the leaked ΔG×q as a metric.
findings_verified.verify_note: Direct code reading confirms the claim. In imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/speculation_agent.py, the partial-close branch of _reconcile (lines 372-376) only sets position, shrinks entry_quantity to the residual fill, and increments diagnostic counters; it records no round trip and updates neither G nor sg_wealth for the units actually closed. The full-close branch (lines 343-356) later credits dG = entry_action*(close_price_cog - entry_price_cog) with sg_wealth += dG * entry_quantity, where entry_quantity is by then the residual only and close_price_cog was overwritten (line 289) with the final close step's price — so the partially-closed units' cognitive P&L is permanently dropped from both the strategy-selection signal (G) and wealth dynamics, and cumulative ΔG×q is not conserved against executed volume. No mitigation exists: SPEC.md §3.2(c) (lines 84-88) only requires recording the partial-fill rate; the module docstring (line 9) documents the residual bookkeeping but not the P&L semantics; and tests/test_wealth_conservation.py is a self-described weak invariant whose only assertions are sg_wealth >= 0, < 10^7, and that sum(dG*q) is numeric — it cannot catch this leak. The path is reachable by design (MARKET_ORDER against a thin opposite book), and the counters are surfaced in run_experiment.py and measurement_7_all.py precisely because partial closes occur. The only refutation angle — that leaving the round trip open until fully closed is intended semantics — fails because even under that reading the final credit should use the originally opened quantity (or the partial fill should be credited at its own close price); as written the filled units vanish under either interpretation. Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: genuine numerical defect in the user's active research line (YH006) biasing wealth/Fig-A results and G, but modest magnitude (~1.2% of round trips), confined to an imported experiment rather than the canonical packages/ SG (ADR designates YH005 as canonical).

findings_verified.title: README claims a LIMIT_ORDER (mid±10%, ttl=3) mitigation that does not exist anywhere in the code
findings_verified.detail: YH006/README.md:183-184 (Limitations, 'LOB artifact (Phase 1 既知)') claims the ~30% zero-fill-open problem was handled by replacing orders with LIMIT_ORDER (mid±10%, ttl=3) and that this avoided the book O(N²) path. No such replacement exists: SpeculationAgent submits only MARKET_ORDER, and its docstring (speculation_agent.py:13-29) documents the real O(N²) mitigation as the opposing-liquidity guard + self-cancel. LIMIT_ORDER appears only in mm_fcn_agent.py (FCN market-makers, ttl=time_window_size, order_margin pricing — not mid±10%/ttl=3); 'mid±10'/'ttl=3' occur nowhere else in the repo, and git history shows LIMIT_ORDER was never in speculation_agent.py. findings.md:165, the root README, and YH006_1 GLOSSARY all consistently treat the LIMIT_ORDER extension as future work (YH006_2) and the zero-fill loss as an open, 'fatal-for-Fig 11-13' limitation. The README should say the O(N²) path was avoided via the opposing-liquidity guard + self-cancel, and that the LIMIT_ORDER (mid±n%, ttl=k) replacement remains planned for YH006_2 with zero-fill still unmitigated.
findings_verified.evidence: README.md:183-184: 'zero-fill open 率 ≈ 30 % (流動性不足で不一致)。LIMIT_ORDER (mid±10 %, ttl=3) で置換し book O(N²) 経路は回避済。' vs speculation_agent.py:13 '設計 A′: MARKET_ORDER + self-cancel + opposing-liquidity guard' and :267-273 'kind=MARKET_ORDER'. grep -rn LIMIT_ORDER over YH006/YH006_1 matches only mm_fcn_agent.py.
findings_verified.suggestion: Correct the README to describe the implemented guard design (or, if a LIMIT variant was tried and abandoned, say so explicitly and note which variant produced the published numbers).
findings_verified.verify_note: Every element of the finding checks out against the code and surrounding docs. (1) imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/README.md:183-184 states, in the Limitations section describing Phase 1 known LOB artifacts, that the ~30% zero-fill-open problem was addressed by replacing orders with 'LIMIT_ORDER (mid±10 %, ttl=3)' and that the book O(N²) path is thereby '回避済' (already avoided). (2) The actual code contradicts this: speculation_agent.py's module docstring (lines 13-29) documents design A′ as 'MARKET_ORDER + self-cancel + opposing-liquidity guard' and explicitly explains that the O(N²) book-explosion path (priority_queue.remove O(N) × cancels, book 300→1600) was fixed by the opposing-liquidity guard, not by limit orders; the file imports only MARKET_ORDER (line 52) and never LIMIT_ORDER. A repo-wide grep confirms LIMIT_ORDER appears in code only in mm_fcn_agent.py (the FCN market-makers, with ttl=self.time_window_size and order_margin pricing — not mid±10%, not ttl=3), and the strings 'mid±10' / 'ttl=3' appear nowhere except this README line. Git pickaxe (-S LIMIT_ORDER) over speculation_agent.py history returns nothing, so no earlier LIMIT_ORDER version ever existed. (3) The project's own findings.md:165 lists the 'LIMIT_ORDER open 拡張' as prospective ('MARKET-only から LIMIT mid±n% / ttl=k への拡張で改善見込み') and calls the 30% zero-fill loss '致命的' (fatal) for reproducing paper 1 Figs 11-13; the root README and YH006_1 GLOSSARY both assign the LIMIT_ORDER extension to the *planned* YH006_2. So the README asserts as completed a mitigation that the rest of the repo consistently treats as future work, while the actually-executed mitigation (guard + self-cancel) is different and leaves the zero-fill problem unmitigated. One nuance: the '回避済' claim about the O(N²) path is substantively true (it was avoided) — the falsehood is attributing it to a LIMIT_ORDER replacement and implying the zero-fill issue was addressed. Severity 'major' is calibrated: this is the research record for the active YH006 2x2 line; a reader (including the researcher at Phase 2 / write-up time) would conclude a still-open, self-described 'fatal' liquidity limitation was already fixed, and would misstate the executed order mechanism in any methods write-up. It does not corrupt computed results, so it is not critical.

findings_verified.title: L2 shrinkage ratio divides two statistically-zero interactions; 'ratio ≈ 1.0 refutes hypothesis A' over-reads noise
findings_verified.detail: compute_shrinkage (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/aggregate_ablation_summary.py:214-221) forms ratio = |a1_mean|/|s3_mean| guarding only against machine zero (1e-10), not statistical zero. Every S3 baseline interaction 95% CI in outputs/tables/tab_S3_interaction.csv contains 0, so the ratio divides two noise-level means and is arbitrarily unstable (0.092 to 3.806 across the five metrics in tab_S5_shrinkage.csv). The L2 pass gate itself is sound (requires the paired shrinkage CI to exclude 0; all metrics correctly fail), but the README (lines 172, 178) over-reads: it presents "Spearman ratio 1.006 / Kendall ratio 1.090 (≈1.0)" as 直接反証 (direct refutation) that the funnel's monotonic structure derives from q heterogeneity, and line 297 promotes this to a Phase 2 main finding motivating S6. The statistically defensible statement is only that neither the S3 nor A1 interaction is distinguishable from zero at n=100 trials — an equivalence claim would need a formal equivalence test (e.g., TOST) against a pre-specified margin, not a point-estimate ratio.
findings_verified.evidence: ratio = abs(a1_mean) / abs(s3_mean)  ... l2_pass = (ratio <= 0.5) and ci_excludes_zero — while every s3 interaction CI in tab_S3_interaction.csv contains 0
findings_verified.suggestion: Gate the ratio on the baseline being significantly nonzero (or report an equivalence-test / CI on the ratio via bootstrap of the ratio itself). Reword the S5 README conclusion: A1 results are consistent with 'no detectable interaction in either condition', which is weaker than 'direct refutation'.
findings_verified.verify_note: All elements of the finding verify. (1) Code: aggregate_ablation_summary.py lines 214-221 compute ratio = abs(a1_mean)/abs(s3_mean) with only a 1e-10 machine-zero guard, never checking that the S3 baseline interaction is statistically nonzero. (2) Data: tab_S3_interaction.csv shows all five S3 interaction 95% CIs straddle zero exactly as quoted; tab_S5_shrinkage.csv shows the symptomatic ratio instability (0.092, 1.006, 1.090, 3.806, 0.759) from means that are all noise-level. (3) README (YH006_1/README.md lines 172 and 178) presents "Spearman ratio 1.006 / Kendall ratio 1.090 (≈1.0)" as the "直接反証" (direct refutation) of hypothesis A simple, and line 297 carries this forward as a Phase 2 main finding justifying S6. With neither S3 nor A1 interaction distinguishable from zero at n=100, a ratio of two noise-level means is uninformative; the defensible conclusion is only that no interaction is detectable in either condition. Refutation attempts fail: the L2 gate does require ci_excludes_zero so no false PASS occurs (all metrics fail, correctly), and README line 177 shows partial awareness of ratio instability for Pearson — but the "direct refutation" claim from ratio ≈ 1.0 is still asserted and drives downstream research decisions. Severity major is correct: real methodological defect in the user's active research line likely to bite (feeds S6 plan and proposal narrative), but no data corruption and no incorrect pass/fail flag.

findings_verified.title: SPEC-mandated multiple-comparison correction (Bonferroni α=0.0125), Mann-Whitney tests, and independent-resample interaction bootstrap are never implemented
findings_verified.detail: SPECv2.0 §5.4 mandates Bonferroni α=0.0125 over 4 test families; §5.3 mandates Mann-Whitney U for two-condition comparisons and an independent-resample bootstrap over the four condition arrays for interaction significance; §5.1 mandates independent (non-paired) seeds. None of this is implemented: all CIs in every aggregation stage are plain 95% percentile bootstraps (bootstrap_ci(..., ci=0.95) at aggregate_full_summary.py:154/317/344/490, aggregate_ensemble.py:343/434, aggregate_ablation_summary.py:163/213, subsample_aggregate.py:318); no bonferroni/0.0125/holm/fdr string exists in any code or plan. stats.py's mannwhitney_u and bootstrap_interaction_ci (the faithful SPEC §5.3 implementations) are dead code — every script imports only bootstrap_ci. The interaction CIs actually computed (aggregate_full_summary.py:313-317, aggregate_ablation_summary.py:162-163 and 208-213) bootstrap positional paired differences (c3[:n]-c2[:n])-(c0p[:n]-c0u[:n]), a different estimator with different coverage than the SPEC's independent-resample design. The L2 pass criterion (aggregate_ablation_summary.py:220-221) applies uncorrected per-metric 95% CI-excludes-zero across 5 INTERACTION_METRICS. Finally, contradicting SPEC §5.1, all conditions reuse the identical seed range (config.py:117 ENSEMBLE_SEED_BASE=1000, seeds 1000..1099 in every ensemble runner), and run_experiment.py seeds np.random.default_rng(seed)/random.Random(seed) directly, so the positional pairing in the analysis makes this a de-facto paired-seed design. No document records these as deliberate deviations. Impact: significance claims (L2 gate, ablation shrinkage) in the active YH006 research line are less conservative than the pre-registered analysis plan; borderline results may not survive the mandated Bonferroni threshold. Not critical: no numerical corruption — the paired bootstrap is itself statistically valid — but conclusions drawn from these tables are docs-drifted from the spec.
findings_verified.evidence: SPEC: 'Bonferroni 補正で α = 0.05 / 4 = 0.0125 を主検定の閾値とする' — vs code: bootstrap_ci(vals, n_resample=10_000, ci=0.95, ...) everywhere; stats.py bootstrap_interaction_ci/mannwhitney_u unreferenced
findings_verified.suggestion: Either implement the spec (Bonferroni-adjusted 98.75% CIs for the 4 primary tests; use bootstrap_interaction_ci or delete it) or amend SPECv2.0 §5.1–§5.4 to describe what the code actually does, noting the L2 verdict was made at unadjusted α=0.05 over 5 metrics.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every element of the finding verifies. (1) SPECv2.0.md line 147 mandates Bonferroni α=0.05/4=0.0125 for the 4 main test families; grep across all YH006_1 code and plans finds no bonferroni/0.0125/holm/fdr — every CI is bootstrap_ci(..., ci=0.95) (aggregate_full_summary.py:154,317,344,490; aggregate_ensemble.py:343,434; aggregate_ablation_summary.py:163,213; subsample_aggregate.py:318). (2) SPEC §5.3 mandates Mann-Whitney U and an independent-resample interaction bootstrap; stats.py:33-68 implement exactly those (bootstrap_interaction_ci resamples the 4 condition arrays independently; mannwhitney_u) but neither is ever imported — all five importers pull only bootstrap_ci, so they are dead code. (3) The interaction CIs actually used (aggregate_full_summary.py compute_interactions lines 313-317; aggregate_ablation_summary.py compute_a1_interactions lines 162-163 and compute_shrinkage lines 208-213) form positional paired differences (c3[:n]-c2[:n])-(c0p[:n]-c0u[:n]) and bootstrap that array — a paired estimator with different coverage than the SPEC design. (4) The L2 gate (aggregate_ablation_summary.py:220-221) tests uncorrected 95% CI-excludes-zero per metric across the 5 INTERACTION_METRICS. (5) SPEC §5.1 line 132 states paired seeds are NOT used, yet config.py:117 sets ENSEMBLE_SEED_BASE=1000 shared by every condition's runner, run_experiment.py seeds default_rng(seed)/Random(seed) directly, and the analysis pairs trials positionally by that shared seed index — a de-facto paired-seed design contradicting the spec. No README/plan documents any deliberate deviation, so nothing mitigates the drift. Severity 'major' is calibrated: no data corruption or numerically wrong computation (the paired bootstrap is itself a valid, often more powerful estimator), but significance conclusions in the user's active research line are drawn under a less conservative regime than the pre-registered SPEC, so borderline L2/ablation pass claims may not survive the mandated correction.

findings_verified.title: Behavioral faithfulness generations bypass the chat template and BOS, producing off-regime output that the report uses as evidence
findings_verified.detail: Every logit measurement (clean probe and behavioral-framing probe) renders prompts through tokenizer.apply_chat_template(...) + assistant prefix (render.py:142-144, 201-203, 215-217). But the free-generation faithfulness path feeds render_behavioral(state) — the raw user text with no chat template — into ProbeModel.generate, which additionally tokenizes with prepend_bos=False, so the Instruct model receives neither its chat format nor a BOS token. Callers: run_all.py:255-256 (fb_recs = rand[:nb], whose comment 'balanced slice' is also not enforced) and finalize_faithfulness.py:110. The archived generations confirm the wrong regime: all 6 freegen_checkpoint.jsonl records open with document-continuation junk (' 2023-02-15 14:00:00', '  Please answer within 5 minutes.', '  | ') and parse_rate is 0.0. REPORT.md §4 then concludes the model 'emits ##Step-N CoT before the JSON, never reaching is_buy in 320 tokens' and cites this as confirming why the clean probe avoids CoT contamination (fixed research decision #3). That conclusion is confounded: a chat-templated run with the same 'Do not deviate from the format' instruction may well emit the JSON directly. The qualitative faithfulness leg (FCLAgent-style behavior check) was never actually measured in the model's operational regime.
findings_verified.evidence: model.py:176-178: toks = self.model.to_tokens(prompt, prepend_bos=False); out = self.model.generate(toks, ...) — called as m.generate(render_behavioral(r["state"]), ...) (finalize_faithfulness.py:110); freegen_checkpoint.jsonl: {"key": "gain_0", "parsed_is_buy": null, "gen": " 2023-02-15 14:00:00\n## Step 1: Analyze the current market condition..."}
findings_verified.suggestion: Route generation through the same chat-templated prompt as build_behavioral_probe (apply_chat_template with add_generation_prompt=True, prepend_bos matching the template), re-run the qualitative check, and re-evaluate the 'CoT contamination' narrative in REPORT.md §4 against in-regime generations.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every claim in the finding is verifiable in the code and archived artifacts. (1) The free-generation path bypasses the chat template: run_all.py:255-256 and finalize_faithfulness.py:110 both call m.generate(render_behavioral(r["state"]), ...), and render.py:125-126 shows render_behavioral returns the raw concatenated user text (PREMISE+INSTRUCTION+information+BEHAVIORAL_ANSWER) with no tokenizer.apply_chat_template call — unlike every logit-probe path (build_clean_probe render.py:211-217, build_clean_probe_variant 196-203, build_behavioral_probe 129-144), all of which apply the chat template plus assistant prefix. (2) model.py:176 tokenizes with self.model.to_tokens(prompt, prepend_bos=False); that flag is justified elsewhere in the file explicitly because "the chat template already includes BOS" (model.py:80-82, render.py:212-213), so for the un-templated behavioral prompt the Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model (config.yaml:21) receives neither its chat format nor a BOS token — a base/document-continuation regime. (3) The archived run confirms the off-regime output: all 6 records in outputs/20260529-092454_7de0bb4/faithfulness/freegen_checkpoint.jsonl open with continuation junk (" 2023-02-15 14:00:00", "  Please answer within 5 minutes."), parsed_is_buy is null on all, and SUMMARY.json reports parse_rate 0.0 with the note "the model emits CoT-style analysis before the JSON => confirms why clean-probe (no generation) avoids contamination". (4) REPORT.md §4 (line 89, Japanese) states that Llama emits "## Step N: Analyze…" CoT before the JSON and never reaches is_buy within 320 tokens, and cites this as backing fixed research decision #3 — a conclusion drawn from generations that were never run in the model's operational (chat-templated) regime, so it is genuinely confounded: a properly templated Instruct prompt with the same "Do not deviate from the format" instruction could plausibly emit the JSON directly. (5) The side claim is also accurate: run_all.py:251-252's "balanced slice" comment is not enforced — fb_recs = rand[:nb] is a plain prefix slice with no gain/loss balancing (contrast finalize_faithfulness.py:100-101, which does balance). Mitigation check: the defect does NOT flip the Stage 0.5 gate decision — the gate (finalize_faithfulness.py:128-153) is driven entirely by the chat-templated clean-probe and behavioral-framing logit measurements, and the free-gen leg is explicitly labeled "qualitative only". But nothing guards the confounded conclusion itself; it is recorded in gate.json, SUMMARY.json, and REPORT.md §4 as evidence for a fixed research decision, and the FCLAgent-style behavioral faithfulness of the model was in fact never measured in-regime. Severity 'major' is calibrated: a real methodological defect in the user's active research line (YH008) that invalidates a documented supporting conclusion, but it does not corrupt the primary quantitative results or the gate outcome, so it falls short of 'critical'.

findings_verified.title: findings.md presents YH006 results that the YH006 dossier explicitly retired or refuted (interaction −0.27; censoring 81.1% vs 0.9%; 'hypothesis A confirmed')
findings_verified.detail: findings.md (imported/speculation-game-info/docs/findings.md), whose stated purpose (line 3) is to be grepped before any new YH to avoid reusing superseded results, still presents three claims that YH006_research_dossier.md (2026-06-07 snapshot) explicitly retired or refuted: (i) line 146 keeps "corr(|ΔG|, horizon) の交互作用 −0.27 (主 finding)" although the dossier (line 26, §5 S3) confirms the F1 interaction is null in the 100-trial ensemble and the −0.27 was single-seed noise; (ii) line 197 keeps "仮説 A primary evidence 確定: LOB censoring_rate = 81.1% vs aggregate 0.9%" although the dossier §6.1 (line 194) lists this comparison as "Retire 済み" (horizon confound, 33x T difference), superseded by matched-τ KM survival (line 97); (iii) the "仮説 A ... 確定" wording contradicts the S5 ablation result "仮説 A 単純版 反証" (dossier lines 94, 159), with only a revised version pending in S6. findings.md ends at the YH006_1 S1/S2/S3 section (2026-05) with no correction; the transcription the dossier scheduled for "YH006_1 完了時" (§7.2, line 227) was never performed. Fix: append/amend the YH006 and YH006_1 sections of findings.md to mark the −0.27 interaction as ensemble-null, the 81.1%-vs-0.9% censoring comparison as retired in favor of matched-τ survival, and hypothesis A as refuted-in-simple-form / revised-pending, and transcribe the dossier's P4–P6 scheme-level lessons.
findings_verified.evidence: findings.md:146: 'corr(|ΔG|, horizon) の交互作用 −0.27 (主 finding)'; findings.md:197: '仮説 A primary evidence 確定: LOB censoring_rate = 81.1% vs aggregate 0.9%'; dossier: 'F1 interaction は ensemble で null と確定' and 'Retire 済み: 「LOB censoring 81.1% vs agg 0.9%」(horizon 交絡)'
findings_verified.suggestion: Update findings.md YH006/YH006_1 sections to the dossier's state (interaction null, survival-gap headline in matched-τ form, hypothesis-A-revised pending S6), or add a prominent 'superseded by YH006_research_dossier.md' banner.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three cited contradictions verified against the actual files. (1) /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/docs/findings.md:3 states the document's purpose exactly as claimed: it must be grepped/read before starting any new YH so prior results are not misused. (2) findings.md:146 (YH006 section, "確認済み") still lists "corr(|ΔG|, horizon) の交互作用 −0.27 (主 finding)", while YH006_research_dossier.md:26 (snapshot 2026-06-07) states "F1 interaction は ensemble で null と確定 ... 単一 seed の −0.27 は LOB の seed 間分散による noise". (3) findings.md:197 (YH006_1 section, "主 finding") states "仮説 A primary evidence 確定: LOB censoring_rate = 81.1% vs aggregate 0.9%", while dossier line 194 (§6.1) states "Retire 済み: 「LOB censoring 81.1% vs agg 0.9%」(horizon 交絡、33x の T 差)" and line 97 records its replacement by matched-τ KM survival (52x/58x at τ=1499). (4) Dossier lines 94 and 159 record "仮説 A 単純版 反証" (S5 ablation), so "仮説 A ... 確定" in findings.md is refuted, not merely stale. I searched findings.md end-to-end: it ends at line 210 with the YH006_1 section (dated 2026-05, S1/S2/S3 only) and contains no null/retire/反証 corrections, no S5+ results, and no YH007/YH008 sections — the transcription promised in dossier §7.2 (line 227: "findings.md へ YH006_1 完了時に転記") never occurred. The finding cannot be refuted: the drift is real, in the user's own highest-priority active research line, and the document's design role makes stale content actively harmful (a researcher starting YH007 who greps findings.md would recover a refuted hypothesis-A confirmation and a noise-level −0.27 as a "主 finding"). Severity: major is correctly calibrated — it is documentation, not canonical code producing wrong results (so not critical), but it is far more than minor because findings.md is the designated anti-rework ground truth for the active YH series and currently propagates three retired/refuted claims.

findings_verified.title: CI transaction_tax intervention is a verified bit-identical no-op (double unit error erased by tick rounding) — extracted verbatim into canonical package
findings_verified.detail: CI's transaction_tax intervention is a bit-identical no-op due to a unit error: the demand multiplier is 1 - rate/mid instead of the intended 1 - rate (compare FW's correct 1 - rate at franke_westerhoff/model.py:183-185), and the residual ~3e-5 demand change is annihilated by tick-size rounding of the fill price. Empirically reproduced on the canonical copy (packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py:198-214): a 0.3% tax produces returns np.array_equal to the untaxed baseline; even a 100% tax moves returns by only ~4e-5. Unit tests only check parameter propagation, not effect. Severity downgraded from critical to major because no active caller exists today — fingerprint_atlas does not sweep transaction_cost for CI, and PRISM (the only pipeline invoking apply_intervention) is retired — so no current results are wrong; the defect is latent in the canonical package and would silently report 'tax has zero effect' on first future use.
findings_verified.evidence:
cost = abs(net_order) * p.transaction_cost
if abs(net_order) > 0:
    net_order *= max(0.0, 1.0 - cost / (abs(net_order) * mid + 1e-12))
# => multiplier = 1 - rate/mid ≈ 0.99997; verified: post-tax returns np.array_equal to baseline
findings_verified.suggestion: Use the FW/LM convention (excess_demand *= 1 - rate), or model the tax as a threshold on expected round-trip profit; add a behavioral regression test asserting tax != no-op. Fix in both imported/PRISM and packages/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all three legs of the finding. (1) Code reads as claimed: ci.py:198-200 computes cost = |net_order|*rate then multiplies net_order by 1 - cost/(|net_order|*mid + 1e-12) = 1 - rate/mid, i.e., the tax rate is spuriously divided by the price level (~100); the FW adapter (fw.py:183-185 and canonical franke_westerhoff/model.py:183-185) divides by |excess_demand| only, giving the intended 1 - rate, confirming the CI version is a unit error not a design choice. (2) Reproduced the no-op empirically on the CANONICAL package copy (packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py, byte-identical at lines 198-214): with seed=42, n_paths=5, transaction_cost=0.003 vs 0.0 yields np.array_equal returns (max diff 0.0) at both default and calibrated (price_impact=0.5) params — the ~3e-5 residual demand change is erased by tick rounding at line 214; even rate=1.0 (100% tax) changes returns by at most ~4e-5. (3) No mitigation exists: imported/PRISM/tests/unit/test_ci_adapter.py:76-88 only asserts the parameter propagates, never that output changes, and the workspace tests/ suite never exercises transaction_tax. The docstring (model.py lines 9-10) still advertises a functioning tax intervention. Severity is miscalibrated, though: nothing active currently calls apply_intervention('transaction_tax') on CI — fingerprint_atlas MODEL_BOUNDS for chiarella_iori (adapters.py:88-100) does not sweep transaction_cost, experiments/ and tests/ have no callers, and PRISM itself is a retired lineage. No existing research result is corrupted; the defect is latent in canonical code and would silently yield the wrong scientific conclusion ('tax has zero effect') on first future use. That matches 'major' (real defect likely to bite), not 'critical'.

findings_verified.title: simulate(n_paths>1) still averages returns pointwise across paths — PRISM's own FATAL-2 defect extracted unfixed into canonical packages (CI, FW, ZI)
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as written, with one numeric correction: reproduced kurtosis for n_paths=20 is +0.08 (not 0.02) in this environment; std ratio 4.43 ≈ sqrt(20) matches exactly (2.02e-4 vs 4.55e-5). Defect present identically in packages/abm_models chiarella_iori/model.py:135, franke_westerhoff/model.py:131, zero_intelligence/model.py:112; PRISM's FATAL-2 fix (per_path_facts) lives only in imported/PRISM pipeline.py/cli and was not carried into packages. All current in-repo callers pass n_paths=1, so the defect is dormant but unguarded on the public ModelAdapter.simulate protocol.
findings_verified.evidence:
for i in range(n_paths):
    rng_i = np.random.default_rng(seed + i)
    returns = self._simulate_one_path(rng_i)
    all_returns.append(returns)
avg_returns = np.mean(all_returns, axis=0)
findings_verified.suggestion: In the packaged models, either return the stacked (n_paths, T) array, raise for n_paths>1, or deprecate the parameter. Same for franke_westerhoff/model.py:123-144 and zero_intelligence/model.py:104-123.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every element of the finding verifies against the repository. (1) The code: packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py:127-146 loops n_paths times with rng seeds seed+i and then computes `avg_returns = np.mean(all_returns, axis=0)` (line 135), returning that pointwise average as SimulatedMarketData.returns with no metadata flag indicating averaging. The identical pattern exists in packages/abm_models/abm_models/franke_westerhoff/model.py:131 and packages/abm_models/abm_models/zero_intelligence/model.py:112, byte-matching the PRISM originals (imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/{ci,fw,zi}.py). (2) The provenance: imported/PRISM/docs/AUDIT_REPORT.md indeed documents this exact line as FATAL-2 ('avg_returnsがstylized factsを破壊', citing np.mean(all_returns, axis=0)), and its fix (per_path_facts) was applied only in imported/PRISM/src/prism/pipeline.py and cli/main.py — no per-path mitigation exists anywhere in packages/. (3) The numerics: I reproduced the effect (uv run, CIAdapter, seed=7): std 2.02e-4 for n_paths=1 vs 4.55e-5 for n_paths=20 — ratio 4.43 ≈ sqrt(20), exactly the CLT variance shrinkage claimed; excess kurtosis -1.99 vs 0.08 (finding said 0.02; same collapse, minor scipy-version difference). (4) Dormancy: grepping n_paths outside imported/ confirms every current caller — chiarella_iori/franke_westerhoff/zero_intelligence __init__.py run() wrappers and tests/test_prism_family_parity.py — passes n_paths=1, so no current result is wrong. Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: it is a real latent defect in the canonical packages whose public simulate(seed, n_paths) API (also declared in _prism_compat.py:169 Protocol) silently returns a degenerate averaged series for any n_paths>1, but it does not currently corrupt active research results (which would warrant critical). No refutation path exists: the code behaves exactly as claimed, the path is reachable via the public API, and no test or caller guards n_paths>1.

findings_verified.title: CNN CV accuracy selects best epoch on the test fold (test-set leakage) — inflates all headline CNN numbers
findings_verified.detail: sf_cnn_cv_accuracy (toy/classifiers.py:102-121) selects the best epoch by accuracy on the held-out fold and reports accuracy on that same fold, making every reported CNN CV number a max-over-epochs (upward-biased) estimate. Empirical measurement on pure-noise nulls at the default runner scale (M=40/class, 15 epochs, 6 reps) shows mean 0.546 for the shipped estimator vs 0.494 for a leakage-free last-epoch variant (+5.2pp bias); it violated the Null-1 50±3% band in 5/6 reps and reached the 0.55 pass boundary in 3/6.
