This biases the pre-registered §14 gates in equivalence_check.py (Null-1 and Pass<=0.55 — the bias alone nearly spans the pass band), and inflates the CNN columns of Finding 0001 and the dose-response tables in working_paper_batch_identification_v0.md; near-threshold entries (e.g., 0.55 rows) are unreliable, though the 0.94-vs-chance qualitative contrast likely survives (~+0.05 bias cannot produce 0.94, and the continuous-regime 0.46 row is structurally pinned at chance because A/B are bit-identical there, so it never exercised the bias). test_classifiers.py has chance-level null tests for LR and XGB but none for the CNN, so the bias is never caught. Severity remains major (not critical): the affected code is in imported/PROV-ABM-atlas, not the canonical packages/ cores or the highest-priority speculation-game line, and the headline qualitative conclusion probably stands — but any quantitative CNN accuracy from this instrument, and both pre-registered gates, need re-running with a proper train/val/test split or fixed-epoch evaluation.
findings_verified.evidence:
with torch.no_grad():
    val = float((model(xe).argmax(1) == ye).float().mean())
if val > best_val:
    best_val, best_state = val, {k: v.clone() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}
...
model.load_state_dict(best_state)
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    accs.append(float((model(xe).argmax(1) == ye).float().mean()))
findings_verified.suggestion: Train for a fixed epoch budget (or carve a validation split out of the TRAIN fold for early stopping) and evaluate the test fold exactly once. Add a CNN null test (identical distributions -> ~0.5) to test_classifiers.py; re-run band_demo/band_analyses/equivalence_check and update the numbers in docs/findings/0001 and the working papers.
findings_verified.verify_note: Code inspection of imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/classifiers.py:102-121 confirms the exact mechanism: after every epoch the model is evaluated on the held-out fold (xe/ye), the state with highest test-fold accuracy is kept (best_val/best_state), reloaded, and accuracy reported on the SAME fold — i.e., the reported per-fold accuracy is max-over-epochs of test accuracy, a textbook test-set-leakage early-stopping bias. No mitigation exists: tests/unit/test_classifiers.py has chance-level null tests for LR and XGB but only a separable-signal test for the CNN (line 69), and no caller uses a separate validation split. Downstream reach verified: equivalence_check.py:102 feeds the pre-registered §14 Null-1 (50±3%, line 203) and Equivalence (Pass<=0.55, lines 207-213) gates; band_demo.py:41 / band_analyses.py:60 produce Finding 0001's CNN=0.94 headline and the dose-response tables in docs/working_paper_batch_identification_v0.md. I additionally quantified the bias empirically by running sf_cnn_cv_accuracy on pure-noise nulls at the runner's default scale (M=40/class, epochs=15, 6 replicates) against a leakage-free last-epoch variant: biased mean 0.546 vs clean 0.494 (+5.2pp); the biased meter exceeded the Null-1 ceiling (0.53) in 5/6 replicates and reached/exceeded the 0.55 pass boundary in 3/6 — so at default settings the bias alone nearly consumes the entire equivalence pass band, exactly as the finding claims. One nuance: Finding 0001's continuous-market CNN=0.46 is not counter-evidence, because in band_demo A and B share base seeds and are bit-identical in the continuous regime, so that row is structurally pinned at chance and never exercises the bias. The qualitative 0.94 headline likely survives (a +0.05 bias cannot manufacture 0.94), but all near-threshold numbers and both pre-registered gates are measured with a meter biased upward by roughly the width of the pass band.

findings_verified.title: A and B run sets share the identical seed list; paired/duplicate samples across classes break CV assumptions (below-chance 0.33/0.46 in Finding 0001)
findings_verified.detail: band_demo.py (lines 54-59, 63-68) and band_analyses.py::_discriminate_returns (lines 51-57) use the identical seed list for both model classes. In the continuous regime (batch=1) A(s) and B(s) are bit-identical by construction, so the CV dataset contains m duplicated feature vectors with opposite labels; StratifiedKFold splits twins across folds and the classifier deterministically mislabels the held-out twin, biasing accuracy below chance. Verified empirically: same-seed LR CV = 0.36 vs disjoint-seed 0.54 (m=40), reproducing the 0.33/0.46 in docs/findings/0001 and 0.38 in the working paper, which are misread as ≈0.5 chance. This bias would also make the pre-registered TOST equivalence band [0.45, 0.55] (program_claims_v1.md) fail for genuinely identical models. In the batch regime the same-seed pairing shares noise realizations across classes (measured cross-class return correlation ≈0.34 at batch=10), violating the i.i.d. assumption of the CV estimate in an uncharacterized direction. Fix: use disjoint seed ranges (e.g. base and base+m) for the two classes, or a paired-analysis design that acknowledges the pairing.
findings_verified.evidence:
lr1, cnn1 = _discriminate(
    _runset("A", 1, args.m, args.seed),
    _runset("B", 1, args.m, args.seed),
    cnn_epochs=args.cnn_epochs,
    seed=1,
)
findings_verified.suggestion: Use disjoint seed ranges for the two classes (e.g., base and base+10_000), or keep pairing deliberately but switch to GroupKFold(groups=seed) so a pair never straddles train/test — the classifiers.py docstring already prescribes grouped CV for the multi-window case. Re-report the continuous-regime numbers, which should then land at ~0.5 rather than below it.
findings_verified.verify_note: See verdict reasoning.

findings_verified.title: basin_dwell_counts crashes with broadcast ValueError whenever the highest-index basin has zero dwell
findings_verified.detail: counts[: pl[in_range].max() + 1] has length max_label+1, but the RHS np.bincount(..., minlength=n_basins)[:n_basins] always has length n_basins. Whenever the largest-index basin is never visited by the trajectory (max_label < n_basins-1), the assignment raises 'could not broadcast input array'. Phantom KDE minima with dwell=0 are explicitly expected by this very module (effective_basin_mask docstring), so this fires on real data and takes down run_atlas / the `atlas` CLI command. Reproduced live: basin_dwell_counts(np.array([0,0,1,1,1]), 4) -> ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (4,) into shape (2,). The tests (test_density.py:190-197) only cover the all-basins-visited and empty cases.
findings_verified.suggestion: Replace with counts = np.bincount(pl[in_range], minlength=n_basins)[:n_basins] (assign the whole array); add a test where the last-indexed basin is unvisited.
findings_verified.verify_note: Reproduced live in the repo venv: basin_dwell_counts(np.array([0,0,1,1,1]), 4) raises ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (4,) into shape (2,). At free_energy.py:383-385 the LHS slice counts[: int(pl[in_range].max()) + 1] has length max_label+1 while the RHS np.bincount(pl[in_range], minlength=n_basins)[:n_basins] always has length exactly n_basins; the assignment only succeeds when the highest-index basin is visited (max_label == n_basins-1). Reachability confirmed: state_atlas/pipeline.py:110 calls basin_dwell_counts(point_labels, stats.n_basins) inside run_atlas; basin indices come from assign_basins/minima in grid-scan order (spatial, not occupancy order), the KDE grid is padded (pad=0.5), and the module's own effective_basin_mask docstring (lines 397-399) explicitly anticipates phantom KDE minima with dwell=0 — so an unvisited highest-index basin is an expected real-data condition, and it crashes the whole atlas pipeline. Tests at tests/test_density.py:190-197 cover only the all-basins-visited and empty cases, so no guard exists. Severity 'major' is correct: hard, loud crash in an imported related project (not canonical packages/ or the active YH research line), no silent data corruption, but likely to fire on real runs.

findings_verified.title: Walk-forward resets persistent-stress run counters at every window start, and dedup keep='last' keeps exactly those freshly-reset dates
findings_verified.detail: Walk-forward backtest resets persistence counters at every window start: classify_series (state_machine.py:93) initializes backw_run/F_p99_run to 0 with no carry-in, and walk_forward_strategy calls it per test window (backtest.py:153). Because step_days=63 < test_days=252 and dedup uses keep='last' (backtest.py:183), every kept date lies within the first 63 days of a freshly-reset window; the first 9 (backw) / 4 (F_p99) days after each quarterly roll can never trigger persistent_stress, and any stress streak straddling a roll boundary is undercounted for the entire kept segment, firing the kill-switch up to backw_persist_days late. Correction to the original impact claim: on those days the per-bar label is still STRESS (slope test needs no counter), so the strategies are already de-risked (vol_carry: SVXY=0, small VXX hedge; risk_overlay: scale near 0) — the backtest is not left carrying vol through sustained stress. The real defect is backtest/deployment inconsistency: reported walk-forward results reflect the STRESS position instead of the documented kill-switch flat position around every roll during sustained episodes (GFC/COVID-type), so the published metrics do not measure the strategy as specified or as it would run live with continuous counters. Fix is leak-free since backw_run depends only on raw slope sign: seed counters from the pre-window slope history (and per-window-atlas F threshold applied to pre-window F if desired).
findings_verified.evidence:
states = classify_series(
            pd.Series(F_test, index=test_idx, name="F"), ... )
...
weights = weights[~weights.index.duplicated(keep="last")].sort_index()
findings_verified.suggestion: Seed the run-length counters from the tail of the train window (run classify_series over train_tail+test and discard the train part), or carry counters across windows.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified in imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/online/backtest.py and state_machine.py. classify_series (state_machine.py:93-102) hard-resets backw_run/F_p99_run to 0 at every call and has no carry-in parameter; walk_forward_strategy calls it once per test window (backtest.py:153-158). With test_days=252/step_days=63, every date's kept row (dedup keep='last', backtest.py:183) comes from the latest window covering it, which started at most 62 trading days earlier — so all kept dates sit in the first 63 days after a counter reset, and the first 9 days after each quarterly roll can never satisfy backw_run >= backw_persist_days (10); streaks straddling the roll are undercounted for the whole kept segment. persistent_stress genuinely drives weights (strategy.py:56-57, 68-69), and the only relevant test (test_online.py:124, prefix-truncation causality) does not cover cross-window counter continuity, so nothing mitigates it. The mechanism and reachability are exactly as claimed. However, the impact statement is overstated: on the suppressed days the per-bar label is still STRESS (slope < 0 needs no counter, state_machine.py:47-52), so vol_carry already holds SVXY=0 with a small VXX hedge and risk_overlay ramps toward 0 — the backtest is not left long SVXY through GFC/COVID-type episodes; the distortion is kill-switch-flat-cash vs STRESS-position (VXX hedge + cash), i.e., a fidelity gap between backtest and a live monitor with continuous counters, not raw stress exposure. Severity major is still defensible since it systematically distorts the walk-forward results of the strategy's headline kill-switch behavior in exactly the episodes it documents handling, in the researcher's own active related project.

findings_verified.title: ANOVA and permutation null both ignore autocorrelation from overlapping 21-day forward-vol windows and persistent basin labels
findings_verified.detail: forward_vols are 21-day realized vols computed at daily steps, so adjacent test observations share up to 20/21 of their return window and are massively autocorrelated; basin labels are also persistent in time. f_oneway assumes independent samples, and the permutation null (rng.permutation of labels) destroys the temporal clustering of labels, so under the true null (persistent labels x overlapping windows) the observed F is inflated relative to the permuted F distribution. Both the p<0.05 gate and the f_stat>null_f_95 gate are anti-conservative; 'edge_detected' can flip to True from pure regime-persistence artifacts. test_random_features_default_to_no_edge uses iid synthetic prices and does not exercise this failure mode.
findings_verified.evidence:
f_stat, p_value = scistats.f_oneway(*groups.values())
...
shuffled = rng.permutation(label_arr)
findings_verified.suggestion: Use a block/circular-shift permutation of the label series (preserving label run-length structure), or subsample non-overlapping forward windows (stride=forward_days), or use HAC-robust group comparison.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified in imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/backtest/walkforward.py. (1) Lines 103-113 compute 21-day forward realized vol at every daily test index, so adjacent test observations share 20/21 returns — vols_v is massively autocorrelated by construction. (2) Basin labels come from a VAE embedding of rolling-window features projected onto a train-fitted free-energy grid (lines 90-94), so labels form persistent runs in time. (3) Line 156 applies f_oneway, which assumes independent samples; lines 163-173 build the permutation null via rng.permutation(label_arr), a point-wise shuffle that destroys the temporal clustering of labels. Under the true null (persistent labels x overlapping vol windows), the observed F is over-dispersed relative to both the parametric F and the exchangeable permutation null, so BOTH gates at lines 176-182 (p<0.05 AND f_stat>null_f_95) are anti-conservative in the same direction — they are not independent safeguards, and edge_detected can flip True from regime-persistence artifacts alone. (4) No mitigation exists anywhere: grep for autocorrel/overlap/block/HAC across the repo finds nothing; SPEC.md and the docstring do not acknowledge the issue (the docstring wrongly claims the bar is 'intentionally high'); the only guard, test_random_features_default_to_no_edge (tests/test_backtest.py:64), is a single fixed-seed run on iid prices — one non-rejection cannot certify calibration, and iid prices lack the regime-persistent volatility that maximally inflates F. A block permutation of contiguous label runs or non-overlapping vol windows would be needed; neither is present. Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: the defect can produce a false 'edge detected' headline result, but the file lives in imported/market-dynamics, a related own project rather than the canonical packages/ cores or the active speculation-game-info line, so 'critical' (wrong results in canonical/active research code) does not apply.

findings_verified.title: Features built on unadjusted close (auto_adjust=False) — dividend and split jumps contaminate returns and realized vol; adj_close is fetched but never used
findings_verified.detail: YFinanceSource fetches with auto_adjust=False (state_atlas/data/yfinance_source.py:67) and carries adj_close per the data contract (data/base.py:20), but no consumer ever reads it: build_features computes all log returns (features/build.py:59-61), realized vol (build.py:64-66), and cross-sectional dispersion (build.py:76) from the unadjusted close, and the backtest's forward realized-vol target does the same (backtest/walkforward.py:96-99). Configured universes (config.yaml) include TLT and HYG (monthly distributions, ~0.3-0.5% ex-dividend drops) and SPY/QQQ/IWM/XLF/XLE (quarterly), so every ex-date injects a spurious negative return into ret_1d/ret_5d/ret_21d and inflates rv_21d with a systematic seasonal pattern; the downstream rolling z-score (build.py:89) dilutes but does not remove the bias. All tests use synthetic fixtures where adj_close equals close, so the suite cannot catch this. Note: no split-prone ETP (SVXY/VXX-style) is in any configured universe, so the catastrophic split scenario is hypothetical rather than realized; the concrete impact is the recurring dividend contamination.
findings_verified.suggestion: Compute returns/vol from adj_close (keep raw close for level-type features like VIX, which has no dividends).
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified end-to-end. (1) YFinanceSource downloads with auto_adjust=False (imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/data/yfinance_source.py:67) and maps 'Adj Close' to adj_close (line 22), which the contract requires (data/base.py:20). (2) build_features reads only df[(t,'close')] for all return horizons (features/build.py:59-61), realized vol (build.py:64-66), and cross-sectional dispersion (build.py:76) — a repo-wide grep shows adj_close is never consumed by any downstream code, only carried and stubbed in test fixtures. (3) The backtest's forward realized-vol target is likewise computed from unadjusted close (backtest/walkforward.py:96-99). (4) Configured universes (config.yaml:6,53-55) include TLT and HYG (monthly distributions) plus SPY/QQQ/IWM/XLF/XLE (quarterly), so ex-dividend price drops inject spurious negative returns and inflate rv_21d with a systematic seasonal pattern. (5) No mitigation exists: DECISIONS.md/SPEC.md contain no deliberate choice of unadjusted prices, and all tests use synthetic frames where adj_close==close, so the suite cannot detect it. The only overstatement in the original finding is the split scenario: no split-prone ETP (SVXY/VXX) is actually in any configured universe, so the 'catastrophic fake return' is hypothetical; the realized contamination is the recurring ~0.3-0.5% monthly TLT/HYG ex-dividend drops (roughly half a daily sigma, partially diluted by the subsequent rolling z-score at build.py:89). Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: a real defect that biases every real-data run of this project, but the project is imported/market-dynamics (a related own project, not the canonical/active SG research line), and the bias is moderate rather than result-destroying for the configured universes.

findings_verified.title: Convergence detector (D-B6 v2) is phase-sensitive: stable limit cycles whose period does not divide 10,000 can never be declared converged
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as written. train() (imported/ABM-Microstructure/src/microstructure/qlearn.py:227-229) compares raw 64-step greedy rollout signatures taken every 10,000 periods, and _greedy_cycle_signature (lines 183-189) starts the rollout from the live training history with no burn-in and no cycle canonicalization (unlike measurement, which uses _MEASURE_BURN_IN=100). Since state is the action history alone, a stable period-L greedy cycle shifts phase by 10,000 mod L between checks, so signatures match only if L divides 10,000 (10,000=2^4·5^4); perfectly stable cycles of period 3, 6, 7, 9, 11, ... are labeled non-converged forever, and persistent exploration (prob exp(-β·t), qlearn.py:54) near a check additionally injects transients that break equality even for divisor-L cycles. certify() (verdict.py:191,198) requires converged_all, so the design map (designmap.py:243) systematically cannot certify cyclic collusive equilibria — the outcome class emphasized in the Klein/Calvano-adjacent literature this harness targets. The docstring (qlearn.py:196-201) claims the criterion is limit-cycle stability, so this is a defect against stated intent. No test or caller guards it. Fix direction: canonicalize the signature (minimal rotation of the detected cycle) or roll out from a fixed initial history with burn-in.
findings_verified.evidence: if t and t % _CYCLE_CHECK_INTERVAL == 0:\n    sig = _greedy_cycle_signature(policies, cfg, history)\n    stable_checks = stable_checks + 1 if sig == last_sig else 0  # (qlearn.py:227-229; signature starts from training `history`, qlearn.py:183-189)
findings_verified.suggestion: Make the signature a pure function of the policy: roll out from a canonical start (initial_history(cfg)), or reduce the probe to its detected cycle and compare a canonical rotation of it. Either keeps the learning trajectory bit-identical while removing phase dependence.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is an accurate reading of the code. _greedy_cycle_signature (qlearn.py:183-189) rolls out 64 deterministic greedy steps starting from the CURRENT training history — no burn-in (contrast _MEASURE_BURN_IN=100 used by measurement) and no rotation canonicalization — and train() compares raw tuples at fixed 10,000-period strides (qlearn.py:227-229, _CYCLE_CHECK_INTERVAL=10_000). Because state is purely the recent action history, once agents are effectively greedy the history at successive checks is the previous one advanced 10,000 steps along the cycle; for a period-L cycle the two 64-step signatures are equal iff 10,000 ≡ 0 mod L. 10,000 = 2^4·5^4, so stable cycles of period 3, 6, 7, 9, 11, ... yield a different signature at every check, stable_checks resets each time, and converged can never become True. The exploration-blip sub-claim also holds: act() explores with prob exp(-β·t) forever, and a blip within memory periods of a check injects an un-burned-in transient into the signature (this part only delays convergence probabilistically). No mitigation exists downstream: verdict.py:191/198 requires converged_all for certification and designmap.py propagates it, so cyclic collusive equilibria with L∤10,000 are structurally uncertifiable; tests only cover determinism and that non-convergence blocks certification. The docstring (qlearn.py:196-201) explicitly states the criterion targets greedy limit-cycle stability, so the phase sensitivity is a defect relative to stated intent, not an accepted limitation. Whether such cycles actually arise in runs is runtime-dependent (I did not simulate), and the failure mode is a visible false negative (converged=False, non-certified) rather than silent wrong numbers, in an imported (though actively developed) repo rather than packages/ — hence major, not critical.

findings_verified.title: Impulse-response gate assumes a fixed-point baseline; on a converged limit cycle it detects spurious 'punishment' and spurious restoration failure
findings_verified.detail: impulse_response() (imported/ABM-Microstructure/src/microstructure/verdict.py) anchors both the punishment check (lines 129-134) and the restoration check (lines 146-148) to a single fixed profile baseline = prof_cf[cfg.ir_pre - 1] (line 118), i.e. it assumes the converged policy sits at a fixed point. But the v2 convergence criterion (qlearn.py train(), D-B6 v2) explicitly certifies stable greedy limit cycles, and its signature check (64-period probe every 10,000 periods) passes cycles of period 2/4/5 (10,000 mod L == 0). On a converged multi-phase cycle: (a) the trajectory itself dips below the single-phase baseline every cycle period, so punished=True fires within ir_punish_lag=10 with no actual retaliation; (b) any cycle of amplitude >1 tick makes restored=False unconditionally, so genuinely collusive cyclic equilibria can never pass the IR gate (false negative); (c) an amplitude-1 cycle keeps restored=True while punished is spuriously True, so combined with high markup and an unprofitable deviation this can yield a false CERTIFIED verdict in certify(). The matched-time counterfactual prof_cf is used only for the deviation_profitable accounting (line 153), not for the punished/restored comparisons where it would fix the phase artifact. No caller or test mitigates this: designmap.py:271 feeds trained results directly into impulse_response with no cycle-length guard, and tests/test_verdict_gate.py pins the classifier only with fixed-point synthetic policies (grim at (HI,HI), flat HI), so the limit-cycle failure mode is untested.
findings_verified.evidence:
baseline = prof_cf[cfg.ir_pre - 1]
...
if any(prof_dev[t][i] < baseline[i] for i in range(1, cfg.n_mm)):
    punish_lag = tau  # (verdict.py:118, 133-134); restored also compares tail to fixed `baseline` (verdict.py:146-148)
findings_verified.suggestion: Compare prof_dev[t] against prof_cf[t] at the same t (punishment = opponents tighter than counterfactual; restoration = dev path rejoins cf path), and add a placebo assertion that the cf path itself never triggers 'punishment'. Add a synthetic 2-cycle policy pair to test_verdict_gate.py.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves exactly as claimed. verdict.py:118 takes a single-phase baseline `prof_cf[cfg.ir_pre - 1]`; lines 129-134 flag punishment whenever any opponent action in the deviation trajectory falls below that fixed profile within 10 periods, and lines 146-148 require the 50-period tail to stay within +/-1 of the same fixed profile. Meanwhile the convergence criterion is explicitly cycle-tolerant: qlearn.py train() docstring says "収束 = greedy limit-cycle が連続安定 (D-B6 v2)", _MEASURE_BURN_IN is commented as the burn-in "until the frozen deterministic dynamics enter a limit cycle", and the signature check (64-period probe every 10,000 training periods) accepts any stable cycle whose period divides 10,000's phase alignment (period 2/4/5 all satisfy 10,000 mod L == 0). So converged=True limit cycles are an intended, reachable output. On such a cycle the counterfactual dynamics themselves dip below the single-phase baseline every cycle period, making punished=True fire with no retaliation, and any cycle of amplitude >1 tick makes restored=False unconditionally (blocking certification of genuine cyclic collusion); an amplitude-1 cycle instead permits a spurious IR pass and hence a false CERTIFIED via certify() line 189. deviation_profitable is the only check done at matched times against prof_cf (line 153) — the auditor's phrase "prof_cf is never used as the comparison at matched times" is slightly imprecise on that one point, but the punished/restored checks indeed never use it. No mitigation exists: designmap.py:271 feeds trained results straight into impulse_response with no cycle guard, and tests/test_verdict_gate.py exercises only fixed-point synthetic policies (grim at (HI,HI), flat HI), exactly as the finding states. Severity major is calibrated: this is the certification gate of the repo's currently active feature (experiment B collusion harness), but whether production-scale trained policies actually land on multi-phase limit cycles (vs fixed points) is a runtime condition, and ABM-Microstructure is a related own project rather than the canonical/highest-priority research line, so it falls short of "critical" while clearly being a real defect likely to bite.

findings_verified.title: atlas_v3 gate.json committed with ok:true despite population poisoned by a diverged GARCH run
findings_verified.detail: notebooks/atlas_v3/gate.json is committed with ok:true although its own numbers prove the fingerprint population contained a diverged non-stationary GARCH(1,1) run: feature_means_raw[0]=5.4746e8 is exactly 6.9e10/126 (the volatility figure commit 0e49cb7 attributes to an alpha+beta=1.09 LHS corner), and after z-scoring by std 6.12e9 every non-garch centroid collapses to -0.0894 on the volatility axis — one of Cont's six axes carried zero separation information for all 8 ABMs and both real references, and one garch specimen sat ~11 sigma out, distorting garch distances and the silhouette. The gate criterion (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/atlas.py:86: sil>0 and inter>intra) has no degeneracy or outlier check, so it certified the poisoned geometry (sil=0.0166). The v3 conclusions recorded in commit bce0b8f (real_spx_p1↔speculation_game=0.36, GARCH blind-spot analysis, real-market placement) were drawn from this effectively 5-axis instrument. The sibling atlas_v3_periods/gate.json shares the same poisoned population (ok:false, silhouette -0.0625) with no in-tree README; the only explanation is in the v4 commit message. v4 fixed the generator bounds and regenerated cleanly, but v3's false ok:true certificate and its poisoned-geometry conclusions remain the committed record with no in-tree correction, and could be re-cited (notably the SG-vs-S&P placement touching the active YH research line).
findings_verified.evidence: notebooks/atlas_v3/gate.json: "ok": true, ... "feature_means_raw": [547457067.4699, 11.8142, ...], "feature_stds_raw": [6120756013.529, ...]; centroids: chiarella_iori [-0.0894, ...], cont_bouchaud [-0.0894, ...], franke_westerhoff [-0.0894, ...] (volatility axis identical across all models)
findings_verified.suggestion: Either regenerate the v3 artifacts with the fixed GARCH bounds (alpha+beta<=0.99 now enforced in synthetic.py) or add a README/note in notebooks/atlas_v3 and atlas_v3_periods stating these artifacts are superseded and were computed on a corrupted volatility axis, so nobody cites the ok:true gate.
findings_verified.verify_note: Direct inspection of notebooks/atlas_v3/gate.json confirms every element of the finding. (1) "ok": true is committed (line 2). (2) The population provably contains the diverged GARCH draw: feature_means_raw[0]=547457067.47 equals 6.9e10/126 (the exact volatility figure commit 0e49cb7 attributes to the non-stationary alpha+beta=1.09 corner), and feature_stds_raw[0]=6.12e9. (3) The volatility axis is degenerate: all 12 non-garch centroids are exactly -0.0894 on axis 0 (garch11=1.7889), so one of six fingerprint features carried zero separation for all ABMs and real references. (4) The gate cannot detect this: packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/atlas.py:86 sets ok = isfinite(sil) and sil>0 and inter>intra — no per-feature degeneracy/outlier check — and sil=0.0166 squeaked past. (5) Commit bce0b8f draws v3's headline conclusions (real_spx_p1↔speculation_game=0.36, GARCH blind-spot 1-NN 0%, real-market placement) from this poisoned geometry. (6) The sibling atlas_v3_periods/gate.json shares identical poisoned raw means/stds and is committed ok:false with no README; the only acknowledgment of the corruption lives in commit 0e49cb7's message. Partial mitigation exists — v4 tightened GARCH bounds (alpha+beta<=0.99), regenerated cleanly (vol mean 6.62), and its commit message documents the failure — but the v3 artifacts were never regenerated, annotated, or marked superseded in-tree, so the false ok:true certificate and the poisoned-geometry conclusions (including the SG-closest-to-S&P claim on the user's highest-priority research line) remain the committed record. This mitigation keeps it below critical but does not refute it.

findings_verified.title: Committed atlas artifacts (v1..v4) are not reproducible from the repository: no DB, no run manifest, no generating command in-tree
findings_verified.detail: notebooks/atlas_v1..v4 and atlas_v3_periods contain only output artifacts (atlas.png, features.png, gate.json). The SQLite runs DB they were computed from is not committed (no *.db/*.sqlite outside imported/), gate.json carries no provenance fields (atlas.py validation_gate emits none; git_commit/seed provenance is written by populate.py only into the absent DB rows), and populate.py's usage docstring points at a nonexistent path (../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db). The atlas commit messages contain result summaries but NOT the exact CLI invocations or seeds, so reproducibility is even weaker than 'documented only in commit messages'. v2 is additionally unreproducible from current code because it was generated with widened chiarella_iori MODEL_BOUNDS that v3 reverted; v3/v4 depend on the Yahoo fetch date via real-market rows. tests/test_fingerprint_atlas.py gates only a synthetic toy population, not the committed artifacts. The notebooks/ naming observation also holds (no notebooks present; propose_cli.py defaults analytics output there).
findings_verified.evidence: notebooks/atlas_v2/ = {atlas.png, features.png, gate.json} only; find for *.db/*.sqlite outside imported/ returns nothing; atlas.py main() writes gate dict with no provenance keys; populate.py:11-13 docstring documents the run command only as usage text pointing at a nonexistent path
findings_verified.suggestion: Write a small manifest (git commit, populate seeds, populate_refs seeds, fetch timestamps, DB sha256) next to each gate.json — atlas.py main() already has all inputs to do so — and/or commit a make/justfile target that regenerates each atlas_vN end-to-end.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every factual element verified. (1) notebooks/atlas_v1..v4 and atlas_v3_periods contain only {atlas.png, features.png, gate.json}; no *.db/*.sqlite exists outside imported/. (2) gate.json (read in full) has no provenance keys; atlas.py validation_gate/main (lines 37, 229-232) never emit git_commit/seed/DB-hash — populate.py:57,87 writes that provenance only into the uncommitted DB runs rows. (3) populate.py:11-13 usage points at ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db, a path absent from the tree; the atlas commit messages (f014e57, bce0b8f, 0e49cb7) contain result summaries but not the exact invocations/seeds — reproducibility is even weaker than the finding claims. (4) No mitigation: tests/test_fingerprint_atlas.py gates a synthetic toy population, not the artifacts; and even though populate.py has deterministic defaults (seed-base 1000, rng-seed 0, n-per-model 12), the v3 commit records that v2 was generated with since-reverted widened chiarella_iori MODEL_BOUNDS, so v2's gate.json is unreproducible from current code regardless of seeds; v3/v4 embed Yahoo-fetch-date-dependent real rows. (5) notebooks/ indeed contains no notebooks (only a misspelled 00_sanity_check.jpynb) and propose_cli.py defaults analytics output there. Severity major is calibrated: not critical (no evidence the committed numbers are wrong; canonical parity claims live elsewhere), but the package's headline validation numbers are irreproducible in a repo whose specs treat provenance as first-class.

findings_verified.title: Worker errors from run_parallel_trials are discarded and missing parquets are skipped with `continue` — failed trials silently shrink the ensemble
findings_verified.detail: parallel._worker_run_trial catches every Exception and returns the traceback as a string (parallel.py:49-51). Three of five drivers discard the returned list entirely: aggregate_ensemble.py:545, ablation_ensemble.py:234, lob_ensemble.py:197 call `run_parallel_trials(...)` without binding the result, so the only trace of a failed trial is a log line. Aggregation then reads whatever parquet exists: aggregate_ensemble.aggregate_ensemble_summaries (lines 214-222) does `except FileNotFoundError: logger.error(...); continue`, and aggregate_full_summary.compute_pooled_bin_var_4cond (lines 97-100) does `if p.exists(): rt_dfs.append(...)`. A single failed trial therefore silently reduces n from 100 and (per the positional-pairing finding) misaligns every subsequent seed pair, with no hard failure anywhere in the pipeline. lob_extension_ensemble.py and ablation_a3_ensemble.py do inspect errs but also only logger.error and proceed.
findings_verified.evidence:
aggregate_ensemble.py:545:            run_parallel_trials(cond, seeds, cond_dir, n_workers, logger)
parallel.py:49-51:    except Exception as e:
        import traceback
        return (cond, seed, 0.0, 0, 0, traceback.format_exc())
aggregate_ensemble.py:220-222:        except FileNotFoundError as e:
            logger.error(...); continue
findings_verified.suggestion: Have every driver collect the error list and raise (or write a machine-readable failure manifest) when any trial errored; in aggregation, assert the observed seed set equals the expected seed range instead of skipping missing files.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every cited line behaves as claimed. parallel.py:49-51 converts any worker exception into a returned traceback string; run_parallel_trials logs it (parallel.py:95-96) and returns normally. aggregate_ensemble.py:545, lob_ensemble.py:197, and ablation_ensemble.py:234 discard the returned results list entirely, so a failed trial produces only a log line. Downstream, aggregate_ensemble_summaries (aggregate_ensemble.py:214-222) skips missing parquets with `except FileNotFoundError: logger.error(...); continue`, Step D (lines 566-569) and aggregate_full_summary.compute_pooled_bin_var_4cond (lines 97-104) use `if p.exists()` guards, and no code path anywhere asserts the realized trial count equals args.n_trials — the pipeline completes, writes ensemble_summary.parquet and the stage report from n<expected trials, and exits 0. lob_extension_ensemble.py:253-260 and ablation_a3_ensemble.py:239 do inspect errs but only logger.error and proceed, as the finding states. No test in the workspace suite guards these drivers. The only caveat: the failure is logged (ERROR with full traceback), so "silent" means no hard failure/exit-code/count-check rather than zero trace; and the "misaligns every subsequent seed pair" clause depends on the separate positional-pairing finding (summary rows here do carry explicit seed columns). Severity "major" is correct: this is the user's own active research line, but harm requires a runtime trial failure to occur, and surviving trials' data remain correct — so not critical, clearly more than minor.

findings_verified.title: YH006 'wealth conservation' test is tautological — the conservation property is never asserted
findings_verified.detail: The test is named and documented as a wealth-conservation invariant for the active research line, but the only assertion touching the computed round-trip sum is a type check. It computes sum_dG_q = sum(delta_G * entry_quantity) and then asserts it is numeric; it never compares it to sg_wealth or any wealth delta. The remaining assertions (sg_wealth >= 0, sg_wealth < 10**7) would pass on an implementation that credits wealth twice, ignores quantity, or drops round trips entirely. The docstring admits w_init is 'unknown' — but w_init is set in the agent's setup() and could be recorded by the test itself. A broken sg_wealth accounting in speculation_agent.py._reconcile (lines 342-371) would sail through.
findings_verified.evidence:
if a.num_substitutions == 0:
    # w_init は setup() 時点の初期値が unknown なのでチェックしにくい。
    # 代わりに sum_dG_q がどの程度なら妥当かを緩く確認。
    assert isinstance(sum_dG_q, (int, float)), "delta_G × q must be numeric"
findings_verified.suggestion: Record each agent's initial sg_wealth after setup (or expose it as an attribute), then assert exactly: a.sg_wealth == w_init + sum_dG_q for agents with num_substitutions == 0, and w_init + sum_dG_q + charity_credits for the rest (track charity per substitute). This turns the test from a tautology into the strongest available accounting invariant for YH006.
findings_verified.verify_note:
Read the cited file in full. The claim is accurate on every point. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/tests/test_wealth_conservation.py, the only assertions are: (1) line 50 `a.sg_wealth >= 0`, (2) line 51 `a.sg_wealth < 10**7`, and (3) line 59 `assert isinstance(sum_dG_q, (int, float))` — a type check on a Python sum of int products, which can never fail. `sum_dG_q` (line 54) is computed but never compared to `sg_wealth` or any wealth delta, so the conservation invariant named in the file's docstring and required by SPEC.md line 273 ("substitute 以外で全 agent cash 合計 + asset × mid が保存に近い") is never tested. The comment at line 53 even states the exact conservation check that should hold for agents with num_substitutions == 0, then skips it.

Not mitigated elsewhere: the companion test_roundtrip_invariants.py only checks per-round-trip delta_G against reconstructed cognitive prices (with a lax <50% mismatch tolerance, line 77) and never touches sg_wealth accounting; no other test in the repo asserts sum(delta_G*entry_quantity) == sg_wealth delta (grep for sum_dG_q/conservation found nothing else). In speculation_agent.py, sg_wealth is credited at line 348 (`self.sg_wealth += int(dG) * int(self.entry_quantity)`) inside _reconcile — a double-credit, quantity-drop, or missed-round-trip bug there would pass all three assertions. The "w_init is unknown" excuse is weak: setup() sets `self.sg_wealth = w` at line 164 and the initial value could trivially be recorded (e.g., stash `self.w_init = w` or snapshot in a test subclass), enabling the exact per-agent check `w_init + sum_dG_q == sg_wealth` for num_substitutions == 0 agents.

Impact is amplified by documentation: docs/findings.md line 155 counts "wealth conservation 1" among the 13 passing verification cases for this active research line (YH006, part of the highest-priority YH001..YH008 lineage), giving false assurance. Severity 'major' is calibrated correctly — it is a genuine testing defect that would let a wealth-accounting bug in active research code sail through, but it is not itself a wrong-results bug in the model code (no evidence _reconcile is currently broken), so not 'critical'.

findings_verified.title: YH006/YH005 experiment test suites never run in CI, and 3 of 4 YH006 test files cannot even be collected in the monorepo environment (pams missing)

The user's highest-priority active research line therefore has zero automated verification, and its tests cannot be executed at all from the canonical environment — nothing detects if a refactor of speculation_agent.py or yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py breaks them. (YH005's 18 tests do pass when invoked manually, but are equally absent from CI.)

Severity 'major' is calibrated correctly: it is a real reproducibility/verification gap likely to bite, but does not itself produce wrong results.

findings_verified.title: delta_G consistency check tolerates up to 50% mismatched round trips — broken accounting passes
findings_verified.detail: The only cross-check of YH006's delta_G accounting (tests/test_roundtrip_invariants.py line 77) tolerates any mismatch fraction below 50%. Empirically the invariant holds exactly — running the test's own scenario across seeds 777/1/2/42 gives a 0.00% mismatch rate (0/162, 0/126, 0/161, 0/146 round trips) — so the comment's justification (1-step drift for session-boundary-crossing round trips after warmup re-stitching) does not occur and the tolerance could be tightened to strict equality. As written, a systematic delta_G bug (index shift, one-sided sign error) corrupting up to ~49% of round trips would pass. Compounding evidence of an unresolved off-by-one attempt: line 61's `P = np.concatenate([[0], res["cognitive_prices"]])` is dead code, never used. The additional weakness that `if valid.any():` (line 65) silently skips verification is real in code but practically unreachable: the adapter (yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py:144-148) guarantees open_t >= 0 and close_t < T after warmup rebasing, and an empty round-trip set is already caught at lines 47-48. Fix: drop the dead P, assert frac_mismatch == 0 (or a tight per-event tolerance if a boundary case is ever demonstrated), and assert valid.all() rather than skipping.
findings_verified.evidence:
# 50% 以下なら設計通り、それ以上なら実装 bug とみなす。
frac_mismatch = mismatch / len(dG_expected)
assert frac_mismatch < 0.5, f"delta_G mismatch rate too high: {frac_mismatch:.2%}"
findings_verified.suggestion: Resolve the 1-step warmup-restitching ambiguity explicitly: classify each round trip as boundary-crossing or interior, require exact equality (mismatch == 0) for interior round trips, allow the ±1-step reconstruction only for boundary ones, and assert valid.any() (or fail loudly) so the check cannot be skipped silently. Delete the dead `P` array.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three factual claims in the finding check out against imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/tests/test_roundtrip_invariants.py: (1) line 77 asserts only `frac_mismatch < 0.5`, so a delta_G accounting bug affecting up to ~49% of round trips passes; (2) line 61 builds `P = np.concatenate([[0], res["cognitive_prices"]])` which is never referenced afterwards (lines 68-69 index res["cognitive_prices"] directly) — a genuine dead leftover from an off-by-one reconciliation attempt; (3) line 65's `if valid.any():` silently skips the entire consistency block if the mask is empty, though in practice this branch is nearly unreachable because the adapter (yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py lines 144-148) guarantees open_t >= 0 and close_t <= T-1 after warmup rebasing, so `valid` was all-True in every run I made. Crucially, I ran the exact test scenario empirically (installed pams 0.2.2 into scratchpad, used the repo venv): across seeds 777, 1, 2, and 42 the actual delta_G mismatch rate is exactly 0.00% (0/162, 0/126, 0/161, 0/146). The reconstruction invariant holds EXACTLY, meaning the 50% tolerance band is not justified by any real boundary-drift effect in this configuration — the comment's rationale about 1-step re-stitching drift does not manifest, and the assert could be `frac_mismatch == 0`. As written, the only cross-check of delta_G (the core round-trip profit accounting of the user's active YH006 research line) is effectively vacuous against realistic bug classes (systematic index shifts, one-sided sign errors). This is a real testing defect, not a misread. Severity 'major' is fair for a vacuous guard on canonical accounting in the highest-priority active research line, with the caveat that current production results are correct (mismatch is 0%), so it is a missing safety net rather than wrong results — sub-point (b) about the valid-mask skip is the weakest part, being practically unreachable given the adapter's filtering plus the earlier no-round-trips assertion at line 47-48.

findings_verified.title: Hill estimator with k = 10% of sample is bulk-contaminated; four inconsistent Hill k-conventions coexist in the repo with no k-sensitivity analysis anywhere
findings_verified.detail: YH006_1's hill_estimator uses n_tail_frac=0.10, i.e. k = 100,000 order statistics on a pooled ~1e6-RT trial or k≈1,000 on a 10k-RT trial. Hill is only consistent as k/n → 0 in the tail region; at 10% depth on an integer-valued |ΔG| distribution whose smallest nonzero value is 1, the estimate is dominated by the bulk and is a shape statistic, not a tail index — yet it is published as 'hill_alpha' with bootstrap CIs in tab_S2/tab_S3 and README comparison tables across conditions. Meanwhile packages/stylized_facts/core.py:86 uses k=sqrt(n) (~223 at T=50k), imported/speculation-game-info/analysis/tail_exponent.py:32 also k=sqrt(n), fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint.py:57 uses tail_frac=0.05 with a cap at 20, and YH005 wealth uses xmin=p90. No file contains a Hill plot, k-sweep, or Clauset-style xmin selection, and no Hill estimate anywhere carries a CI (asymptotic SE α/√k is trivial to add). Cross-condition Hill comparisons can reorder as k varies, so single-k point estimates are fragile evidence.
findings_verified.evidence:
k = max(int(len(sorted_vals) * n_tail_frac), 10)   # analysis.py:138, n_tail_frac=0.10
vs core.py:86  k = int(np.sqrt(sorted_desc.size))
vs fingerprint.py:57  tail_frac: float = 0.05, HILL_ALPHA_CAP = 20.0
findings_verified.suggestion: Standardize on one Hill routine in packages/stylized_facts with (a) a k-sweep/Hill-plot diagnostic, (b) asymptotic SE α/√k or bootstrap CI, and (c) documented k; for |ΔG| (discrete, min 1) consider a discrete power-law fit instead of continuous Hill.
findings_verified.verify_note:
Every checkable claim in the finding holds up against the code.

1. The cited k-convention is real: /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/analysis.py:132-147 defines hill_estimator(values, n_tail_frac=0.10) with `k = max(int(len(sorted_vals) * n_tail_frac), 10)` (line 138). It is called with n_tail_frac=0.10 at both call sites: aggregate_ensemble.py:198 (per-trial "hill_alpha") and reanalyze_phase1.py:416 (pooled Phase-1 "hill_alpha_dG"). reanalyze_phase1.py:82-83 confirms the pooled samples are C0u=1,041,712 and C0p=1,049,903 round trips, so k≈104,000 order statistics — 10% depth on a sample that size is far outside the tail regime where Hill is consistent.

2. ΔG is integer-valued as claimed: run_experiment.py:306 stores delta_G as np.int64, so the smallest nonzero |ΔG| is 1 and the 10%-depth order statistic sits in the discrete bulk. The resulting number is a bulk-shape statistic, not a tail index.

3. It is published as evidence: README.md lines 28, 60, and 110 present hill_alpha with bootstrap CIs across all four conditions as a cross-condition comparison table, and SPECv2.0.md (lines 124, 284) explicitly frames "ΔG distribution Hill exponent condition-differences" as a Plan-B research indicator. The bootstrap CIs are resampled over trials, so they capture trial-to-trial variance but none of the k-choice bias — the dominant error source at k = 10% of n.

4. The four inconsistent conventions coexist exactly as reported: packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py:86 uses k=sqrt(n); imported/speculation-game-info/analysis/tail_exponent.py:32 also sqrt(n); packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint.py:57,64 uses tail_frac=0.05 with HILL_ALPHA_CAP=20; stylized_facts/core.py:140-154 (and YH005/analysis.py:140, playground/assets/sf.py:58) use xmin=p90. A repo-wide grep for k-sweeps, Hill plots, or Clauset xmin selection finds none in code — the only Clauset reference (playground/yh001.html:47) says the Clauset MLE was run outside this repo and omitted here. No Hill estimate in the repo carries an analytic SE.

5. Not mitigated anywhere: no test constrains hill_alpha beyond regression pinning of previously computed values (reanalyze_phase1.py EXPECTED table checks reproducibility of the same biased estimator, which validates determinism, not correctness).

Refutation angles considered and rejected: (a) the Hill formula itself is correctly implemented, but the finding does not claim otherwise — it targets the k choice; (b) hill_alpha is an auxiliary "Plan B" indicator rather than the headline ρ(h,|ΔG|) metric, which argues against critical but not against major; (c) within-repo comparisons all use the same k=10% convention in YH006_1, so orderings are internally uniform — however Hill bias at fixed k/n is distribution-dependent, so conditions with differently-shaped bulks are biased by different amounts, which is exactly the fragility claimed.

Severity: major is correctly calibrated. YH006_1 is the user's own active research line and the numbers appear in published comparison tables, but the affected quantity is a secondary indicator and the code is deterministic and internally consistent — this is a methodological/numerics defect likely to bite (misinterpreting a bulk statistic as a tail index, non-robust cross-condition ordering), not silent corruption of the canonical headline results.

findings_verified.title: fingerprint_atlas — a full arXiv+LLM research pipeline with scheduled auto-committing CI — exists in no spec, ADR, or README
findings_verified.detail: packages/fingerprint_atlas — a ~20-module arXiv+LLM research pipeline (atlas, fingerprint, populate, propose, arxiv_ingest, methods_draft, Groq LLM calls) — appears in no spec, ADR, or README: spec §4's target tree and the README list packages/ as abm_models, stylized_facts, market, regimes, provenance only, and no markdown file in the repo mentions "fingerprint" at all. Yet it is a root dependency (pyproject.toml:19, workspace member :38), carries a large share of the test suite, and drives two scheduled GitHub Actions workflows (ingest_arxiv.yml weekly cron "0 21 * * 0"; propose.yml weekly cron "0 9 * * 1"), both with 'permissions: contents: write' and Groq API usage. Correction to the original claim: the workflows do not auto-commit to the repo's mainline — both use peter-evans/create-pull-request to push a run-scoped branch and open a PR (base: claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw), so changes require a human merge. The docs-drift itself is fully confirmed and is aggravated by the spec's self-declared ground-truth status and its explicit non-goal ('本 spec の期間中、金融ABM核に属する新規リポを作ってはならない'): the largest divergence between documented and actual repo behavior is this spec-invisible package and its scheduled LLM CI. Fix: add fingerprint_atlas to the spec/README structure (or a new spec/ADR describing its scope, CI schedule, and PR-based write model).
findings_verified.evidence: spec §4 target tree has no fingerprint_atlas; root pyproject.toml line 19 depends on 'fingerprint-atlas'; .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml: 'schedule: - cron: "0 21 * * 0"' + 'permissions: contents: write'.
findings_verified.suggestion: Write a spec/ADR for fingerprint_atlas (purpose, scope, why it lives in the consolidation monorepo, CI write-access rationale) and add it to README's package map, or move it out per the spec's own convergence non-goal.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every checkable element of the finding is true. packages/ actually contains fingerprint_atlas (~20 modules: atlas, fingerprint, populate, propose, arxiv_ingest, methods_draft, arxiv_cli, db, etc. plus colocated tests), yet a case-insensitive grep for "fingerprint" across all .md files in the repo returns zero hits — the spec's §4 target tree (specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md:112-132) lists only abm_models/stylized_facts/market/regimes/provenance, the README repeats that list, specs/ has only 001 and docs/adr only 0001. Root pyproject.toml line 19 declares 'fingerprint-atlas' as a dependency (workspace member at line 38). Both scheduled workflows exist as claimed: ingest_arxiv.yml (cron "0 21 * * 0") and propose.yml (cron "0 9 * * 1"), each with 'permissions: contents: write' and Groq LLM calls. The spec's non-goal quote appears verbatim at line 68. One correction to the detail: the workflows do NOT auto-commit to main — both use peter-evans/create-pull-request@v7, which pushes a new branch (ingest-arxiv/<run_id>, propose/<run_id>) and opens a PR against base 'claude/vibrant-ptolemy-fapuxw' (itself not main), so there is a human merge gate; 'autonomous write access to main' is overstated. That softens the CI-risk angle but does not touch the core docs-drift claim, which stands: the repo's largest spec-invisible subsystem is a root dependency driving scheduled LLM-backed CI, while the spec declares itself ground truth and explicitly forbids new research lines during consolidation. Severity major is calibrated: not critical (no wrong results/data corruption; existing 74 tests pass and canonical model code is untouched), but well above minor because anyone (including automation) treating the spec/README as ground truth — which the repo's own docs instruct — will materially misread what the repository does and what its CI spends API quota on.

findings_verified.title: C_ticks calibration script crashes at setup: MMFCNAgent never registered (documented pipeline step 1 is unrunnable)
findings_verified.detail: calibrate_c_ticks.py builds the C1 config (which via configs/_base.py:31 declares "class": "MMFCNAgent") but constructs SequentialRunner without any runner.class_register() call, so pams' class_finder cannot resolve the custom agent and the script dies during _setup. Verified: `uv run --with pams --with matplotlib --with scipy python calibrate_c_ticks.py` raises `AttributeError: class for MMFCNAgent is found 0 times`. This is the mandatory first step of the experiment pipeline (run_experiment.py's docstring: "先に calibrate_c_ticks.py 必須"; SPEC.md §3.2(e)), so the full documented C1→calibrate→C2→C3 reproduction path is broken at step 1. The drift happened when MMFCNAgent replaced plain FCNAgent in configs/_base.py: tests and run_experiment.py were updated to register it, this script was not. Verified fix: registering MMFCNAgent lets the identical C1 run complete (64s) and produce a calibration value.
findings_verified.evidence:
runner = SequentialRunner(
        settings=cfg,
        prng=random.Random(seed),
        logger=saver,
    )
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    runner.main()   # <- no runner.class_register(MMFCNAgent) anywhere; cfg["FCNAgents"]["class"] == "MMFCNAgent"
findings_verified.suggestion: Add `from mm_fcn_agent import MMFCNAgent` and `runner.class_register(MMFCNAgent)` before runner.main(); add a tiny-steps invocation of calibrate() to the test suite so pipeline entry points can't silently rot.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified line-by-line and reproduced the crash. (1) /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/calibrate_c_ticks.py builds the C1 config via configs/c1.py -> configs/_base.py, whose _FCN_AGENTS block (line 31) sets "class": "MMFCNAgent". (2) calibrate_c_ticks.py (lines 44-50) constructs SequentialRunner and calls runner.main() with no import of mm_fcn_agent and no runner.class_register() anywhere in the file. (3) Reproduced: running calibrate() in the repo's uv environment with pams raises `AttributeError: class for MMFCNAgent is found 0 times` during setup, exactly as claimed. (4) The drift explanation holds: grep shows every other YH006 runner (run_experiment.py:69, tests/test_parity.py:44, tests/test_wealth_conservation.py:37, tests/test_roundtrip_invariants.py:38, all experiments/* scripts) registers MMFCNAgent; only calibrate_c_ticks.py was missed. (5) No mitigation: outputs/C_ticks_calibration.json is not committed (git ls-files shows no calibration artifact; outputs/ does not exist), and run_experiment.py:110-112 merely falls back to a hardcoded c_ticks=0.03 with a warning — meaning the SPEC.md §3.2(e) calibrated value cannot be produced at all, so the documented C1->calibrate->C2/C3 reproduction path is genuinely broken at step 1 in the user's highest-priority active research line. Severity 'major' is correctly calibrated: the failure is a loud crash (not silent wrong results, so not critical), but it blocks a mandatory documented pipeline step and would push any reproduction run onto the uncalibrated fallback parameter.

findings_verified.title: With calibration file absent and calibration script broken, run_experiment.py silently runs with c_ticks=0.03 — ~300x smaller than the SPEC-calibrated value (~9.0; profiler used 28.006)
findings_verified.detail: run_experiment.py reads outputs/C_ticks_calibration.json and falls back to c_ticks=0.03 with only a one-line warning if it is missing. Today the file does not exist in the repo (outputs/ is absent and gitignored) AND the script that produces it crashes (see calibrate_c_ticks.py finding), so every fresh reproduction takes the fallback. I re-executed the SPEC §3.2(e) procedure with run_experiment's own defaults (C1, warmup=200, main=1500, num_fcn=30, seed=777): median|Δmid| = 3.0079, so C_ticks = 9.02; profile_c3_plan_a.py hardcodes c_ticks=28.006 from an apparently longer calibration run. c_ticks is the quantization scale for h(t)=quantize(Δmid, C_ticks) ∈ {-2..+2} (SPEC.md §3.2, speculation_agent.py:212), so 0.03 vs ~9–28 saturates essentially every nonzero Δmid into the extreme bins, giving the SG agents a qualitatively different information structure than the specified experiment. All YH006 tests use c_ticks=0.03 explicitly, so the suite cannot catch this. Results produced via the fallback are not comparable to the calibrated experiment yet differ only by a warning line in the log.
findings_verified.evidence:
if calib_path.exists():
        with open(calib_path) as f:
            c_ticks = float(json.load(f)["c_ticks"])
    else:
        c_ticks = 0.03
        print(f"[run_experiment] warning: no calibration file, using c_ticks={c_ticks}")
# measured today: SPEC procedure gives median|Δmid|=3.0079 → C_ticks=9.02; profile_c3_plan_a.py:33 uses c_ticks=28.006
findings_verified.suggestion: Make the missing calibration file a hard error (or at least require an explicit --c-ticks override) instead of a plausible-looking fallback that is 2–3 orders of magnitude off; commit the calibration JSON used for published figures for provenance.
findings_verified.verify_note:
Every factual element of the finding checks out against the repository and live execution.

1. Fallback path exists and is the only reachable path today. run_experiment.py:106-112 reads outputs/C_ticks_calibration.json and silently falls back to c_ticks=0.03 with a single print warning. The outputs/ directory does not exist in the working tree and is gitignored (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/.gitignore:6, confirmed via git check-ignore), so no fresh clone ever has the file.

2. The producer script really does crash. I executed calibrate_c_ticks.py's calibrate() in a venv with pams 0.2.2 + numpy: it raises AttributeError: "class for MMFCNAgent is found 0 times" (pams class_finder), because calibrate_c_ticks.py builds the C1 config (which declares MMFCNAgent) but never calls runner.class_register(MMFCNAgent) — unlike run_experiment.py:68-69 which does. So the documented workflow (README.md:45-47: run calibration first) is broken and the 0.03 fallback is what every fresh reproduction actually gets.

3. The magnitude claim reproduces exactly. Running the SPEC §3.2(e) procedure with run_experiment's defaults (C1, warmup=200, main=1500, num_fcn=30, seed=777, MMFCNAgent registered) I measured median|Δmid| nonzero = 3.0079, giving C_ticks = 9.0237 — identical to the auditor's numbers. README.md:76 documents the actually-used value as "c_ticks ≈ 28.0 tick" and profile_c3_plan_a.py:33 hardcodes 28.006. Either way the 0.03 fallback is ~300–930x too small.

4. Consequence is as claimed. history_broadcast.py:18-24 quantize() maps any dp > C to +2 (and symmetrically to -2), so with C=0.03 against typical |Δmid| ≈ 3, every nonzero price move saturates to ±2; the h ∈ {±1} bins are effectively never populated and the SG agents' history index mu degenerates relative to the specified experiment. Results are silently non-comparable to the calibrated run.

5. No mitigation elsewhere. All three YH006 test files pin c_ticks=0.03 explicitly (tests/test_parity.py:36, test_roundtrip_invariants.py:33, test_wealth_conservation.py:32), so the suite cannot detect the wrong-scale fallback. The only trace is one warning line plus the value stored in result["_meta"]["c_ticks"].

Severity check: "major" is correctly calibrated. This is the user's own active research line (speculation-game-info), and any fresh reproduction today silently produces scientifically non-comparable results — a real defect that will bite. It stops short of "critical" because the historical/documented results were produced with the calibrated value (~28), the fallback prints a warning, and _meta records the c_ticks actually used, so already-published outputs are not corrupted; the damage is confined to future re-runs that ignore the warning line.

findings_verified.title: Unvalidated backend string silently falls back to the pure-Python reference implementation
findings_verified.detail: SpeculationGame.run dispatches with `fn = simulate if self.backend == "vectorized" else run_reference`. Any typo ('vectorised', 'Vectorized', 'numpy') silently selects run_reference. Because the two are bit-identical the results stay correct, but at BASELINE_PARAMS (N=1000, T=20000) the reference's per-agent Python loops (O(N*S*T) with nested step-7 virtual loops) are orders of magnitude slower, turning a typo into a mysterious multi-hour run instead of an immediate error. This contrasts with the strict asserts on history_mode/decision_mode inside both simulate() and run_reference().
findings_verified.suggestion: assert self.backend in ("vectorized", "reference") in run() or __post_init__, matching the mode asserts in the simulators.
findings_verified.verify_note: packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/__init__.py:58 does exactly what the finding says: `fn = simulate if self.backend == "vectorized" else run_reference`, with no validation anywhere (no __post_init__, no assert, dataclass field is a plain str defaulting to "vectorized"). Any misspelling or alternative string silently routes to run_reference, the per-agent pure-Python loop, which at BASELINE_PARAMS (N=1000, T=20000) is orders of magnitude slower. Both in-repo callers (experiments/speculation_game/baseline.py:21 and tests/test_sg_parity.py:39) hard-code backend="vectorized", so no current path is broken — correctness is unaffected (implementations are bit-parity per docs/backbone_parity.md) and only future/interactive use with a typo hits the footgun. This is a genuine but low-impact defect; severity "minor" is correctly calibrated (not major: no wrong results, no active caller affected). Contrast with the strict mode checks inside simulate()/run_reference() supports the inconsistency claim.

findings_verified.title: tests/test_rng.py is an empty 0-byte placeholder — the RNG/seed-policy test was never written

It also slightly inflates confidence when reading the test directory listing.
findings_verified.evidence: wc -c tests/test_rng.py -> 0 bytes.

findings_verified.verify_note: Verified: /home/user/financial-abm-lab/tests/test_rng.py is exactly 0 bytes (wc -c = 0) and has been empty since the initial commit (git log shows only "first_commit"). It contributes zero collected tests while its name implies RNG/seed-policy coverage. No other file dedicated to RNG discipline exists; determinism is only indirectly exercised via seeded parity tests (test_sg_parity.py, test_classical_parity.py, test_prism_family_parity.py), which do pin draw-order stability for the specific covered models but do not enforce a workspace-wide no-global-RNG/seed-policy rule. So the defect is real but partially mitigated: the parity story itself is tested, only the intended dedicated RNG test is missing. This is test-suite hygiene, no wrong results are produced. Severity minor is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: Docstring falsely claims reference and vectorized MG implementations are seed-identical
findings_verified.detail: The module docstring of packages/abm_models/abm_models/minority_game/model.py (lines 19-21) falsely claims run_reference and simulate consume the RNG in the same order and produce seed-identical results. Initialization is in fact stream-identical, but tie-breaking is not: Agent.decide draws rng.choice(best) per agent (line 52) while simulate draws an (N,S) uniform perturbation every step (lines 175-176). Since all scores are tied at t=0, trajectories diverge immediately for every seed (verified: N=11,M=3,S=2,T=50,seed=7 gives attendance [2,8,5,7,...] vs [5,8,8,8,...]). No test covers run_reference (tests/test_classical_parity.py only tests simulate vs the imported YH003 simulate, which carries the same false docstring). The docstring's claim that Agent is the YH004/YH005 base class is also stale — gcmg defines its own BaseMGAgent and the canonical SG has its own separately parity-tested reference. Severity minor: pure documentation drift; no active pipeline uses run_reference, the vectorized path is bit-parity tested, and the two implementations are distributionally equivalent — the risk is a human relying on the claimed invariant for future validation.
findings_verified.evidence: 設計メモ line 20-21: 「両者は同じ RNG 消費順序なので同じ seed で結果が一致する。」 vs decide(): `s_idx = int(rng.choice(best))` (line 52) against simulate(): `perturb = rng.uniform(0.0, 0.5, size=(N, S)); chosen = np.argmax(scores + perturb, axis=1)` (lines 175-176)
findings_verified.suggestion: Either delete/correct the claim (state distributional equivalence only), or make the reference consume the same perturbation-based tie-break and add a parity test mirroring tests/test_sg_parity.py.
findings_verified.verify_note: Empirically reproduced exactly as reported. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/abm_models/abm_models/minority_game/model.py, the docstring (lines 19-21) claims "両者は同じ RNG 消費順序なので同じ seed で結果が一致する" (same RNG consumption order, so same seed gives identical results). Running run_reference(11,3,2,50,7) vs simulate(11,3,2,50,seed=7) in the project venv gives attendance [2,8,5,7,...] vs [5,8,8,8,...] — divergence at t=0, matching the auditor's numbers. Root cause verified at the cited lines: initialization IS seed-identical (I confirmed strategy tables and initial history match bit-for-bit — per-agent rng.choice((S,2^M)) x N consumes the same stream as one (N,S,2^M) call), but the per-step strategy selection is not: decide() (line 52) draws rng.choice(best) per agent, while simulate() (lines 175-176) always draws an (N,S) uniform block. At t=0 all scores are zero, so every agent is tied across all S strategies, guaranteeing immediate divergence for any seed — the claim is false in principle, not just in this run. No mitigation exists: tests/test_classical_parity.py:51-56 only compares packaged simulate() against the imported YH003 simulate() (which contains the identical false docstring); run_reference is never exercised by any test or experiment. However, severity is miscalibrated. This is pure docs-drift with no wrong results in any active path: simulate() is what all experiments/tests use and it is bit-parity verified against the imported original; the two implementations are distributionally equivalent tie-breaks. Moreover, the docstring's forward-pointer that Agent is the base class for YH004/YH005 is itself stale — gcmg/model.py defines its own independent BaseMGAgent (line 37) and the canonical SG (packages/abm_models/sg/) has its own reference/vectorized pair with real bit-parity tests; nothing inherits from this Agent. The defect bites only a human reader who trusts the false invariant for future validation work, so it is genuine but low-impact: minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: ZI docstring claims Gode-Sunder ZI-C budget constraint; no constraint or double auction exists in the code
findings_verified.detail: The ZI model's docstring and describe_complexity() label it Gode & Sunder (1993) Zero-Intelligence Constrained with a 'budget constraint' and 'random limit orders', but _simulate_one_path (model.py:142-165) contains no valuations, no budget bound, and no double-auction matching — it is a Gaussian mid-quote random walk with a linear price-impact term on binomial order imbalance. The provenance label is contradicted by the implementation; rename/relabel the model (e.g. 'unconstrained ZI-style noise baseline') or note the deviation from ZI-C.
findings_verified.evidence: Docstring lines 4-6: 'Prices emerge purely from random order flow against a budget constraint.' vs _simulate_one_path(): `buy_prices = prices[t-1] + rng.normal(0, p.noise_scale * prices[t-1], ...)` — no valuation or budget bound anywhere (lines 142-158).
findings_verified.suggestion: Relabel as 'ZI random-quote null baseline (ZI-U-flavored)' or add the actual budget constraint + CDA crossing if the Gode-Sunder citation is to stand.
findings_verified.verify_note: Read packages/abm_models/abm_models/zero_intelligence/model.py in full. The docstring (lines 1-11) claims 'Prices emerge purely from random order flow against a budget constraint' and cites Gode & Sunder (1993) ZI-C, and describe_complexity() (lines 128-132) repeats 'Zero-Intelligence Constrained ... random limit orders ... (Gode & Sunder 1993)'. But _simulate_one_path (lines 142-165) implements a mid-quote-plus-price-impact random walk: Gaussian quotes centered on the previous price, binomial buyer/seller split, price = mid + price_impact * excess_demand * prev_price, then tick rounding. There are no agent valuations/redemption values, no budget bound on bids/asks, and no continuous double auction matching — the entire defining content of the 'C' in ZI-C is missing. Nothing elsewhere in the file or its ComplexitySpec corrects the label. The claim accurately describes the code; this is genuine docs/provenance drift, not a numerical bug, so 'minor' severity is correctly calibrated (the model runs as an intended null baseline; only its literature attribution is wrong, which matters somewhat given the fingerprint-atlas provenance focus but does not corrupt results).

findings_verified.title: binomial_theory uses P[rS < r_min] while the simulator deactivates on scores <= r_min (strict/non-strict off-by-one)
findings_verified.detail: In simulate(), an agent is active iff best_score > r_min (line 224), so the inactivity probability under the paper's independent-binomial approximation is P[rS <= r_min]^S. binomial_theory computes k_thresh = ceil((rm+T)/2) - 1, i.e., P[rS < r_min]. Because rS is integer-valued with the parity of T, the two differ by the central binomial pmf (~1/sqrt(T)) exactly at attainable r_min values such as the common r_min=0. Numerical check (N=1001, M=2, S=2, T_win=20, r_min=0): simulated mean active = 509.5; theory-as-coded gives 831.2, while the <=-consistent version gives 654.8 — the coded version roughly doubles the approximation error against the curve it is meant to be compared with (paper Fig. 1 style plots).
findings_verified.evidence:
# P[rS < r_min] = P[2X - T < r_min] = P[X < (r_min + T)/2]
k_thresh = np.ceil((rm + T_win) / 2.0) - 1  (lines 284-287)
vs simulate(): active_mask = best_scores > r_min_vec (line 224)
findings_verified.suggestion: Use P[rS <= r_min] (k_thresh = floor((rm + T_win)/2)) to match the simulator's strict-> activity rule, and note the parity of rS in the docstring.
findings_verified.verify_note: Code reading confirms the inconsistency exactly as reported. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/packages/abm_models/abm_models/gcmg/model.py, simulate() line 224 uses `active_mask = best_scores > r_min_vec`, so an agent is INACTIVE iff its best score rS <= r_min (the argmax perturbation in [0,0.5) never changes the integer score used in this comparison). Under the paper's independent-binomial approximation, the per-strategy inactivity probability consistent with the simulator is therefore P[rS <= r_min] = binom.cdf(floor((r_min+T)/2), T, 0.5). binomial_theory() lines 283-293 instead compute k_thresh = ceil((rm+T)/2) - 1, i.e., P[rS < r_min]. Since rS = 2X - T is integer-valued with the parity of T, the two differ by the binomial pmf at the threshold (~1/sqrt(T)) whenever r_min hits an attainable value — notably the default-style r_min = 0 with even T_win. I reproduced the numerical check: N=1001, M=2, S=2, T_win=20, r_min_static=0.0 gives simulated mean active = 509.5, coded theory = 831.2, while the <=-consistent version gives 654.8 (binom.cdf(10,20,0.5) vs cdf(9,...)). So the coded curve roughly doubles the discrepancy against the simulator it is plotted against. The function is actually used in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH004/run_simulation.py:218 (theory overlay on plots), so it affects real research output, but only a diagnostic/approximation curve — the simulation dynamics and any bit-parity claims are untouched, and the binomial approximation is itself crude (the paper notes it only holds in the dilute phase). Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: simulate() docstring claims dt = 1/steps_per_unit, but Params.dt is an independent constant that does not track steps_per_unit
findings_verified.detail: The docstring says 'steps_per_unit : 1 time unit あたりの simulation step 数 (Δt = 1/steps_per_unit = 0.01)', but the transition probabilities and _pdot always use Params.dt = 0.01 regardless of steps_per_unit. Setting steps_per_unit=50 does not make dt=0.02; it makes one recorded 'integer step' equal 0.5 model-time units, silently changing the sampling interval of prices (and hence the timescale of recorded returns) while leaving the physics unchanged. This is live: tests/test_classical_parity.py runs steps_per_unit=20, and fingerprint_atlas MODEL_BOUNDS sweeps steps_per_unit in (50, 100) as if it were a physical knob — those atlas samples differ from the default only in return-sampling frequency, conflating aggregation scale with model behavior in the fingerprints. Also, pdot_window=20 is documented as '0.2 time unit' which likewise only holds for dt=0.01.
findings_verified.evidence: Docstring lines 253-256: 'steps_per_unit : int — 1 time unit あたりの simulation step 数 (Δt = 1/steps_per_unit = 0.01)' vs Params.dt: float = 0.01 (line 39) used in step() independently of steps_per_unit.
findings_verified.suggestion: Either derive dt = 1.0/steps_per_unit inside simulate() (and pdot_window accordingly), or reword the docstring to 'recording interval' and remove steps_per_unit from LHS sweeps.
findings_verified.verify_note: The finding is accurate on every point. (1) packages/abm_models/abm_models/lux_marchesi/model.py:256 docstring states 'Δt = 1/steps_per_unit = 0.01', but Params.dt is an independent constant (line 39, dt: float = 0.01) used throughout step() (lines 143, 157-163) and _pdot() (line 108); steps_per_unit is only the inner-loop count in simulate() (line 273) and never touches dt. Setting steps_per_unit=50 therefore does not change Δt; it makes each recorded 'integer step' span 50 micro-steps of dt=0.01 = 0.5 model-time units, changing only the price sampling interval. (2) The claimed live usages are real: tests/test_classical_parity.py:45 uses steps_per_unit=20, and packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/adapters.py:71 sweeps steps_per_unit over (50, 100) in MODEL_BOUNDS as an LHS 'model parameter' — those atlas samples differ from default only in return-aggregation frequency, so the sweep conflates sampling scale with model dynamics in the fingerprints (a comment at adapters.py:44-45 even acknowledges only wrapper knobs are swept). (3) The pdot_window comment at model.py:103 ('0.2 time unit = 20 sim steps') indeed holds only because dt=0.01 by coincidence with the default steps_per_unit=100. No mitigation exists anywhere: nothing derives dt from steps_per_unit and no test guards the relationship. Severity 'minor' is calibrated correctly: the physics is unchanged and the default configuration is self-consistent; the harm is misleading documentation plus a mildly confounded (but not wrong) atlas parameter sweep in a classical (non-canonical) model, not corrupted results in the active YH research line.

findings_verified.title: calibrate_baseline mutates self.params in place and hardcodes price_impact, and apply_intervention/simulate ignore the CalibrationArtifact
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as written. One nuance to add: for FW, FWParams.price_impact default is already 0.5, so calibrate_baseline's hardcoded 0.6 is the only real override there; the dramatic default-vs-hardcoded gap (0.01→0.5, 0.005→0.5) applies to ZI and CI. Also note the pattern is inherited verbatim from imported/PRISM adapters, and the bit-identical parity tests pin this behavior, so any fix must update the parity baseline or diverge deliberately from the retired PRISM lineage.
findings_verified.evidence:
self.params.noise_scale = target_vol
self.params.price_impact = 0.5   # overrides ZIParams default 0.01, hardcoded
self.params.n_steps = pre_data.n_days
... def apply_intervention(self, calib, intervention): new_params = ZIParams(... self.params ...)  # calib unused
findings_verified.suggestion: Make calibrate_baseline return a new adapter (or have apply_intervention/simulate read calib.calibrated_params), and have wrapper _adapter() copy params via dataclasses.replace instead of mutating the shared object.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every factual element of the finding verifies against the code. (1) packages/abm_models/abm_models/zero_intelligence/model.py:61-63 — calibrate_baseline mutates self.params in place and hardcodes price_impact=0.5, overriding ZIParams.price_impact default 0.01 and any caller-configured value; same at chiarella_iori/model.py:74-76 (0.5 vs CIParams default 0.005) and franke_westerhoff/model.py:70-72 (0.6).
