(2) apply_intervention (zi model.py:78-102) takes calib but never reads it — new_params is built entirely from self.params; simulate() likewise reads only self.params. So the frozen CalibrationArtifact is indeed decorative and correctness depends on mutable adapter state and the calibrate-before-intervene call order. (3) The CI/FW wrapper _adapter() methods (chiarella_iori/__init__.py:23-26, franke_westerhoff/__init__.py:23-26) do `p = self.params or CIParams(); p.n_steps = self.n_steps`, mutating a caller-supplied params object; the ZI wrapper does not (it constructs a fresh ZIParams). Mitigating context that keeps severity at minor: the pattern is copied verbatim from the retired PRISM lineage (imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/zi.py has the identical hardcoded price_impact=0.5 and calib-ignoring apply_intervention), the parity tests (tests/test_prism_family_parity.py) depend on this behavior being preserved, and the in-repo callers (the wrapper classes and PRISM pipeline) always follow the calibrate→intervene→simulate ordering, so no current usage produces wrong results. It is a latent misuse hazard for direct-adapter callers, i.e. an architecture defect, correctly rated minor.

findings_verified.title: Default-scale CB run accumulates ~2-4 GB of per-step cluster sizes and prints progress unconditionally from library code
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as reported, with one nuance: fingerprint_atlas.adapters.build_model already silences the progress print (report_every=10**9) and uses small N/T bounds, so batch atlas runs are unaffected; the ~2.2 GB accumulation (measured: 5418 clusters/step × 8 B × 50000 steps = 2.17 GB, transiently doubled by np.concatenate at line 90) and unconditional stdout printing bite only when using ContBouchaud wrapper defaults or direct simulate() calls, e.g. experiments/classical/baseline.py and notebooks.
findings_verified.evidence:
cluster_sizes_all.append(sizes)  (line 81)
...
all_sizes = np.concatenate(cluster_sizes_all)  (line 90)
and: if report_every and t % report_every == 0: print(f"  step {t}/{T}")  (lines 77-78)
findings_verified.suggestion: Accumulate a bincount histogram of cluster sizes instead of raw arrays (exact for the power-law tail analysis), and gate progress output behind a callback/logging or default report_every=0.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against packages/abm_models/abm_models/cont_bouchaud/model.py. (1) Memory: line 81 appends the full per-step cluster-size array unconditionally and line 90 concatenates. Empirically measured with the repo venv: _cluster_sizes(10000, 0.9) returns ~5418 int64 components per step, so at the ContBouchaud wrapper defaults (N=10000, c=0.9, T=50000 in cont_bouchaud/__init__.py) the list holds ~2.17 GB, and np.concatenate transiently roughly doubles that — the auditor's 2-4 GB figure is accurate. There is no flag to disable collection; experiments/classical/baseline.py only needs kurtosis/Hill on returns yet still pays for cluster collection (T=20000 there, ~0.9-1.8 GB). (2) Printing: lines 77-78 print unconditionally with report_every defaulting to 5000, and the wrapper passes it through. Partial mitigation exists: the atlas adapter (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/adapters.py, build_model) sets report_every=10**9 for cont_bouchaud and its LHS bounds use N=2000-5000, T=2000-3000, so batch atlas runs neither print nor blow up memory (~tens of MB). But the mitigation is caller-side workaround, not a fix, and default/direct usage (wrapper defaults, baseline experiment, notebooks) hits both issues. Severity minor is correctly calibrated: performance/ergonomics only, no wrong results.

findings_verified.title: Classical models are covered only by copy-parity tests; no behavioral test validates any literature-defining property
findings_verified.detail: test_classical_parity.py and test_prism_family_parity.py assert only that the packaged copy equals the imported copy bit-for-bit — which is tautological with respect to scientific correctness: any bug present in YH001-004 or PRISM passes both sides identically (and indeed the confirmed issues above — MG docstring claim, GCMG theory off-by-one, CI tax no-op — all pass the suite). None of the hallmark properties the wrappers themselves advertise are tested anywhere in tests/: MG sigma^2/N U-shape vs alpha=2^M/N ('σ²/N が α=2^M/N に対し U 字を描く'), GCMG excess kurtosis >> 0, CB power-law cluster sizes / fat-tailed returns, LM z staying near compute_zbar() with volatility clustering, or ZI/CI/FW producing non-degenerate volatility after calibration. The class-based MG/GCMG reference implementations (Agent/Market, BaseMGAgent/GCMGAgent/GCMGMarket) have zero test coverage. A cheap seed-pinned behavioral suite (e.g., sigma2_over_N at 3 alphas, kurtosis bounds, cluster-size tail slope) would catch regressions that parity tests structurally cannot.
findings_verified.evidence:
def test_minority_game_parity():
    ...
    a, b = orig.simulate(**kw), packaged(**kw)
    np.testing.assert_array_equal(a["attendance"], b["attendance"])  # copy == copy only
findings_verified.suggestion: Add slow-marked behavioral tests per model asserting the documented stylized property within loose bounds, plus a smoke test of the MG/GCMG class-based reference implementations.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against the repository. tests/test_classical_parity.py (CB/LM/MG/GCMG) and tests/test_prism_family_parity.py (ZI/CI/FW) contain only np.testing.assert_array_equal comparisons between the packaged copy and a dynamically imported original (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH00x/model.py or PRISM). Every assertion in both files is copy==copy; there are no bounds, statistical, or property assertions. A grep of tests/ for kurtosis, sigma^2, U-shape, power-law/cluster tails, or zbar finds hits only in test_preference.py and test_arxiv_ingest.py, which test the fingerprint/preference pipeline, not the ABM models. find over packages/ finds zero test files, so the class-based MG/GCMG implementations indeed have no coverage anywhere. The only slow-marked test is in test_sg_parity.py (SG, also parity-only). So the claim is factually accurate: any bug shared by both copies (as with the already-confirmed MG/GCMG/CI issues) passes the whole suite, and no literature-defining property is validated. This is a genuine testing gap, not a code defect; the copy-parity tests do serve their stated purpose (spec 001 O3: bit-identical extraction), so severity "minor" is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: ACF uses a mixed normalization (numerator mean over n-lag pairs, denominator variance over n), inflating large-lag values by ~n/(n-lag) vs the standard estimator
findings_verified.detail: _acf computes out[lag-1] = nanmean(x_d[:-lag]*x_d[lag:]) / var where var = mean over all n valid points. The standard (Box-Jenkins/statsmodels) ACF divides the sum of n-lag products by n, guaranteeing |rho|<=1 and positive semidefiniteness; this variant multiplies it by n/(n-lag). For the parity metric vol_acf at lag 500 on T=20000 returns that is a ~2.6% systematic inflation (lag 200: ~1%), the same order as the 5% parity tolerance. It is self-consistent across the repo (both sides of the parity test use it), so parity holds, but any comparison of these ACF values with statsmodels output, empirical-literature ACFs, or a future reimplementation will show a lag-dependent bias, and values can exceed 1 in edge cases. Additionally, when max_lag >= number of samples the pair array is empty and np.nanmean emits a RuntimeWarning and returns NaN unguarded. Same code duplicated in imported/.../YH005/analysis.py:41.
findings_verified.evidence:
prod = x_demeaned[:-lag] * x_demeaned[lag:]
m = np.nanmean(prod)          # mean over n-lag pairs
out[lag - 1] = m / var        # var is mean over all n points
findings_verified.suggestion: Either switch to the standard n-denominator estimator (sum of pair products / (n_valid * var)) or document the variant explicitly next to the recorded parity constants; guard lag >= len(x).
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified in packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py: line 32 computes var as the mean of squared deviations over all n valid points, while lines 41-42 compute the lag numerator as np.nanmean over the n-lag pair products. This equals the standard (divide-by-n) ACF estimator scaled by n/(n-lag), exactly as claimed (~2.6% at lag 500 for T~20000). It is the known 'unbiased' autocovariance variant, so arguably a methodological choice, but it loses |rho|<=1 and positive-semidefiniteness guarantees and biases comparisons against statsmodels/literature ACFs. The edge case is also real: for max_lag >= number of samples, x_demeaned[:-lag] is empty, so np.nanmean emits a RuntimeWarning and yields NaN unguarded. Mitigation check: all repo uses (tests/test_sg_parity.py, YH005 analysis, etc.) use the same function on both sides, so parity results are unaffected — consistent with minor severity. The duplicate in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/analysis.py exists as stated.

findings_verified.title: plot_order_size_time_series: log_return and dp modes are misaligned by one step relative to each other and to the order-count panels
findings_verified.detail: In log_return mode (the default), r = np.diff(np.log(p_safe)) (core.py:374) gives r[i] = ln p[i+1] − ln p[i], the return realized by step i+1's orders (prices[t] is recorded post-step in sg/vectorized.py:210 while num_orders_by_size[t] holds step t's orders), but it is plotted at x=i via t_r = np.arange(x0, r_slice_end) on a sharex figure whose lower panels show size_hist at x=t. dp mode (r = p - p_prev, plotted at x=t) is correctly aligned. So the default-mode figure shifts returns one step earlier relative to the order-count panels, and the y-label "r(t) = ln p(t) − ln p(t−1)" mislabels the plotted values. Fix: plot log returns at np.arange(x0+1, r_slice_end+1) (with matching slice), or diff-align as in dp mode. Same code in imported speculation-game-info YH005 analysis.py. Plot-only defect; returned statistics unaffected.
findings_verified.evidence:
r = np.diff(np.log(p_safe))  # length T-1; r[i] spans (i, i+1)
...
t_r = np.arange(r_slice_start, r_slice_end)  # plots r[i] at x=i, same axis as size_hist[t]
findings_verified.suggestion: In log_return mode plot r at t_r = arange(x0+1, r_slice_end+1) (or prepend NaN so r aligns with the step that realized it), matching the dp-mode convention.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py:354-414 and the simulator packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/vectorized.py:209-226 (reference.py identical). The simulator records prices[t] = price AFTER step t's orders and num_orders_by_size[t] = step t's orders, so the return attributable to step t's orders is p[t]-p[t-1]. In dp mode (core.py:379-380) r has length T with r[t]=p[t]-p_prev[t], plotted at x=t — correctly aligned with size_hist[t]. In log_return mode (the default, core.py:374) r=np.diff(np.log(p_safe)) has r[i]=ln p[i+1]-ln p[i], the return caused by step i+1's orders, yet t_r=np.arange(x0, r_slice_end) plots it at x=i on a sharex axis with the order-count panels at x=i. So the two modes disagree by one step and in the default mode the return shown at t corresponds to orders shown at t+1 below it; the axis label "r(t) = ln p(t) − ln p(t−1)" is also wrong for what is plotted. No caller/test mitigates it (function only saves a PNG; returned aggregate dict is unaffected). Severity minor is correctly calibrated: it distorts a mechanism figure's claimed contemporaneity by one step but corrupts no numerical results or data.

findings_verified.title: Legacy analysis stack duplicates the canonical estimators with conflicting conventions: raw kurtosis (Gaussian=3) vs excess (Gaussian=0), and NaN-unaware ACF
findings_verified.detail: summarize() reports "kurtosis" as np.mean(z**4) (raw, Gaussian=3) while the canonical packages/stylized_facts kurtosis_windowed returns z**4 mean - 3.0 (excess, Gaussian=0) — two repo functions named kurtosis differing by exactly 3, an easy source of cross-experiment misreads (the recorded parity value kurt_1=3.63 is excess; the legacy function would report 6.63 for the same series). abs_return_autocorrelation (volatility_clustering.py:28-37) is also NaN-unaware: a single NaN return (which log_returns_from_prices produces for p<=0) poisons mean/var and yields an all-NaN ACF silently. summarize() currently has no callers in the repo (only YH005_PLAN.md references it), so this is dead-but-importable code that drifts from the canonical package.
findings_verified.evidence:
"kurtosis": float(np.mean(((r - mean_r) / np.sqrt(var_r)) ** 4) if var_r > 0 else 0.0)  # raw, no -3
vs packages/stylized_facts/core.py:109: return float((z ** 4).mean() - 3.0)  # excess
findings_verified.suggestion: Either delete the legacy imported/analysis stack (superseded by packages/stylized_facts) or rename the key to kurtosis_raw and add a NaN guard, so future readers cannot mix the two conventions.
findings_verified.verify_note: All factual claims check out on inspection. (1) imported/speculation-game-info/analysis/stylized_facts.py:41-43 computes "kurtosis" as np.mean(z**4) with no -3 subtraction (raw kurtosis, Gaussian=3), while the canonical packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py kurtosis_windowed returns (z**4).mean() - 3.0 (excess, Gaussian=0) — two same-named metrics in one repo differing by exactly 3. (2) analysis/volatility_clustering.py abs_return_autocorrelation uses plain abs_r.mean()/var()/np.mean with no NaN handling; a single NaN return propagates to mean and var, making every ACF lag NaN silently (var==0 guard doesn't fire since NaN != 0). (3) Dead-but-importable status verified: the legacy analysis/ package contains only __init__.py (empty), stylized_facts.py, tail_exponent.py, volatility_clustering.py; no code in the repo imports it. YH005's `from analysis import log_returns_from_prices, stylized_facts_summary` resolves to the sibling experiments/YH005/analysis.py (those names do not exist in the legacy package), and the only reference to summarize() is documentation in YH005_PLAN.md. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated: it is a genuine convention conflict and a latent NaN trap, but the code is unreachable by any current caller, sits in imported/ (not the canonical packages/), and produces no wrong results today — the risk is future misuse/drift, which is exactly minor/architecture.

findings_verified.title: Docstring states alpha < 3 implies infinite variance ('Mandelbrot condition') — mathematically wrong
findings_verified.detail: Docstring (line 4) wrongly claims variance is infinite for alpha < 3; correct condition is alpha <= 2. The (2,3) cubic-law regime has finite variance but diverging fourth moment. Also, hill_estimator (line 38) divides by np.sum(log_ratios) without guarding sum==0, returning inf instead of NaN for degenerate all-equal tails, unlike the canonical guarded implementation. Docs-only defect in an imported (non-canonical) analysis helper; no numerical results affected.
findings_verified.evidence: """...α < 3 なら分散が無限 (Mandelbrot 条件)。..."""  # tail_exponent.py:4
findings_verified.suggestion: Correct to: variance infinite iff alpha <= 2; alpha in (2,4) implies finite variance, infinite fourth moment. Add the sum<=0 guard mirroring the canonical implementation.
findings_verified.verify_note: Line 4 of imported/speculation-game-info/analysis/tail_exponent.py literally states "α < 3 なら分散が無限 (Mandelbrot 条件)". For tail index α, moments of order >= α diverge, so variance is infinite iff α <= 2; α in (2,3) — the cubic-law regime central to SG fat-tail claims — has finite variance. The docstring is therefore mathematically wrong exactly as the finding claims. The secondary edge case is also real: line 38 `alpha = k / np.sum(log_ratios)` lacks the sum==0 guard present in the canonical hill_mle_tail_index, yielding inf plus a RuntimeWarning for degenerate input. However, this is documentation-only in an imported analysis helper; the estimator computes correct values on real data, so no results are corrupted. Severity "minor" is appropriately calibrated.

findings_verified.title: Silent failure modes in active indicator code: quantile_slopes swallows all exceptions into NaN; hill_estimator lets NaN inputs poison the sort order (guarded to NaN, not detected)
findings_verified.detail: quantile_slopes wraps sm.QuantReg(...).fit in `except Exception: out[tau] = nan` — any real failure (singular design, convergence failure, wrong dtype, API change) is indistinguishable from a legitimately small sample, and q90-q10 slope diff (one of the 5 main indicators) silently drops out of the ensemble without any log line. hill_estimator (line 134) filters with values[values != 0], which retains NaN (NaN != 0 is True); np.sort places NaN last so the descending slice [::-1] puts NaNs at the top of the 'tail', making log_ratios NaN — the mean_lr finiteness guard then returns NaN, so the result is safe but any NaN contamination in dG silently zeroes out the Hill indicator instead of being reported. Both patterns follow the documented 'return NaN, caller handles' policy, but neither distinguishes data-driven NaN from software failure.
findings_verified.evidence:
try:
    model = sm.QuantReg(y, X).fit(q=tau, max_iter=5000)
    out[tau] = float(model.params[1])
except Exception:
    out[tau] = float("nan")
...
abs_vals = np.abs(values[values != 0]).astype(np.float64)  # NaN passes the != 0 filter
findings_verified.suggestion: Catch only the expected exceptions (np.linalg.LinAlgError, ValueError) and log a warning with tau and n; in hill_estimator drop NaNs explicitly (values[np.isfinite(values) & (values != 0)]).
findings_verified.verify_note: Both claims verified against the source. (1) analysis.py:111-116 wraps sm.QuantReg().fit in a bare `except Exception: out[tau] = nan`; quantile_slope_diff (lines 123-125) propagates NaN, and the ensemble aggregators (aggregate_ensemble.py lines 195, 337, 372, 581) use .dropna(), so any software failure in quantile regression silently removes the q90-q10 indicator from the ensemble with no log line, indistinguishable from a small-sample NaN. (2) analysis.py:134 filters with values[values != 0], which retains NaN (NaN != 0 is True in numpy); np.sort places NaN last, the [::-1] slice puts NaNs at the top of the tail, log_ratios becomes NaN, and the finiteness guard at line 145 returns NaN — safe output, but NaN contamination in dG silently nulls the Hill indicator rather than being reported. Callers pass delta_g to hill_estimator without dropna (aggregate_ensemble.py:100), so the path is reachable in principle. No mitigation: no logging or NaN-source accounting exists for these indicators (only w_init NaNs are counted, line 298). Severity minor is correct: this is an observability/robustness defect in active research code (YH006), but it cannot produce wrong numeric results — only NaN/dropped trials — and requires an unusual runtime failure or upstream NaN contamination to trigger.

findings_verified.title: arxiv_id keeps the version suffix despite comment claiming it is stripped — paper revisions create duplicate rows and duplicate Groq spend
findings_verified.detail: arxiv_ingest.query_arxiv stores the versioned arxiv id ('2412.01234v2') despite the inline comment claiming the version is stripped (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py:108-109). Because literature_methods.arxiv_id is UNIQUE on the exact string (db.py:208) and both the metadata upsert (db.py:265) and the already_extracted skip in ingest() (arxiv_ingest.py:246-249) compare exact versioned strings, an arXiv paper revision causes a re-run of the same query to insert a second row and pay for a second Groq extraction. _select_literature_for_context (propose.py:291) has no base-id dedup, so both versions can occupy slots in the 7-paper proposer context. classify_references works around this on the reference side via _arxiv_base (propose.py:159), but ingest never normalizes. Impact is duplicate rows, one redundant LLM call per revision, and one wasted prompt slot in a support pipeline — low-impact but genuine; fix by stripping the version at ingest (e.g. use r.get_short_id() plus _arxiv_base-style stripping) or deduping by base id at context selection.
findings_verified.evidence:
# entry_id like 'http://arxiv.org/abs/2412.01234v2' → strip prefix + version
eid = r.entry_id.rsplit("/", 1)[-1]
findings_verified.suggestion: Normalise at ingest: base = re.sub(r'v\d+$', '', eid); store base in arxiv_id (optionally keep the version in a separate column) and upsert on the base id, refreshing metadata when a newer version arrives.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves exactly as claimed. arxiv_ingest.py:108-109: the comment says "strip prefix + version" but `r.entry_id.rsplit("/", 1)[-1]` only strips the URL prefix — 'http://arxiv.org/abs/2412.01234v2' is stored as '2412.01234v2'. db.py:208 makes literature_methods.arxiv_id UNIQUE on that versioned string, and upsert_literature_metadata (db.py:265) checks existence with an exact `WHERE arxiv_id = ?` match, so after an arXiv revision (the arxiv library's entry_id always carries the latest version) a re-run of the same query inserts a second row. In ingest() (arxiv_ingest.py:246-249) the already_extracted check also matches the exact versioned id, so the new row gets an independent Groq extraction — duplicate spend. Downstream, _select_literature_for_context (propose.py:291-313) has no base-id dedup, so v1 and v2 rows can each occupy a slot in the literature_top_n=7 prompt budget (propose.py:316,341). The version-stripping helper _arxiv_base (propose.py:159-161) is used only by classify_references (propose.py:198,204), never at ingest. tests/test_arxiv_ingest.py only tests unversioned ids, so nothing guards this. No refuting evidence found; the db.py schema comment even acknowledges versioned strings may be stored, but that does not prevent the duplication. Severity recalibrated: the defect is genuine and will trigger on common arXiv revisions, but impact is one redundant LLM extraction (small cost) plus a duplicated paper wasting one of seven proposer prompt slots in a support pipeline — not wrong results in canonical/active research code — so minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: _validate_proposal never type-checks param or predicted_fingerprint values from the LLM
findings_verified.detail: _validate_proposal (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/propose.py:424-446) validates key presence only: params values and predicted_fingerprint values are never type- or range-checked, despite MODEL_BOUNDS being available in scope and the prompt mandating values within ±30% of bounds. Non-numeric values are accepted and stored. Failures then surface in execute_proposal (propose_cli.py): (a) bad params crash inside build_model/model.run (lines 236-237) and the except at 241-243 marks the proposal 'rejected', conflating type errors with review rejection; (b) string predicted_fingerprint values crash at (predicted_vec - mu_feat) (propose_cli.py:268-271) AFTER insert_run (line 247) has committed the run row and BEFORE update_proposal_status (line 281), leaving an orphaned run row and a proposal stuck in 'proposed'/'approved' state.
findings_verified.evidence:
if not isinstance(p["params"], dict) or not p["params"]:
    return False, "params must be a non-empty dict"
# require every key ... (no check that values are numeric / within ±30% of bounds)
missing = set(FEATURE_NAMES) - set(pf.keys())
findings_verified.suggestion: In _validate_proposal, coerce every param and fingerprint value with float() (reject on failure) and enforce the documented ±30% bound tolerance so invalid proposals are rejected at proposal time, not execution time.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against the code. (1) _validate_proposal (propose.py:413-447) checks only key presence for both params (lines 424-430: isinstance dict + required keys from MODEL_BOUNDS) and predicted_fingerprint (lines 440-446: isinstance dict + FEATURE_NAMES key coverage). There is no isinstance(v, (int, float)) check on any value and no range check, even though MODEL_BOUNDS[p["target_model"]] is right there in scope. A proposal with params={'N': 'about 300', ...} or predicted_fingerprint={'hill': 'high', ...} passes and is persisted by insert_proposal (propose.py:488-498). (2) The downstream consequences are exactly as claimed in propose_cli.py execute_proposal: build_model(p["target_model"], p["params"]) at line 236 is inside a try whose except (lines 241-243) sets status='rejected' — so a type-garbage proposal gets the same status as an LLM-review rejection. Worse, string params for cont_bouchaud go through float(lo + ...)? No — LHS sampling is separate; build_model passes values straight to the dataclass constructor, so a string N either crashes there or is stored on the instance and fails/mis-behaves during model.run(). (3) The predicted_fingerprint path: the try block ends at line 240; insert_run commits the run row at line 247, then predicted_vec = np.array([...]) at 268-270 builds a dtype='<U..' array from string values, and (predicted_vec - mu_feat) at line 271 raises numpy UFuncTypeError. That exception propagates with the run row already committed and update_proposal_status (line 281) never reached — proposal stays 'proposed'/'approved' with an orphaned run, the same inconsistent-state mode the finding describes. No test guards this: tests exercise dry_run_payload with well-formed numeric proposals only. Severity 'minor' is calibrated correctly: this is the exploratory LLM-atlas pipeline (not canonical SG/parity code), the JSON-mode prompt makes numeric values the overwhelmingly common case, and the failure is loud (crash) rather than silently corrupting research results — the only silent path (mis-instantiated dataclass surviving run()) is unlikely.

findings_verified.title: distance_matrix uses np.nansum, so NaN feature pairs silently contribute zero distance
findings_verified.detail: np.nansum over squared diffs treats any dimension where either vector is NaN as distance 0, shrinking distances for NaN-laden fingerprints instead of scaling by the number of observed dimensions or excluding the rows. Most callers pre-filter via collect_population, but preference.novelty_score (preference.py:114 uses the same nansum pattern on unfiltered rows) and propose._describe_sparse_regions / _per_family_centroids feed load_runs output straight in — a partially-NaN run looks artificially close to everything, biasing the 'sparse region' and novelty diagnostics handed to the LLM.
findings_verified.evidence:
diff = fps_std[:, None, :] - fps_std[None, :, :]
return np.sqrt(np.nansum(diff ** 2, axis=-1))
findings_verified.suggestion: Either require finite inputs (assert) and filter at every call site, or rescale: d^2 * (n_features / n_observed_pairs), which is the standard partial-distance correction.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is accurate on every point checked. (1) fingerprint.py:195 (`np.sqrt(np.nansum(diff ** 2, axis=-1))`) and preference.py:114 (identical pattern in novelty_score) do treat any NaN feature dimension as contributing zero squared distance, with no scaling by observed-dimension count and no row exclusion. (2) NaN fingerprints are a real, designed-in state: fingerprint.py:150-151 returns an all-NaN vector for series shorter than 50, and lines 166-171 set acf_abs_long/acf_decay to NaN for short series; standardize() uses nanmean/nanstd so NaNs survive standardization. (3) The unfiltered paths exist exactly as reported: propose.py summarize_corpus (line 318) calls load_runs(db_path) and hands the raw rows to _per_family_centroids (line 351) and _describe_sparse_regions (line 354, which calls distance_matrix at line 254) — neither goes through collect_population (db.py:475-489), which is the only NaN filter and is used only by atlas.py. Likewise label_cli.py:258-259 feeds load_runs output directly into propose_next_k → novelty_score (preference.py:152-165) with no finite check. Concrete failure: a run with an all-NaN or partially-NaN fingerprint gets pairwise distances of 0 (or shrunken), so it appears maximally dense — it is excluded from the "most isolated" sparse-region list it might genuinely belong to, and its novelty score in the active-learning labeling loop is understated, biasing both the LLM proposal context and the UCB ranking. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated: this distorts exploratory/diagnostic heuristics (LLM prompt context, labeling order), not canonical model results or the parity-verified SG/classical cores, and the main atlas path is already guarded by collect_population.

findings_verified.title: Fallback player-count estimate for priceless models uses a wrong formula (2*max - min instead of max + min)
findings_verified.detail: Fallback N estimate in series_for_fingerprint (adapters.py:194) uses 2*max−min instead of max+min on an attendance series that fluctuates around N/2, yielding ≈N/2+3δ and corrupting excess = 2A − N. Currently dead code because MG and GCMG always return a 2-D "actions" array, but reachable through the exported API if a future variant omits actions (e.g. memory-saving attendance-only runs).
findings_verified.evidence:
n_players = int(2 * att.max() - att.min()) if att.size else 0
n_players = max(n_players, 1)
excess = 2.0 * att - n_players
findings_verified.suggestion: Use int(att.max() + att.min()) (or better: require N in result/params and raise instead of guessing — a silent wrong N is worse than an error).
findings_verified.verify_note: Attendance in both priceless models is the one-side headcount around N/2: minority_game/model.py:186 sets attendance[t] = (N + excess) // 2, and gcmg/model.py:236 sets attendance[t] = (actions == 1).sum(). Reconstructing N from such a series requires att.max() + att.min() ≈ N; the fallback at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/adapters.py:194 computes int(2*att.max() - att.min()) ≈ N/2 + 3δ, so excess = 2*att - n_players (line 196) would be shifted by roughly N/2, distorting the attendance_excess fingerprint exactly as claimed. The branch is currently dead in practice — MG returns "actions" unconditionally (actions_log allocated at model.py:165, returned at :206) and GCMG likewise (:256), and all in-repo callers (populate.py:73, propose_cli.py:238, label_cli.py:103) pass fresh model results — but series_for_fingerprint is exported public API (__init__.py) that accepts arbitrary result dicts, so the defective path is reachable, merely unexercised. Latent, no wrong results today; severity minor is calibrated correctly.

findings_verified.title: Docstrings still describe a 6-D fingerprint and a Llama default model; code is 9-D and gpt-oss-120b
findings_verified.detail: FEATURE_NAMES has 9 entries (v4), but the package docstring says '6-D vector' listing only the v1 six features; db.py:5 documents fingerprint_json as 'the 6-vector'; populate.py:7 says 'fingerprint(series) -> 6-vector'. Separately, propose.py:8 says the proposer calls 'free Llama 3.3 70B by default' while DEFAULT_GROQ_MODEL (propose.py:44) is 'openai/gpt-oss-120b' with a long comment explaining Llama was rejected. Anyone reading headers to understand the stored schema or to reproduce results will be misled about both the vector dimensionality and the model that generated stored artifacts.
findings_verified.evidence: """fingerprint_atlas — ... one return series -> a 6-D vector of stylized facts (volatility, kurtosis, Hill tail index, return ACF, |return| ACF, leverage)." vs fingerprint.py FEATURE_NAMES = [9 names]
findings_verified.suggestion: Sweep the package for '6-vector'/'6-D'/'Llama 3.3' and align with FEATURE_NAMES/DEFAULT_GROQ_MODEL; consider deriving the docstring counts programmatically in one place.
findings_verified.verify_note: All four cited docstrings match the finding verbatim. packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/__init__.py:3-4 says "6-D vector" listing the v1 six features, while fingerprint.py:19-31 defines FEATURE_NAMES with 9 entries (v4, adding acf_absret_long, acf_absret_decay, agg_kurt_decay) and fingerprint() returns len(FEATURE_NAMES)=9. db.py:5 says fingerprint_json is "the 6-vector"; populate.py:7 says "fingerprint(series) -> 6-vector". propose.py:8 claims "free Llama 3.3 70B by default" while propose.py:44 sets DEFAULT_GROQ_MODEL = "openai/gpt-oss-120b", with a comment explicitly saying other free-tier models (i.e., Llama) were rejected after A/B testing. No code misbehaves — pure documentation drift — so minor severity is correctly calibrated for a docs-drift finding that misleads readers about stored schema dimensionality and provenance of LLM-generated artifacts.

findings_verified.title: Proposal status transitions are unguarded: approve/reject can overwrite 'executed' and enable duplicate execution
findings_verified.detail: update_proposal_status writes any status string with no state-machine check. `propose_cli approve 7` on an already-executed proposal resets status to 'approved' while executed_run_id/actual_* survive via COALESCE; execute_proposal only refuses when status not in ('proposed','approved'), so the reset proposal can be executed again, inserting a second run row and overwriting the calibration measurements (prediction_error is recomputed against a different standardisation population, muddying the analytics learning curve). Also, execute_proposal's failure path sets status='rejected' (propose_cli.py:242), conflating 'human rejected the idea' with 'model crashed', which biases any later analysis of rejection reasons.
findings_verified.evidence: cur = con.execute("UPDATE proposals SET status = ?, updated_at = ?, ... WHERE id = ?", (status, ...))  # no check of current status
findings_verified.suggestion: Enforce allowed transitions (proposed→approved/rejected, approved→executed/rejected) inside update_proposal_status, and add a distinct 'failed' status for execution errors.
findings_verified.verify_note: db.py:174-198 update_proposal_status performs an unconditional UPDATE with no check of the current status; propose_cli.py cmd_approve (line 195) passes any id straight through, so approving an already-executed proposal resets status to 'approved'. execute_proposal's only guard (propose_cli.py:222) accepts 'approved', so the proposal re-executes: insert_run creates a second run row and the final update_proposal_status call (lines 281-286) supplies non-None executed_run_id/actual_fingerprint/actual_novelty_distance/prediction_error, which override the prior values through COALESCE (COALESCE keeps old values only when the new argument is NULL). prediction_error is recomputed against the then-current run population's standardisation (lines 258-273), so the overwritten value is not comparable to the original — muddying learning-curve analytics as claimed. The crash path at line 242 indeed sets status='rejected', conflating model failure with human rejection. No test or caller mitigates this. Impact is limited to the exploratory fingerprint_atlas proposal pipeline and requires manual CLI misuse (approve of an executed id), so 'minor' severity is correct.

findings_verified.title: execute_proposal — the highest-risk function in the package — has no test coverage
findings_verified.detail: test_propose.py exercises the dry-run propose/validate/store path and drives update_proposal_status directly with hand-made values (line 330: prediction_error=1.0), but never calls propose_cli.execute_proposal, so the index-misalignment bug, the None-formatting crash in cmd_auto, and the non-numeric-params failure mode are all unreachable by the suite. A test that populates a tiny DB including one deliberately NaN-fingerprint run and then executes a dry-run-stored proposal would have caught the critical finding above.
findings_verified.evidence: grep shows only 'update_proposal_status(..., prediction_error=1.0,)' — no import or call of execute_proposal in tests/
findings_verified.suggestion: Add an integration test: insert 3 clean runs + 1 NaN-fingerprint run, store a proposal via dry_run_payload, run execute_proposal with a fast model (e.g. minority_game with tiny T), and assert prediction_error/actual_novelty are finite and computed against the correct row.
findings_verified.verify_note: Grep across the repo shows execute_proposal is defined only in packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/propose_cli.py (line 207) and is never imported or called from tests/. tests/test_propose.py's "end-to-end" test (test_end_to_end_execute_links_run_and_records_error, line 303) explicitly says it drives "the execute logic directly without going through the CLI argparser" and reimplements the execution path inline (build_model → run → fingerprint → insert_run → update_proposal_status with hand-made prediction_error=1.0), so none of execute_proposal's actual code — including its error-computation, index alignment, or cmd_auto formatting — is exercised by the suite. The claim is exactly as stated: a coverage gap that would let the previously reported bugs pass CI. Severity 'minor' is correct for a testing-category finding (it causes no wrong results by itself).

findings_verified.title: ingest() reloads and re-parses the entire literature table for every paper in the batch
findings_verified.detail: Inside the per-paper loop, load_literature(db_path) fetches ALL rows (full abstracts + tag parsing) just to answer two point queries: 'does this arxiv_id exist?' and 'is it extracted?'. That is O(batch_size × table_size) rows materialised per ingest run; with a few thousand ingested papers and --max 200, that's hundreds of thousands of full-row parses per invocation, all on the hot path between rate-limited API calls. It also races with nothing (single user) but keeps re-opening connections per call.
findings_verified.evidence:
for p in papers:
    # upsert metadata (cheap)
    before_rows = load_literature(db_path)
    had_id = any(r["arxiv_id"] == p["arxiv_id"] for r in before_rows)
findings_verified.suggestion: One `SELECT arxiv_id, extracted_by_model FROM literature_methods` before the loop into a dict, updated locally as rows are inserted — or a targeted point query per paper.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is accurate. In packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py, inside the `for p in papers:` loop (line 229), line 231 calls `load_literature(db_path)` on every iteration. `load_literature` (db.py:308-333) executes a SELECT of all 19 columns (including full abstracts, mechanism_summary, and tag fields that get parsed into lists) over the whole literature_methods table with no WHERE clause when called with defaults, opening a fresh sqlite connection each time. The result is used only for two point checks: `had_id = any(r["arxiv_id"] == p["arxiv_id"] ...)` (line 232) and `already_extracted = any(...)` (lines 246-249). Both could be answered by a single indexed SELECT of two columns for one arxiv_id (arxiv_id is UNIQUE), or by one table load before the loop. Cost is O(batch_size × table_size) full-row materialisations per ingest run, exactly as reported. It is purely a performance issue in tooling (the LLM/arXiv pipeline is rate-limited anyway, so wall-clock impact is masked but real for large tables); correctness is unaffected. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: Lint step is neutered with '|| true' — ruff can never fail CI despite the step claiming to lint
findings_verified.detail: ci.yml:22 appends `|| true` to `uv run ruff check packages experiments src tests`, making the lint step decorative: ruff's exit code is discarded, so the job stays green on any lint failure. There is no other lint enforcement (no pre-commit hook; other workflows don't run ruff). Running the same command locally yields 31 errors (F401 x13, E702 x8, F541 x4, F841 x3, E741 x3), meaning CI would fail today without the suppression — confirming the step currently masks real findings. Mitigating the original 'major' rating: all 31 errors are unused-import/unused-variable/style issues, none are bug-class (no F821), and the test step is independently enforced. Fix: either drop `|| true` after cleaning the 31 errors (18 auto-fixable via `ruff check --fix`), or rename the step to make its advisory nature explicit.
findings_verified.evidence: ci.yml:22 'run: uv run ruff check packages experiments src tests || true'
findings_verified.suggestion: Drop '|| true'; if some paths are not yet clean, use per-path ruff configuration or --exit-zero only for the not-yet-migrated trees, keeping packages/ and experiments/ strict.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is factually accurate. /home/user/financial-abm-lab/.github/workflows/ci.yml line 22 reads `run: uv run ruff check packages experiments src tests || true`, so the "Lint (packages + experiments)" step can never fail the job regardless of ruff's exit code. I verified there is no compensating enforcement elsewhere: no .pre-commit-config.yaml exists, and the other two workflows (ingest_arxiv.yml, propose.yml) do not run ruff. Running the exact CI command in the repo confirms the step is currently swallowing real failures: ruff reports 31 errors (13x F401 unused imports, 8x E702, 4x F541, 3x F841 unused variables, 3x E741 ambiguous names), so without `|| true` CI would be red today — the codebase is demonstrably not lint-clean despite the green lint step. However, the severity is miscalibrated: none of the 31 swallowed errors are correctness-class (no F821 undefined names; all are unused-import/unused-variable/style findings), tests are enforced separately and all pass, and an advisory-only lint step is a deliberate pattern in some repos. This is genuine hygiene debt and false-confidence signaling, but it hides no defect that produces wrong results, so it is minor rather than major.

findings_verified.title: The fabm harness is empty scaffolding: config.py, the example YAML, and both harness test files are 0 bytes, and make_rngs is dead code
findings_verified.detail: src/fabm/ is inert leftover scaffolding from the pre-monorepo repo: config.py, __init__.py, configs/experiment_example.yaml, tests/test_config.py, and tests/test_rng.py are 0 bytes, and src/fabm/rng.py's make_rngs (a correct SeedSequence.spawn(5) multi-stream pattern) is imported by nothing; the root pyproject also lacks [build-system], so the src/ layout is never installed. This is not docs-drift: the README section citing it is explicitly an appendix labeled '当初の設計メモ' — conventions the original scaffold espoused, to be adopted in Stage B (in progress per specs/001) — not a claim of existing functionality. Nor do the empty test files mislead the suite: pytest collects 0 tests from them (exit 5 when run alone), so the 74 green tests are all real. Remaining issue is housekeeping: delete or implement the placeholders, and if the multi-stream RNG discipline is meant to survive into Stage B, wire make_rngs into packages/experiments rather than leaving it dead; empty test files could otherwise silently mask a never-implemented harness later.
findings_verified.evidence: wc -c: src/fabm/config.py 0 bytes, configs/experiment_example.yaml 0 bytes, tests/test_config.py 0, tests/test_rng.py 0; Grep 'fabm' across the repo matches only README.md; pyproject.toml has [project] but no [build-system].
findings_verified.suggestion: Either delete src/fabm + configs/ + the empty test files (with a spec note that the harness was deferred), or implement the promised pydantic config schema and wire make_rngs into experiments; never keep 0-byte test files.
findings_verified.verify_note: The physical facts verify: src/fabm/config.py, src/fabm/__init__.py, configs/experiment_example.yaml, tests/test_config.py, and tests/test_rng.py are all 0 bytes; src/fabm/rng.py contains a correct but never-imported make_rngs (grep for 'fabm' hits only README.md); pyproject.toml has no [build-system], so the src/ layout is never installed. However, the two arguments used to justify 'major' severity both fail. (1) Docs-drift: the README section is explicitly headed 'Appendix — fabm 当初の設計メモ (Stage B で packages 化する規約)' and states these are conventions the ORIGINAL scaffold espoused, to be adopted during Stage B core design ('元 financial-abm-lab scaffold が掲げていた規約。Stage B の core 設計時に踏襲する'). specs/001 confirms Stage B is 進行中. The README documents historical/planned design intent, not a claim of current functionality — no drift. (2) The 'sharpest hazard' claim is wrong in effect: running the two empty test files yields pytest exit code 5 (no tests collected); they add zero tests to the 74-test suite and cannot fabricate passing coverage. No canonical or active research code (packages/, experiments/, imported/speculation-game-info) touches fabm. What remains is genuine but inert leftover scaffolding: dead placeholder files and an unwired make_rngs — low-impact housekeeping, correctly 'minor' per the given definitions (genuine but low-impact), not 'major'.

findings_verified.title: Prompt-injection path from arbitrary arXiv abstracts into repo-committed content under contents:write, plus inline interpolation of workflow inputs into shell
findings_verified.detail: Indirect prompt-injection surface exists: ingest_arxiv.yml (lines 98-106, 122-145) sends untrusted arXiv titles/abstracts to Groq and commits the LLM's extracted fields to data/literature_methods.json via an auto-PR under contents:write/pull-requests:write, with a PR body encouraging quick merges; propose.py (lines 341-356) later injects those fields verbatim into the proposal-generation prompt; line 102 also inline-interpolates a workflow_dispatch input into a shell script (hygiene issue only, dispatch-gated). Impact is capped well below 'major': the propose CI workflow rebuilds its DB from scratch and never loads the committed snapshot, so the ingest→propose chain only operates on the researcher's local machine; proposal 'execution' is a whitelisted, bounds-validated local ABM parameter sweep (propose_cli.py:207-256, MODEL_BOUNDS check at propose.py:422), not code execution; both workflows trigger only on schedule/dispatch and commits are pinned via add-paths to two data/markdown paths. Residual risk is adversarial steering of literature summaries and proposal text/parameters in human-reviewed PRs of a solo research repo — content pollution, not code/secret compromise. Recommended hardening: move inputs.no_extract into env, treat literature fields as untrusted in prompts, and soften the 'just merge' review hint.
findings_verified.evidence: ingest_arxiv.yml:26-28 'permissions: contents: write / pull-requests: write'; line 102 inline '${{ github.event.inputs.no_extract || 'false' }}' inside a run script; PR body lines 143-144 'Or just merge'; propose.py:341-355 injects load_literature rows into the LLM context.
findings_verified.suggestion: Scope permissions to the minimum per job, move all github.event.inputs.* into env: blocks instead of inline ${{ }} in run scripts, sanitize/length-cap LLM-derived fields before committing, and treat literature-derived text as untrusted in the propose prompt (delimit + instruct the model accordingly); keep 'execute' strictly parameter-driven (build_model from a whitelist, which it currently is — preserve that invariant with a test).
findings_verified.verify_note: The data flow the auditor describes is real and I can point to the exact lines: ingest_arxiv.yml lines 98-106 feed arbitrary arXiv abstracts through Groq and lines 122-145 commit the LLM output to data/literature_methods.json via auto-PR under contents:write/pull-requests:write (lines 26-28), with a PR body inviting low-scrutiny merges (line 144); packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/propose.py lines 341-356 inject the stored mechanism_summary/tags/novelty_signal verbatim into the propose LLM prompt; line 102 of the workflow inline-interpolates a workflow_dispatch input into a run script. So the finding cannot be refuted on facts. However, the impact is materially overstated on three points, which drops severity to minor: (1) the committed snapshot never feeds the propose CI job — propose.yml (lines 48-61) rebuilds the DB from scratch via populate/populate_refs/methods_cli seed and no code path loads data/literature_methods.json, so in Actions the literature context is empty and the ingest→propose chain only completes when the researcher manually ingests and proposes locally; (2) 'executed downstream' is not code execution — propose_cli.py execute_proposal (lines 207-256) only calls build_model on a target_model validated against the MODEL_BOUNDS whitelist (propose.py:422) and runs a local, human-initiated simulation; add-paths pins commits to data/literature_methods.json and proposals/**, so the LLM cannot alter code or workflows; (3) both workflows trigger only on schedule/workflow_dispatch, so the ${{ inputs }} interpolation is reachable only by the solo researcher, and GITHUB_TOKEN can open but not merge PRs. Worst-case harm is adversarial pollution of human-reviewed literature summaries and proposal prose/parameter suggestions — research-content bias, not compromise of secrets, code, or canonical results.

findings_verified.title: Global '*.parquet' and '*.csv' ignore rules conflict with tracked research data and will silently exclude future experiment outputs
findings_verified.detail: .gitignore lines 12-13 ignore all parquet/csv files repo-wide, yet imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/data/**.parquet files are already tracked (added despite the rule). This creates a split-brain: existing tracked artifacts keep updating normally, but any NEW parquet/csv produced by the active YH research line or experiments/ will be invisible to git status and silently never committed — a data-loss trap for a solo researcher whose findings depend on recorded run outputs. It also masks fixture files a test might legitimately need.
findings_verified.evidence: .gitignore:12-13 '*.parquet' / '*.csv'; git ls-files shows imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/data/C0p/agents_1000.parquet etc. tracked.
findings_verified.suggestion: Scope the rules to output directories (e.g. experiments/runs/**, data/raw/**) instead of global extensions, or add explicit negations for tracked data trees.
findings_verified.verify_note: Root /home/user/financial-abm-lab/.gitignore lines 12-13 contain global '*.parquet' and '*.csv'. `git ls-files` shows 2,535 tracked parquet/csv files (e.g. imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/data/C0p/agents_1000.parquet), so the split-brain exists. `git check-ignore -v` on hypothetical new paths (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH008_new/data/x.parquet, experiments/foo/out.csv, tests/fixtures/foo.csv) confirms all are matched by .gitignore:12-13; none of the nested imported/*/.gitignore files re-include them. Thus any new experiment output or CSV fixture would be invisible to git status and never committed unless force-added — exactly as the finding claims. Severity: it is a latent data-loss trap for the active research line, not an existing wrong result; hygiene/minor is fair, arguably borderline, so minor stands.

findings_verified.title: YH005_1 README observed-numbers table contradicts the committed phase1_metrics.json for the same seed
findings_verified.detail: README.md's 観測された数値 table (lines 55, 56, 59) contradicts the committed frozen artifact outputs/phase1_metrics.json on four auxiliary values — median wealth 41.0 vs 31.0, max wealth 998 vs 600.0, horizon p90/max 8/484 vs 7.0/476 — while every other row (α=2.54, K=10,419,681, corr +0.416, all hold ratios, order-size means, price range) matches the JSON exactly, showing the wealth/horizon rows were copied from an earlier run and never re-checked. Both files entered the repo in the same upstream commit, so the README was stale at commit time. Impact is limited: all paper-comparison values match the JSON, the discrepant cells have no expected/paper counterpart ("—"), and the JSON remains the authoritative reproducible record for seed=777. Fix: update the four README cells from phase1_metrics.json.
findings_verified.evidence:
README: 「median wealth | 41.0」「max wealth | 998」「p90 / max | 8 / 484」
phase1_metrics.json: "median_wealth": 31.0, "max_wealth": 600.0, "p90_horizon": 7.0, "max_horizon": 476
findings_verified.suggestion: Re-run phase1_mechanism_figures.py once and regenerate README table, figures, and JSON from the same run; note the code version/commit in the JSON.
findings_verified.verify_note: The discrepancy is real and verbatim. imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005_1/README.md lines 55-56 state "median wealth | 41.0" and "max wealth | 998", and line 59 states "p90 / max | 8 / 484", while the committed outputs/phase1_metrics.json (the script's own aggregated artifact, written by phase1_mechanism_figures.py:148-150) records "median_wealth": 31.0, "max_wealth": 600.0, "p90_horizon": 7.0, "max_horizon": 476 for the identical params block (seed=777, N=1000, T=50000). No alternative-definition escape exists: the README table's other rows (α=2.536→2.54, K=10,419,681, median/mean horizon 2/3.3, corr +0.4156→+0.416, all five hold ratios, order-size means 415.9/0.8/0.1, peak_large 12, price range [92.00, 107.13]) match the same JSON sections exactly, so the wealth/horizon rows cannot be a different metric — they are stale values from a different run/code state. I searched the repo for the README values (998, 41.0, 484) and found no other artifact producing them. Git history shows README and JSON entered in the same upstream commit (ef77e7f, the only commit touching either file), so the README was already inconsistent at commit time and was never reconciled — this is docs drift, not artifact regeneration lag. However, severity is miscalibrated: every scientifically load-bearing number in the README (α, K, corr, hold ratios, the values compared against the paper) agrees with the frozen JSON; the four discrepant cells are auxiliary rows whose "expected/paper value" column is "—", the authoritative JSON artifact is committed alongside and self-describing, and the figures were committed with the JSON in the same commit. No code produces wrong results. This is a genuine but low-impact documentation defect in the active YH00x line: minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: Committed null-test metrics JSON is stale: records the old 0.15 acceptance key with value false, contradicting code and README
findings_verified.detail: null_tests.py now checks ok_baseline = vol_acf_at_50 > 0.10 and writes the key "baseline_gt_0.10" (null_tests.py:87,133), and README §8.2 reports the null test as passing under the revised >0.10 criterion. But the committed artifact still contains "baseline_gt_0.15": false — i.e., the checked-in evidence file says the acceptance FAILED under the plan's original criterion and was never regenerated after the threshold was relaxed. Anyone auditing the outputs sees a failing acceptance record while README claims success. (README does disclose the 0.15→0.10 revision as a post-hoc re-calibration against the paper's fit, but the artifact should match the code that claims to produce it.)
findings_verified.evidence:
"acceptance": {
    "baseline_gt_0.15": false,
...
vs null_tests.py: ok_baseline = vol_acf_at_50["baseline"] > 0.10; "baseline_gt_0.10": ok_baseline
findings_verified.suggestion: Re-run `python run_simulation.py null_tests --seed 777` to regenerate the JSON so the stored acceptance keys/values match the current code.
findings_verified.verify_note: The committed artifact imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/outputs/null_tests_metrics.json line 18 contains "baseline_gt_0.15": false, while null_tests.py line 87 computes ok_baseline = vol_acf_at_50["baseline"] > 0.10 and line 133 writes key "baseline_gt_0.10". The stored baseline value 0.1193 fails the old 0.15 criterion (hence false) but passes the current 0.10 criterion, confirming the JSON predates the threshold revision and was never regenerated. The checked-in evidence file thus records a failing acceptance and a key the current code cannot produce, contradicting the README's claim of a passing null test. No mitigation found: the raw metrics are identical (only the threshold changed), so the scientific conclusion holds, but the artifact is stale. Severity minor is correct — a reproducibility/documentation inconsistency in an evidence file, not wrong results in canonical code.

findings_verified.title: 3-model comparison narrative contains two factual errors (SG τ=500 ACF sign; MG 'period 2^M=32' explanation) plus stale test count
findings_verified.detail: imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/README.md contains three documentation errors: (1) line 232 states SG vol ACF decays to +0.01 at τ=500, contradicting its own table (line 244: −0.02) and outputs/three_models_metrics.json (−0.0184); (2) line 246 explains MG's ACF=+1.00 at τ=200 as cycling at "multiples of 2^M=32", but 200 is not a multiple of 32 — re-running run_mg (seed=123, S=1, N=1000, M=5) shows the excess series is exactly periodic with period 8, with ACF≈1 at all multiples of 8 (incl. 40, 200) and negative ACF at 25/50/100; (3) line 178's "14 ケース全て緑" is stale — Phase 1 (YH005_1) added 4 test cases and test_parity.py now collects 18 parametrized cases, as YH005_1/README.md line 76 correctly states.
findings_verified.evidence:
L232: 「SG: 綺麗な slow decay (τ=1 で 0.25 → τ=500 で 0.01) ✓」 vs JSON "500": -0.018427
L246: 「周期 2^M=32 の倍数で…」 — measured ACF=1.0 at lags 40 and 200 (neither is a multiple of 32), true period divides 8
findings_verified.suggestion: Correct the τ=500 claim to 'decays into noise by τ≈500', re-derive the MG periodicity statement (cycle in the coupled (μ, tie-perturbation-free) dynamics, period divides 8 here), and bump the test count.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three sub-claims verified directly against the repo. (1) README line 232 claims SG vol ACF decays "τ=1 で 0.25 → τ=500 で 0.01", but the README's own table at line 244 shows −0.02 and outputs/three_models_metrics.json gives vol_acf["500"] = −0.018427 — the τ=500 value is negative, so "→ 0.01" is factually wrong. (2) Line 246 attributes MG's ACF=+1.00 at τ=200 to cycling "at multiples of the period 2^M=32"; 200 is not a multiple of 32. I re-ran the actual code (_mg_gcmg_baseline.run_mg, N=1000, M=5, S=1, seed=123): the post-transient excess series is exactly periodic with period 8 (verified by array equality on the tail), and |excess| ACF ≈ 1.0 at lags 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 64, 96, 200 (all multiples of 8) but is negative at 25, 50, 100. The stated mechanism is wrong; the true limit-cycle length is 8, and 200 shows ACF≈1 only because 200 = 25×8. The artifact conclusion (MG degenerate/periodic) still stands, as the auditor noted. (3) Line 178 says "14 ケース全て緑", but YH005_1/README.md line 76 explicitly states "既存 14 + 新規 4 = 18 parity/invariant テスト", and tests/test_parity.py now contains 10 test functions whose parametrizations (5+3+1+1+1+1+1+1+2+2) total 18 cases — the count is stale. Severity minor is correctly calibrated: pure documentation drift in an imported experiment README, no code or results affected.

findings_verified.title: Plan-mandated _assert_sign_convention() smoke test was never implemented; sign conventions are asserted only in a comment
findings_verified.detail: YH005_PLAN.md §9-7 item 1 requires, '必須、wiring 前', writing `_assert_sign_convention()` inside _mg_gcmg_baseline.py to programmatically verify that YH003's excess=2·attendance−N and YH004's excess=actions.sum() share SG's 'buy-dominant ⇒ D>0' sign convention (with automatic sign flip in reconstruct_price if not). The shipped file contains only a docstring claim ('符号規約 (Step 2-9-7 確認済み)') and no check anywhere in the module or tests. A silent sign flip in either upstream model (e.g. if YH003's attendance definition changed) would invert the MG/GCMG virtual price series in the flagship 3-model comparison figure without any test failing.
findings_verified.evidence: docstring only: 「符号規約 (Step 2-9-7 確認済み): YH003: … "A 側優勢で excess > 0" を "buy 優勢 (SG の D > 0)" と識別して採用 (一致)」 — no _assert_sign_convention function exists in the file or tests/
findings_verified.suggestion: Add the planned smoke test (drive both models with a biased strategy table or check excess vs attendance identity) or downgrade the plan text to record the manual check.
findings_verified.verify_note: Grep across the entire repository shows the only occurrence of `_assert_sign_convention` is in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/YH005_PLAN.md:698, which marks it 必須 (mandatory, before wiring) and specifies it must live in _mg_gcmg_baseline.py with an automatic sign flip in reconstruct_price() if needed. The shipped _mg_gcmg_baseline.py contains only a docstring claim (lines 8–13, 「符号規約 (Step 2-9-7 確認済み)」) and no programmatic check; reconstruct_price() (line ~41) unconditionally does p0 + cumsum(excess/N). YH005/tests/ contains only test_parity.py with no sign-convention assertion, and the workspace tests/ suite has none either. So the plan-mandated smoke test was replaced by a comment: a future sign change in YH003's attendance convention or YH004's action encoding would silently invert the MG/GCMG virtual price series in compare_three_models.py with no test failure. Severity minor is calibrated: the current signs were apparently verified manually (docstring) and code presently behaves correctly; the defect is a missing guard against future regressions in an active but experiment-level research line, not a present wrong result.

findings_verified.title: YH001 README conflates powerlaw's PDF exponent with the CCDF tail exponent; the 'discrepancy vs theory' it explains away is actually near-perfect agreement
findings_verified.detail: The interpretation section states the theoretical prediction as '累積分布のテール指数は α = μ = 1.5' and then says the measured α ≈ 2.6 (c=0.99–1.0, from powerlaw.Fit) is 'larger than theory' due to finite-size effects. But powerlaw.Fit(...).power_law.alpha is the DENSITY exponent (P(x) ~ x^{-α}), not the CCDF exponent: for Cont-Bouchaud at criticality the return density follows P(Δx) ~ |Δx|^{-(1+μ)} with μ=3/2, i.e. the correct theoretical value for the quantity being measured is α = 5/2. The measured 2.57 (c=1.0) and 2.65 (c=0.99) therefore match theory almost exactly, and the finite-size-effect paragraph explains a discrepancy that does not exist. The simulation and estimator are fine; only the exponent-convention bookkeeping in the write-up is wrong (the same convention question recurs in tail_analysis.py's 'α=3 finite variance boundary' line, which is correct under the PDF convention).
findings_verified.evidence: 「累積分布のテール指数は α = μ = 1.5 が理論的予測となる。ここで得た α ≈ 2.6 (c=0.99-1.0) は理論値より大きいが、これは有限サイズ効果…」 — powerlaw's alpha is the PDF exponent; theory predicts 1+μ = 2.5
findings_verified.suggestion: Rewrite the paragraph: theory predicts density exponent 1+μ=5/2 (CCDF exponent μ=3/2); observed 2.57–2.65 confirms it. Remove the finite-size apology.
findings_verified.verify_note: README (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH001/README.md:62-67) itself states the return DENSITY is P(Δx) ~ |Δx|^{-(1+μ)}, μ=3/2, but then quotes the theoretical prediction as the CCDF exponent α = μ = 1.5 and compares it against the measured α ≈ 2.6. The measured value is fit.power_law.alpha from powerlaw.Fit (tail_analysis.py:23-25 and run_simulation.py:123-124), which is the PDF exponent p(x) ∝ x^{-α}. The like-for-like theoretical value is therefore 1+μ = 2.5, and the measured 2.57 (c=1.0) / 2.65 (c=0.99) match it closely. The finite-size-effect explanation addresses a discrepancy that only exists because of the exponent-convention mismatch. Simulation and estimator code are correct; the α=3 finite-variance line in the plots is also consistent with the PDF convention. Defect is confined to the write-up's interpretation section in an imported experiment README, so minor is the right severity.

findings_verified.title: Diagnostic fill counters mix the warmup session (execution disabled, all opens structurally zero-fill) into headline rates
findings_verified.detail: The _meta diagnostic counters (num_zero_opens, num_partial_opens/closes, num_cancels_sent, close_* counters) at yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py:221-228 are lifetime sums including the warmup session, where withOrderExecution=False (configs/_base.py:58) makes every SG open a guaranteed zero-fill counted at speculation_agent.py:323. Unlike prices/action_log/submit_log/round_trips, which the adapter filters by warmup_steps, these counters are unfiltered, biasing the zero-fill/partial-fill rates printed by run_experiment.py:135 and any liquidity-tuning conclusions (e.g. README.md:183's ≈30% zero-fill claim) upward. Impact is limited to diagnostics — headline parity arrays are correctly filtered.
findings_verified.evidence: yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py:221-228: '"num_zero_opens": int(sum(a.num_zero_opens for a in sg_agents)), ... "close_cancelled": int(sum(a.close_cancelled for a in sg_agents))' — raw lifetime counters; counters increment in _reconcile (speculation_agent.py:323-324) during warmup too because agents submit opens while execution is off.
findings_verified.suggestion: Record counters as (t, event) tuples like action_log, or maintain separate warmup/main counters, and recompute the zero-fill rate on main-session events only.
findings_verified.verify_note: configs/_base.py:53-61 sets session 0 (warmup) to withOrderPlacement=True / withOrderExecution=False, and C2/C3 include SGAgents for all sessions. In speculation_agent.py:251-277 SG agents submit open MARKET_ORDERs during warmup whenever the opposite book side is non-empty (FCN limit orders populate it since placement is on); with execution disabled these can never fill, so _reconcile (speculation_agent.py:321-328) increments num_zero_opens for every warmup open. The adapter's _meta block (yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py:217-228) sums these agent counters over the full lifetime with no warmup filtering — in contrast to the same function's t_rel/warmup_steps filtering of prices, action_log, submit_log, and round_trips (lines 93, 106, 123, 143-148). run_experiment.py:135 prints zero_open from this _meta and README.md:183 quotes a "zero-fill open 率 ≈ 30 %" liquidity diagnostic, so the contamination reaches user-facing numbers. Minor caveat: I could not verify read-only that the README's 30% was computed from these lifetime counters specifically, but the code defect (warmup contamination of the diagnostic counters) stands regardless.

findings_verified.title: SPEC wealth semantics (§3.2(d)/§4.5) and run sizes contradict the implemented 2-account design and actual runs
findings_verified.detail: SPEC §3.2(d) specifies self.cash_amount as the SG cash (overwritten in setup per §4.5, reset on substitute, wealth = cash + asset*mid). The code deliberately implements the opposite: a separate sg_wealth cognitive account, with cash_amount explicitly left at the PAMS-drawn value (speculation_agent.py:31-39 'Wealth 2-account 設計 (Phase 1 固有決定)', :164-166 comment). Additional drift: SPEC's conditions table says C2/C3 use N=500 SG with warmup 500 / main 5000 and calibration over 5000 steps, while the executed runs (README reproduction block and run_experiment defaults) use N=100, warmup 200 / main 1500, calibration 1500; SPEC Phase-2 adapter keys log_returns and wealth_snapshots are absent from the adapter; SPEC §3.2(e) says C_ticks = 3×median(|Δmid|) but calibrate_c_ticks.py:61-65 uses the median of NONZERO |Δmid| (falling back to the plain median).
findings_verified.evidence: SPEC.md:90-94: '`self.cash_amount` (PAMS 標準 attr) を SG の cash として扱う ... substitute 時: cash_amount を int(B + prng.random() * 100) に reset' and SPEC.md:206-207 'self.cash_amount = w  # PAMS 標準 attr を上書き' vs speculation_agent.py:164-166 'self.sg_wealth = w; # self.cash_amount ... super().setup が draw した値のまま残す'; calibrate_c_ticks.py:61 'abs_diffs_nz = abs_diffs[abs_diffs > 0]'.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add a 'deviations from SPEC' section (or amend §3.2(d)/§4.5) recording the 2-account decision, the nonzero-median calibration rule, and the actually-used N/T; SPEC is cited as design ground truth by the monorepo docs.
findings_verified.verify_note: All four sub-claims verified against the code. (1) SPEC.md §3.2(d) lines 90-94 and §4.5 lines 199-211 specify overwriting self.cash_amount as the SG cash and wealth = cash_amount + asset*mid; speculation_agent.py lines 31-39 document a deliberate 2-account design and lines 158-166 set self.sg_wealth = w while explicitly leaving cash_amount at the PAMS-drawn value ('super().setup が draw した値のまま残す'). (2) SPEC's conditions table (lines 36-37) says C2/C3 use N=500 SG with warmup 500 / main 5000 and §3.2(e) calibration over 5000 steps; run_experiment.py defaults are warmup_steps=200, main_steps=1500, num_sg=100 (lines 40-43, 95-96) and calibrate_c_ticks.py uses warmup 200 / main 1500 (lines 32-33). (3) yh006_to_yh005_adapter.py returns prices, cognitive_prices, round_trips, etc., but has no log_returns or wealth_snapshots keys promised by SPEC Phase 2 (lines 243-247). (4) SPEC line 73 says C_ticks = 3×median(|Δmid|) but calibrate_c_ticks.py:61-65 computes 3×median of NONZERO |Δmid| with fallback to the plain median. The drift is real, but the code is the intentional design (code comments label it a Phase 1 decision) and no computation is wrong; it can mislead a future reader of SPEC. Severity minor (docs-drift) is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: max_normal_orders=500 default silently drops agents per step once agent count exceeds it (SPEC's N=500 + 30 FCN config would be truncated)
findings_verified.detail: Latent reproducibility trap, not a defect in executed results: run_experiment.py's --max-normal-orders default of 500 overrides the config modules' 2000 default and, combined with SPEC's N=500 SG + 30 FCN (530 agents), causes pams SequentialRunner to silently skip up to ~30 random agents per step (skipped agents miss cancel/reconcile/virtual-G updates inside submit_orders). All actually-executed YH006 runs use num_sg=100 (130 agents) and are unaffected. Severity minor is correct.
findings_verified.evidence: pams/runners/sequential.py:407-425: 'agents = self._prng.sample(agents, len(agents)); n_orders = 0; for agent in agents: if n_orders >= session.max_normal_orders: break ... n_orders += 1' vs run_experiment.py:44 'max_normal_orders: int = 500' and SPEC.md:36-37 (C2/C3: N=500).
findings_verified.suggestion: In run_condition, assert max_normal_orders >= num_fcn + num_sg (or set it to that sum automatically) and document the pams truncation semantics.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against pams 0.2.2 source (pams/runners/sequential.py, _collect_orders_from_normal_agents): agents are prng-shuffled and the loop breaks once n_orders >= session.max_normal_orders, where n_orders counts agents returning non-empty orders; skipped agents never have submit_orders called, so SG agents miss cancel/reconcile/_update_virtual. run_experiment.py line 44/99 defaults max_normal_orders=500 and explicitly passes it to c2_config/c3_config, overriding those configs' own safer defaults (configs/c2.py and c3.py default max_normal_orders=2000 with num_sg_agents=500). SPEC.md's condition table specifies N=500 SG for C2/C3, so a SPEC-faithful run (--num-sg 500, 530 agents) with the script default silently truncates up to ~30 randomly-sampled agents per step with no warning, making results agent-sampling-dependent. Executed runs (README: --num-sg 100, 130 agents) are unaffected, so this is latent, not affecting committed results.

findings_verified.title: advance_to swallows all exceptions and substitutes dp=0.0, silently flattening the shared cognitive history
findings_verified.detail: advance_to (history_broadcast.py:71-76) catches all exceptions from market.get_market_price — including pams' AssertionError raised for None/missing prices — and substitutes dp=0.0 with no logging, then advances last_t, so the flattened step is never recomputed. In the current pams version and experiment wiring the except path appears unreachable (prices for t-1/t-2 are always populated and tt>=2 guards negative indices), so no existing YH006 results are affected; the risk is latent silent corruption of the shared h(t)/mu/P state if the pams API or data invariants ever change. Recommended fix: narrow or remove the except, or at minimum log and count occurrences.
findings_verified.evidence: history_broadcast.py:70-77: 'try: p_prev = market.get_market_price(tt - 1); p_pprev = market.get_market_price(tt - 2); dp = float(p_prev) - float(p_pprev) except Exception: dp = 0.0'.
findings_verified.suggestion: Catch nothing (or only the specific expected exception for t<2, which is already guarded by 'if tt >= 2'), and count any fallback in a visible diagnostic (e.g. self.num_price_read_failures surfaced via _meta).
findings_verified.verify_note: The cited code is exactly as described: imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/history_broadcast.py lines 71-76 wrap the mid-price delta computation in a bare `except Exception: dp = 0.0` with no logging, counter, or re-raise. Because SharedHistoryState is shared across all SG agents (attached to the simulator in get_or_init) and advance_to also sets self.last_t inside the same loop, a swallowed exception is not retried later — the step is permanently recorded as h=0 in mu, P, and h_series. I checked the pams library actually installed (pams/market.py, get_market_price -> _extract_data_by_time): it raises AssertionError when the stored price is None or when a future time is referenced, and AssertionError is a subclass of Exception, so genuine data problems would be silently converted to h=0 rather than surfacing. Mitigation check: in a normal pams run, _market_prices[tt-1] and _market_prices[tt-2] for tt <= current time are always populated (setup writes t=0, the runner updates each step), and the tt >= 2 guard prevents negative indices — so the except path is almost certainly never taken in the experiment as configured, and there is no evidence of h(t) corruption in actual YH006 outputs. That prevents escalation, but it does not refute the finding: the defect is real (silent masking of any failure in a scientific state variable, with idempotent last_t advancement making it unrecoverable), it is just latent. No test guards it — nothing in the repo asserts the except branch is unreachable or checks h_series against prices. Severity minor is correctly calibrated: genuine defensive-coding defect in the user's own active research line (YH006), but currently unreachable under the installed pams behavior, so it does not presently produce wrong results.

findings_verified.title: OVERVIEW.md describes a stale project state: claims ablations A1/A3 are not yet run while README/data contain completed S4–S5.8 results
findings_verified.detail: OVERVIEW.md ('まだわからないこと': 'ablation実験…はまだ実施していない'; '次にやること（保留中）: ablation A1 … ablation A3') reflects the post-S3 state. The repository actually contains completed S4 calibration (q_const=3), S5 A1 ablation with 100 trials per condition (data/C2_A1, data/C3_A1, tab_S5_shrinkage.csv, L2 0/5 fail), S5.5–S5.8 robustness/survival stages, and S6 tau_max calibration (logs/S6_tau_max_calibration.json). A reader (or downstream consolidation tooling in the monorepo) trusting OVERVIEW gets the pre-ablation narrative, including 'F1 was reproduced' without the later L2-fail revision of hypothesis A. The lineage diagram also marks the current position as 'ablation は次'.
findings_verified.evidence: OVERVIEW: '…ablation実験…はまだ実施していない。' and '## 次にやること（保留中） … ablation A1（因果検証・核心）' — vs README sections 'Stage S5 — A1 ablation … L2 pass 件数: 0 / 5' and existing data/C3_A1, logs/S6_tau_max_calibration.json
findings_verified.suggestion: Update OVERVIEW.md through S5.8 (A1 ran and L2 failed; hypothesis A revised; freeze confirmed stationary in S5.8; S6 A3 pending), since it is the document non-specialists will read first.
findings_verified.verify_note: The drift is real. OVERVIEW.md line 73 states 'しかしこれを確かめる「ablation実験」...はまだ実施していない' and lines 79-98 list ablation A1/A3 under '次にやること（保留中）' with the lineage marker 'YH006_1 ← 今ここ：F1 の再現確認 + 統計的確定（ablation は次）'. But the same experiment directory contains completed A1 ablation results: data/C2_A1 and data/C3_A1 exist, README.md line 166 has 'Stage S5 — A1 ablation (C2_A1 / C3_A1) + KPI L2 判定' with 'L2 pass 件数: 0 / 5' (line 212), plus S5.5-S5.8 robustness/survival stages, S4 q_const calibration (logs/S4_q_const_calibration.json) and logs/S6_tau_max_calibration.json. Critically, the README documents that the A1 ablation refuted the simple hypothesis A ('仮説 A 単純版 (反証)') and introduced a revised hypothesis, so OVERVIEW's pre-ablation narrative is not merely incomplete but conveys a superseded causal conclusion. No caller or test mitigates this; it is a documentation file in the user's own active research lineage (YH006_1). Severity 'minor' (docs-drift, no code/results affected) is correctly calibrated — though within the highest-priority research line, the stale narrative could mislead consolidation/summary work, it corrupts no results.

findings_verified.title: agent_lifetime_samples_to_df computes sample_idx with an O(n²) scan over all accumulated rows
findings_verified.detail: For every appended lifetime sample, sample_idx is computed as len([r for r in rows if r["agent_id"] == aid]) — a full scan of every row appended so far across all agents (also repeated at line 213 for the final censored segment). Aggregate trials have ~13k lifetime samples (T=50000, median lifetime ≈390, N=100), so this is ~10^8 dict comparisons per trial × 200+ trials, executed inside the parallel workers. A per-agent counter (enumerate over subs) is equivalent and O(n). It completed in practice, but it inflates the dominant per-trial cost for the cheap aggregate world and will bite harder for any future longer-T or larger-N run (e.g., a T=50000 LOB extension).
findings_verified.evidence: "sample_idx": len([r for r in rows if r["agent_id"] == aid]),  — inside the per-sample loop, rows spans ALL agents
findings_verified.suggestion: Replace with for sample_idx, t_sub in enumerate(subs) and use len(subs) for the final censored segment.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified at imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/adapter.py lines 202 and 213: `"sample_idx": len([r for r in rows if r["agent_id"] == aid])` runs inside the per-sample loop, and `rows` accumulates samples across ALL agents (the outer loop at line 196 iterates aid over range(N_total), appending to a single shared list). Each append therefore scans every previously appended row, giving O(n²) total work; since agents are processed sequentially and rows from other agents can never match aid, the scan is pure waste and a per-agent counter (e.g., enumerate over subs, with the final censored row getting len(subs)) is exactly equivalent. Callers confirmed: run_experiment.py lines 154 and 327 invoke it once per trial. Output values are correct — this is purely a performance defect in code that has already completed its runs, so severity minor (performance category) is correctly calibrated, not major/critical.

findings_verified.title: lifetime_median/p90 in ensemble_summary treat censored samples as observed deaths (biased under 73–91% LOB censoring); biased values persist in tables
findings_verified.detail: compute_trial_metrics pools censored and uncensored lifetime samples ('censored sample も含めて全 sample で計算') and takes median/p90.
