For C2 (91% censoring) and C3 (73%) these are dominated by the T=1500 window and are attributes of the window, not of agents; for agg they are mildly downward-biased. The team recognized this later (S3 Yuito mandate switched primary indicators to p25/conditional-median/censoring-rate, and S5.7 did proper KM), but the biased columns remain 'lifetime_median'/'lifetime_p90' in ensemble_summary.parquet and in the S2/S3 README CI tables labelled only as 補助, and the S5.6 verdict inputs (lifetime_p25 in collect_trial_stats, and tau_max L_50 candidates a/d) still mix censored samples as events. The only guard is a heuristic flag (median > T/2). Note q_const calibration is unaffected (q, not lifetime), and tau_max primary (p25 of a 73%-censored pool) is deliberately conservative-short, which is the safe direction for a cap.
findings_verified.evidence: # censored sample も含めて全 sample で計算 (Phase 2 主目的では KM 解析しない)\nlifetimes = lifetime_samples_df["lifetime"].to_numpy(...); lifetime_median = float(np.median(lifetimes))
findings_verified.suggestion: Rename the columns (e.g., lifetime_median_incl_censored) or replace with KM-median now that km_from_counts exists, so future consumers of ensemble_summary.parquet cannot mistake them for survival medians.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves exactly as claimed. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/aggregate_ensemble.py lines 123-129, compute_trial_metrics pools all lifetime samples ("censored sample も含めて全 sample で計算") and computes np.median / np.percentile(...,90) over the mixed pool; the only mitigation is the heuristic flag lifetime_median_censoring_flag (median > T/2), not a statistical correction, so lifetime_median/lifetime_p90 in ensemble_summary.parquet are censoring-biased under the stated 73-91% censoring. The claim about S5.6 inputs is also verified: aggregate_mmfcn_sensitivity.py lines 157-177 computes lifetime_p25 = np.percentile(lifetimes, 25) over the full pool including censored samples (the censored mask is used only for censoring_rate and conditional_median, not to exclude censored rows from p25). No downstream code corrects these columns; the team's later switch to p25/conditional-median/censoring-rate and KM in S5.7 mitigates the research conclusions but does not fix the persisted biased columns. However, the pooling is a documented, deliberate design choice (explicit code comment, columns labelled auxiliary/補助, and p25-of-censored-pool is conservative-short which is the safe direction for the tau_max cap), and q_const calibration is unaffected. This is a genuine but low-impact statistical bias, so severity minor is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: No unit tests for the YH006_1 statistical core; RNG-replay w_init reconstruction is verified only for the uniform branch and is drift-coupled to aggregate_sim internals
findings_verified.detail: The code/ directory ships no tests (only Phase-1 YH006/tests exists). Untested load-bearing logic includes: km_from_counts/greenwood_band/bootstrap_km_ci (bespoke KM instead of lifelines), the three copies of the re-censor convention, adapter.round_trips_to_df's w_open/w_close cumulative reconstruction across substitute events, and _replay_w_init_aggregate, which re-executes the exact RNG consumption order of aggregate_sim.simulate_aggregate (strategies → init_u100 → init_active → mu0 → pareto uniforms). Its own docstring says bit-parity was verified only for uniform mode ('test_aggregate_parity で uniform mode の bit-一致が verified 済'); I read aggregate_sim.py:77-92 and confirmed the pareto branch order currently matches, but any future edit to aggregate_sim's init sequence silently corrupts every w_init-derived metric (wealth_persistence_rho, corr_w_init_h, corr_winit_wt_T1..T10) with no assertion to catch it. The same replay pattern is duplicated in reanalyze_phase1.reconstruct_w_init_aggregate.
findings_verified.evidence: """aggregate_sim と同じ RNG 消費順を replicate して w_init を抽出 ... test_aggregate_parity で uniform mode の bit-一致が verified 済。""" — pareto branch has no parity test
findings_verified.suggestion: Add a small pytest: (a) toy KM vs lifelines or hand values; (b) for both wealth modes, assert _replay_w_init_aggregate(seed) equals a w_init snapshot exported from simulate_aggregate via the existing snapshot_callback at t=0; (c) round-trip w_close continuity (w_close[k]==w_open[k+1] absent substitutes).
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified directly: (1) imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/ contains no tests (only YH006/tests exists). (2) _replay_w_init_aggregate (run_experiment.py:84-105) replicates simulate_aggregate's init RNG order (YH006/aggregate_sim.py:80-92 — strategies, init_u100, init_active, mu0, then pareto uniforms) and the two currently match, so no present bug. (3) test_aggregate_parity.py covers only uniform-mode bit-parity with YH005, pareto determinism, and uniform≠pareto sanity; no test asserts the pareto-branch replay equals the wealth actually used by the sim, and simulate_aggregate does not expose w_init, so the replay is the sole source of w_init for pareto conditions. (4) The replay is duplicated in reanalyze_phase1.reconstruct_w_init_aggregate. Any future edit to aggregate_sim's init sequence would silently corrupt all w_init-derived metrics with no assertion to catch it. No guard or caller mitigates this. Severity 'minor / testing' is correctly calibrated: code is correct today; this is a coverage/fragility gap, not a current wrong-results defect.

findings_verified.title: S5.6 'semantic_identical' baseline verdict is based only on sum(delta_g) — a weak equivalence check
findings_verified.detail: check_baseline_consistency falls back from sha256 to a 'semantic' comparison that checks only row count, column names, and |sum(delta_g_new) − sum(delta_g_ref)| < 1e-6, then declares 'semantic_identical'. Sums are permutation-invariant and can coincide under compensating differences (e.g., two RTs swapped between agents, or +x/−x perturbations), so a genuine behavioral divergence in the mmfcn_order_volume=None path could be labelled identical. Contrast with the stronger element-wise np.array_equal over 8 columns used by every determinism guard (lob_ensemble.py:124-128, ablation_ensemble.py:162-166) — the correct tool already exists in the codebase. Low practical impact because the sha256 path reportedly passed (README: 'baseline bit-一致 PASS'), but the fallback would be the active check exactly when something subtle changed.
findings_verified.evidence: dg_diff = float(np.abs(new_df["delta_g"].sum() - ref_df["delta_g"].sum()))\nif dg_diff < 1e-6: return {"verdict": "semantic_identical", ...}
findings_verified.suggestion: Reuse the guard-style comparison: sort both frames by (agent_id, t_open) and np.array_equal on the 8 rt columns before claiming semantic identity.
findings_verified.verify_note: aggregate_mmfcn_sensitivity.py lines 101-116 behave exactly as reported: when sha256 differs, check_baseline_consistency verifies only row count, sorted column names, and |sum(delta_g_new) - sum(delta_g_ref)| < 1e-6 before returning verdict "semantic_identical". Sum equality is permutation-invariant and admits compensating errors, so a real behavioral divergence in the mmfcn_order_volume=None baseline could be reported as identical. The caller (line 362) writes the verdict into the aggregation report unguarded; no test or other code mitigates it. Element-wise np.array_equal checks used in lob_ensemble.py/ablation_ensemble.py confirm a stronger check exists in the codebase. Impact is low because the fallback is dormant (sha256 path passed per logs/README) and it is a diagnostic label in one experiment's aggregation script, not canonical model code — severity minor is calibrated correctly.

findings_verified.title: REPORT_v3 attributes the behavioral-framing sign flip to a 'reason-generation artifact', contradicting the measurement's own no-generation design
findings_verified.detail: REPORT_v3.md:20 (generated by run_p0_5.py:94, also persisted in gate_v3.json) attributes the behavioral-framing loss-context ATH sign flip (-0.0704) to a "reason-generation artifact", but that number was measured by run_p0.py:78-79 via build_behavioral_probe (render.py:129-144), a logit-only read at the is_buy slot with order_price pre-filled — explicitly "no free generation" per its own docstring, which says it isolates the effect of the reason-REQUESTING answer format, not reason generation. The flip is therefore an answer-format/framing effect mislabeled as a generation artifact; the same format amplifies the disposition proxy 15x (+0.2599 vs +0.0172), confirming the format dominates effect sizes. Correction to the original finding: the dismissal is NOT load-bearing for the PASS decision. The P0.5 decision rule (run_p0_5.py:87-108, pre-registered in the module docstring as Yuito's judgement) counts sign flips only among the 3 clean-probe wordings; the out-of-family behavioral flip was intentionally out of scope, having already been escalated in REPORT_v2's "Robustness caveat (Stage 2 risk #1)" which P0.5 was designed to resolve. Impact is limited to a misleading mechanism attribution in archived report prose that could bias future interpretation of the framing sensitivity.
findings_verified.evidence: REPORT_v3.md: 'the behavioral-framing inversion is a reason-generation artifact' vs finalize_faithfulness.py:80-81: 'P(sell) read at is_buy slot of the FCLAgent reason-requesting answer format (order_price pre-filled). Directly comparable to clean-probe on same states.'
findings_verified.suggestion: Reword the verdict to 'answer-format (framing) effect of unknown mechanism', and add the framing flip to the Stage-2 risk register alongside the wording caveat rather than resolving it by assertion.
findings_verified.verify_note: The core claim is verified. The -0.0704 loss-context behavioral-framing ATH originates from run_p0.py:78-79, which uses build_behavioral_probe — a pure logit read at the is_buy slot with order_price pre-filled in the assistant prefix (render.py:129-144), whose own docstring says "no free generation" and that it "isolates the effect of the *reason-requesting answer format*" and "does not capture free CoT". No reason text is generated before the decision token, so the sign flip cannot be a reason-GENERATION artifact; it is an answer-format/framing effect. Nevertheless run_p0_5.py:94 hardcodes the verdict string "the behavioral-framing inversion is a reason-generation artifact", which lands verbatim in REPORT_v3.md:20 and gate_v3.json. The 15x disposition amplification under the same format (+0.2599 vs +0.0172) confirms the answer format dominates effect sizes, so the mislabel is substantively misleading. HOWEVER, the finding's "load-bearing" sub-claim is refuted: run_p0_5.py:87-108 counts sign flips only WITHIN the 3 clean-probe wordings, per the pre-registered judgement in the module docstring ("Judgement (Yuito): all 3 positive -> PASS; >=2 null or any sign-flip -> WORDING_ARTIFACT_DETECTED"). The out-of-family behavioral flip was never an input to that rule, was already prominently escalated in P0's REPORT_v2 "Robustness caveat (Stage 2 risk #1)" (explicitly asking Yuito to rule PASS-with-caveat vs gate-failing), and P0.5 is the follow-up that caveat demanded. The PASS decision is computed mechanically from n_pos==3 before the verdict prose; fixing the prose would not change the routing. So the defect is a genuine but non-decision-altering mechanism mislabel in an archived report/gate JSON: docs-drift, severity minor rather than major.

findings_verified.title: Code-semantics doc misstates the strategy-switch tie-break as random; the code keeps the incumbent — the exact semantics of the known Java/Python divergence
findings_verified.detail: §1.6 says: 'real close のたびに active_idx ← argmax G (tie はランダム、speculation_agent.py:362-369)'. The cited code does the opposite of a random tie-break for the common case: it keeps the current strategy whenever it is in the argmax set, and randomizes only among tied-best strategies when the incumbent is not among them (matching the YH005 design-hole 4.1 convention recorded in findings.md:89). This is precisely the tie-break semantics where all Python SG implementations diverge from the dissertation Java reference (ties ~3.5% of close events — known finding, not re-reported here). A document that promises file:line-level fidelity ('全主張に file:line を張る') and is meant to feed proposal/oral prep should not blur this contract, because future parity or ablation work reading this doc would implement the wrong rule.
findings_verified.evidence: speculation_agent.py:363-366: best = np.flatnonzero(self.G == g_max).tolist(); if self.active_idx not in best: new_idx = best[self.prng.randrange(len(best))] — vs doc: 'active_idx ← argmax G (tie はランダム)'
findings_verified.suggestion: Correct §1.6 to 'incumbent keeps its seat if tied-best; otherwise uniform among argmax', and cross-reference the Java-reference tie-break divergence so the distinction stays visible.
findings_verified.verify_note: The doc at imported/speculation-game-info/docs/refs_sg_lob_code_semantics.md §1.6 states "real close のたびに active_idx ← argmax G (tie はランダム、speculation_agent.py:362-369)". The cited code (imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006/speculation_agent.py:362-369) computes best = np.flatnonzero(self.G == g_max) and only switches — randomizing among tied-best — when `self.active_idx not in best`. Thus when the incumbent is in the argmax set (the common tie case) it deterministically keeps its seat; randomization happens only when the incumbent is strictly beaten by a tie among others. The doc's blanket "tie はランダム" therefore misstates the exact tie-break semantics that constitute the known Java/Python divergence, in a document that explicitly promises file:line fidelity and is intended to feed proposal/oral prep and future parity/ablation work. No correction or caveat appears elsewhere in the section. Severity minor is appropriate: it is documentation drift, not executable canonical code, but genuinely misleading on the one contract point known to matter.

findings_verified.title: config.yaml describes the pre-fix ATH semantics and carries dead probe/state keys that the code never reads
findings_verified.detail: Config comments at imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH008/src/config.yaml:79-84 describe pre-fix ATH semantics (nearness = price/ATH; no_drop nearness 1.0; dropped_near in [0.95,0.99]) that post-fix states.py:_ath_for no longer implements: no_drop_nearness is never read, ATH = max(price, purchase), so loss-context no_drop nearness is ~[0.87,0.98] and dropped_near current-price nearness ~[0.83,0.97]. These stale comments are replicated into every per-run config_snapshot.yaml/provenance.json. Additionally dead config keys: probe.true_group_seed/false_group_seed/extra_candidates (freeze_groups.py hardcodes its own lists, adding TRUE/FALSE all-caps variants absent from config), gain_loss.deadband_gain_abs (unreachable: min |unrealized_gain| = 5 > 1), and unused center/tick keys. Impact is limited because states.py's docstring documents the actual semantics and each ath_pair record stores measured nearness values in its meta, but a reader interpreting the snapshot alone would misdescribe the loss-context ATH contrast.
findings_verified.evidence: config.yaml:81: 'no_drop_nearness: 1.0  # ATH == current price (no drop)' vs states.py:62: 'if cond == "no_drop": return base, price / base' where base = max(price, purchase); freeze_groups.py:15: TRUE_SPELLINGS = [..., "TRUE", " TRUE"] vs config true_group_seed (4 variants, unread)
findings_verified.suggestion: Update the ath comments to the post-fix reference-high semantics (per-context actual nearness ranges), delete or wire the dead keys, and have freeze_groups read its spelling lists from config so the snapshot is truthful.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three sub-claims verified against the code. (1) states.py _ath_for (lines 54-70) never reads states.ath.no_drop_nearness; it sets base = max(price, purchase) and returns nearness price/base for no_drop. With loss_mult in [1.02,1.15], loss-context no_drop nearness is price/purchase in ~[0.87,0.98], not the documented 1.0 ("ATH == current price"); dropped_near current-price nearness = near*(price/purchase) in ~[0.83,0.97], outside the documented [0.95,0.99]. The stale comments are propagated into every archived config_snapshot.yaml and provenance.json under outputs/. (2) Grep confirms probe.true_group_seed / false_group_seed / extra_candidates appear only in config and snapshots; freeze_groups.py:15-18 hardcodes its own spelling lists including "TRUE"/" TRUE"/"FALSE"/" FALSE" variants not in config. (3) deadband_gain_abs=1.0 is dead: min |unrealized_gain| = 1 * 0.02 * 250 = 5 > 1, so the filter at states.py:86 never fires; center/tick keys are likewise unread. Partial mitigation exists — states.py's docstring documents the post-fix no_drop semantics, and each ath_pair record's meta stores the actual nearness_no_drop/nearness_dropped values (states.py:131-134), so raw data self-describes — which supports keeping severity at minor (docs-drift in an imported experiment archive; no computation is wrong).

findings_verified.title: YH008 scripts hardcode machine-local absolute paths and mutate earlier runs' output directories in place
findings_verified.detail: YH008 archived scripts are coupled to the GPU staging box: finalize_faithfulness.py:21 and run_p0_5.py:29 hardcode /root/yh008_work/outputs/20260529-092454_7de0bb4, config.yaml pins outputs_root=/root/yh008_work/outputs and HF cache /workspace/.hf, and git provenance shells 'cd /workspace/speculation-game-info' — none parameterized on relocation, so the archived copy cannot re-run (partially documented in README_STAGING.md). finalize_faithfulness.py writes gate.json and SUMMARY.json into the v1 run directory, so that archived run is a composite of run_all.py (stages 0–1) and a later finalize execution; however, evidence (finalize-schema gate/SUMMARY, missing behavioral.json, free-gen checkpoint files, docstring noting fp32 free-gen was prohibitively slow) indicates run_all never completed its own gate for this run, so no earlier gate was overwritten — finalize completed the run. Separately, run_p0.py:32-33 sh() returns the truthy "<err ...>" string, making the `or "nogit"` fallback at line 46 dead; a failed git call would embed "<err Command ...>" into run_id and output paths (run_p0_5's sh handles this correctly).
findings_verified.evidence: finalize_faithfulness.py:21: RUN = "/root/yh008_work/outputs/20260529-092454_7de0bb4"; :173-175 rewrites os.path.join(RUN, "stage0_5_gate", "gate.json"); run_p0.py:32-33: 'except Exception as e: return f"<err {e}>"' then :46 'or "nogit"' (dead fallback)
findings_verified.suggestion: Parameterize paths via config/CLI on the relocated copy, write finalize outputs under a new run_id (or versioned filenames like gate_v1_final.json) instead of overwriting, and make sh() return a falsy sentinel on error.
findings_verified.verify_note: Hardcoded machine-local paths verified at finalize_faithfulness.py:21, run_p0_5.py:29, config.yaml paths (/workspace/.hf, /root/yh008_work/outputs), and 'cd /workspace/speculation-game-info' git provenance in run_p0.py:46 and run_all.py:50 — the archived copy cannot re-run as archived. The run_p0.py sh() dead-fallback is also verified: it returns the truthy string f"<err {e}>" so `or "nogit"` never fires and a failed git call would embed "<err Command ...>" into run_id/output path (run_p0_5's sh correctly returns "nogit"). finalize_faithfulness.py:173-175/194 does write gate.json and SUMMARY.json into the earlier v1 run directory, so the archived run mixes two script executions. One sub-claim is overstated, though: the archived gate.json/SUMMARY.json carry finalize's schema (fallback_subbranch, clean_probe, wallclock_finalize_s), the run dir lacks run_all's faithfulness/behavioral.json (written before run_all's gate) and instead holds generation checkpoint files, and finalize's docstring explains fp32 free-gen was prohibitively slow — so run_all very likely never wrote a gate for this run; finalize completed the run rather than destroying a prior gate. REPORT.md matches the finalize gate.

findings_verified.title: Archived run contains faithfulness artifacts produced by no archived script (behavioral_checkpoint.jsonl / _v2.jsonl)
findings_verified.detail: The v1 run's faithfulness/ directory holds behavioral_checkpoint.jsonl (23 records) and behavioral_checkpoint_v2.jsonl (5 records) whose schema includes a clean_p_sell field written by none of the archived src scripts (run_all.py writes behavioral.json — absent here; finalize_faithfulness.py writes freegen_checkpoint.jsonl with a different schema). The generating code — evidently one or two intermediate faithfulness-loop variants — was not archived, so these artifacts cannot be traced to code, and it is not determinable which prompt regime produced them (their generations show the same raw-continuation junk, e.g. ' 2023-02-15 14:00:00'). For a pipeline that otherwise snapshots config, pip freeze and git HEAD per run, untraceable artifacts inside the run record are a provenance hole.
findings_verified.evidence: behavioral_checkpoint.jsonl: {"key": "gain_0", ..., "clean_p_sell": 0.7337547834488017, "gen": " 2023-02-15 14:00:00\n## Step 1: ..."} — no script in src/ writes a record with clean_p_sell or the filename behavioral_checkpoint*.jsonl

findings_verified.verify_note: Verified directly. (1) The run directory imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH008/outputs/20260529-092454_7de0bb4/faithfulness/ contains behavioral_checkpoint.jsonl (23 records) and behavioral_checkpoint_v2.jsonl (5 records) with the exact schema claimed, including a clean_p_sell field and raw-continuation junk generations (" 2023-02-15 14:00:00\n## Step 1: ..."). (2) No file under experiments/YH008/src/ (or anywhere in the repo) contains the strings "clean_p_sell" or "behavioral_checkpoint" — grep over the whole repo returns only the artifacts themselves. The archived finalize_faithfulness.py writes only freegen_checkpoint.jsonl (line 102) and behavioral_framing_raw.json; run_all.py writes behavioral.json per README_STAGING.md, which is indeed absent from this run dir. (3) Git history offers no rescue: git log -S clean_p_sell (incl. --pickaxe-all over *.py) hits only commit 2cf5e68, which added the artifacts, not any generating code. (4) README_STAGING.md explains why: the run was executed from an out-of-repo staging dir (/root/yh008_work) during a disk-quota outage, and only the final src variant was rsynced back — the intermediate faithfulness-loop script versions that produced behavioral_checkpoint*.jsonl were never committed. REPORT.md's own artifact inventory (line 126) does not even list the behavioral_checkpoint files, confirming they are orphans from superseded code. So the artifacts are genuinely untraceable to any archived code or prompt regime. Severity: minor is correctly calibrated — this is a provenance gap in intermediate checkpoint files of an archived v1 run (superseded by the P0/P0_5 runs); the reported results (SUMMARY.json, REPORT.md) are traceable to the archived final scripts, so no wrong results in active research code.

findings_verified.title: metrics.py: 'frac_positive' has two incompatible semantics across outputs, and marginal_disposition crashes on an empty gain/loss cell
findings_verified.detail: In bootstrap_mean_ci (line 28), frac_positive = (v > 0).mean() — the fraction of raw data values that are positive (v1 paired disposition: 0.62). In marginal_disposition's disposition_proxy (line 57), frac_positive = (d > 0).mean() where d is the bootstrap distribution of the group-difference — a pseudo one-sided p-value complement (v1 marginal: 1.0). Both appear under the same key name in the same metrics.json, inviting misreading (e.g., 'only 62% of pairs positive' vs '100% of resamples positive' are different levels of evidence). Secondary issues: marginal_disposition raises ValueError from rng.integers(0, 0, ...) if either the gain or loss cell is empty (lines 49-50; bootstrap_mean_ci handles the empty case but the difference bootstrap does not), and paired_ath_asymmetry uses seed+1 for both the gain and loss sub-splits (line 78) — statistically harmless on different data but inconsistent with the pipeline's otherwise careful seed hygiene.
findings_verified.evidence: metrics.py:28: '"frac_positive": float((v > 0).mean())' (raw values) vs :57: '"frac_positive": float((d > 0).mean())' where d = bg - bl (bootstrap draws); :49: 'bg = ga[rng.integers(0, len(ga), ...)]' with no len==0 guard
findings_verified.suggestion: Rename one key (e.g., boot_frac_positive for the bootstrap-based one), guard the empty-cell path, and give each sub-split its own seed offset.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH008/src/metrics.py. (1) Dual semantics of "frac_positive" is real: line 28 computes float((v > 0).mean()) over the raw data values (used by paired_disposition via paired_diff_ci — fraction of raw per-pair diffs > 0, and also inside p_sell_gain/p_sell_loss where it degenerates to "fraction of nonzero P(sell)"), while line 57 computes float((d > 0).mean()) over the bootstrap distribution d = bg - bl of the group-mean difference — a one-sided bootstrap p-value complement. Both are emitted under the identical key name into the same stage1/metrics.json (run_all.py:199, 217-225 nests marginal_disposition, paired_disposition, and ath_asymmetry into one file), so the mixed semantics within one output file is confirmed. (2) Crash on empty cell is real in code: lines 49-50 call rng.integers(0, len(ga), ...) with no len==0 guard, and np.random.Generator.integers(0, 0) raises ValueError ("low >= high"); bootstrap_mean_ci (lines 16-17) guards this case but the difference bootstrap does not. Reachability is runtime-dependent: make_random_states (states.py:80) draws gain/loss at ~50% each over n_random states, so an empty cell requires either a tiny n_random or all P(sell) in one group being NaN — unlikely in practice, which supports keeping severity low rather than refuting the code-level defect. (3) Seed reuse confirmed: paired_ath_asymmetry line 78 passes seed+1 for both the "gain" and "loss" sub-splits; statistically harmless since the data differ, but inconsistent with the seed hygiene used elsewhere (e.g. marginal_disposition uses seed, seed+1, seed+2). No test or caller mitigates any of these. This is imported experiment code (YH008), not the canonical packages/ core; the numbers themselves are not wrong — the issue is misinterpretation risk plus a latent edge-case crash. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: hypotheses.md and literature.md describe an abandoned Brock-Hommes program referencing experiment directories that do not exist
findings_verified.detail: hypotheses.md's three hypotheses reference 'experiments/03_mixed_sweep', 'experiments/01', 'experiments/02', 'experiments/05 switching=True' — none exist (the repo has YH001-YH008), and no YH experiment implements chartist-ratio sweeps, Type C/V information structures, or Brock-Hommes switching. literature.md §'本研究の位置付け' likewise frames the project as 'SG + Brock-Hommes スイッチング接続', which is not the actual YH001-YH008 program (classical reproductions → SG-on-LOB → LLM mech-interp); it also credits '論文1' (Physica A 524:503-518) to 'Katahira & Chen 2019', dropping Hashimoto and Okuda, whom findings.md and the YH007 README correctly include. The top-level README advertises both files ('docs/hypotheses.md 検証したい仮説', 'literature.md 先行研究整理') as live knowledge docs alongside findings.md, whose protocol is to read docs/ before each new YH — so these stale skeleton files sit on the critical reading path.
findings_verified.evidence: hypotheses.md:8: '検証: experiments/03_mixed_sweep で n_c を 0.1→0.9 でスイープ' (no such directory); literature.md:39-41: 'Speculation Game (Layer 1) に Brock-Hommes スイッチング (Layer 2) を接続し…' vs actual YH program in README.md
findings_verified.suggestion: Replace both with the actual research program (or fold the still-relevant Brock-Hommes idea into a 'future directions' note) and fix the paper-1 author list.
findings_verified.verify_note: All factual claims verify. (1) imported/speculation-game-info/docs/hypotheses.md lines 8, 17-18, 26 reference experiments/03_mixed_sweep, experiments/01, experiments/02, and experiments/05 — the experiments/ directory contains only YH001..YH008 (plus YH005_1, YH006_1), so none of the cited paths exist. (2) The three hypotheses (chartist-ratio sweep, Type C/V information structures, Brock-Hommes switching) describe a program not implemented by any YH experiment; the README's own directory table shows the actual program (classical reproductions → SG → SG-on-LOB → LLM interventions). (3) literature.md line 9 credits the Physica A 524:503-518 paper to "Katahira & Chen 2019", whereas YH005/YH005_1/YH007 READMEs correctly cite Katahira, Chen, Hashimoto, Okuda (2019). (4) literature.md lines 39-41 frame the project as SG + Brock-Hommes switching, contradicting the actual research line. (5) README.md lines 48-49 advertise both files as live docs alongside findings.md, whose stated protocol is to read docs/ before starting a new YH, so the stale content sits on the active reading path. Nothing mitigates this — no deprecation notice in either file. It is documentation drift only (no code or results affected), so minor severity is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: apply_direction_hook tears down ALL model hooks on exit, breaking any composed-intervention usage in Stage 3
findings_verified.detail: The context manager adds a single named hook but its finally clause calls self.model.reset_hooks(), which removes every hook on the model — not just the one it added. The Stage-3 design (KO + Rescue, dose-response Amp, specificity controls) plausibly composes interventions (e.g., KO at layer L while a control direction is amplified, or nested with-blocks over layers); with the current implementation, exiting the inner context silently deletes the outer context's hook while the outer with-block believes it is still active, producing wrong 'combined' measurements with no error. Latent today (Stage 3 not yet run) but this is exactly the API the backend-neutral surface promises downstream code can rely on.
findings_verified.evidence: model.py:167-171: self.model.add_hook(name, hook); try: yield; finally: self.model.reset_hooks()  — reset_hooks() is global, not scoped to `name`
findings_verified.suggestion: Track and remove only the added hook (e.g., keep the handle and call model.reset_hooks() replaced with hook-point-specific removal, or assert no other hooks are registered on entry).
findings_verified.verify_note: imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH008/src/model.py lines 167-171 do exactly what the finding says: add_hook(name, hook) then a finally: self.model.reset_hooks(), which in TransformerLens removes ALL hooks on the model, not only the named one added by this context manager. Exiting an inner apply_direction_hook block would therefore silently delete any outer block's hook, corrupting composed KO+Amp / multi-layer specificity measurements with no error. Mitigating facts checked: (a) grep shows zero current callers of apply_direction_hook in the repo (Stage 3 not yet implemented), so the failure is latent; (b) the same-layer KO+Rescue combination is handled inside a single hook via mode='rescue' and does not require nesting. But the API is explicitly documented (module docstring line 10, addendum_v0.3.2.md, stage2_implementation_brief.md) as the backend-neutral composable surface downstream Stage-3 code will rely on, and no test or caller guards against nesting. The defect is real, exactly as evidenced, and 'minor' severity is correctly calibrated (latent, imported research line, but would produce silent wrong results if composed).

findings_verified.title: Paired DiD bootstrap indexes pre and post fact arrays that are filtered independently — misaligned stock pairing or IndexError
findings_verified.detail: _bootstrap_did_ci (imported/PRISM/src/prism/empirical/did.py:116,120-124) performs a paired bootstrap, indexing treatment_pre_vals and treatment_post_vals (and the control pair) with a single index drawn from len(pre_vals), but the arrays come from _compute_stock_facts (lines 85-90), which drops stocks per period independently when a fact estimate is NaN. If post is shorter this raises IndexError; if compositions differ otherwise it silently mis-pairs stocks, corrupting the CI95 fed to GroundTruthDelta; the point estimate (line 186) also mixes stock sets. Impact is limited: the sole production caller (scripts/validate_jpx_cell.py via fetch_jpx_dataset) strips NaN rows wholesale and intersects tickers pre/post (jpx_data.py:140-141,183-204), so the <30-observation filter cannot fire asymmetrically there, leaving only rare estimator NaN failures (e.g., volatility_clustering's exception path, estimators.py:95-97) as a trigger; and the module lives only in the retired PRISM lineage, not in the extracted packages/. Fix: intersect instrument_ids across pre/post before building the value arrays (or index pre/post via a shared id->value map).
findings_verified.evidence:
n_treat = len(treatment_pre_vals)
...
t_idx = rng.integers(0, n_treat, size=n_treat)
t_diff = np.mean(treatment_post_vals[t_idx]) - np.mean(treatment_pre_vals[t_idx])  # arrays not aligned by stock
findings_verified.suggestion: Intersect stocks by instrument_id across pre and post before building the value arrays (keep only stocks with valid facts in both periods), then bootstrap the paired differences.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves exactly as claimed. _compute_stock_facts (did.py:85-90) filters stocks independently per period (n<30 or NaN fact value), and _bootstrap_did_ci (did.py:116,120-124) draws t_idx = rng.integers(0, len(treatment_pre_vals)) and applies the same index to both treatment_post_vals and treatment_pre_vals — a paired resample over arrays that are not guaranteed aligned. If post is shorter an IndexError is near-certain within 2000 resamples; if compositions differ otherwise, the pairing silently shifts and corrupts the CI95 that feeds to_ground_truth_delta and the validate_jpx_cell.py output. Line 186's point estimate likewise mixes different stock sets pre vs post. Tests (test_did.py) only exercise four equal-length arrays, so nothing guards the defect. However, severity is miscalibrated: (1) the only production caller (scripts/validate_jpx_cell.py) feeds data from fetch_jpx_dataset, whose _fetch_returns_matrix drops every row with any NaN (jpx_data.py:140-141) and intersects tickers pre/post (183-204), so all columns reaching did.py are NaN-free and equal-length — the <30-valid-observations trigger cannot fire asymmetrically on this path; (2) the only remaining asymmetric-drop trigger is compute_fact returning NaN in one period, which for real daily data requires the rare volatility_clustering exception path (estimators.py:95-97) or degenerate zero-variance returns; (3) the most probable manifestation when it fires is a loud IndexError, not silent corruption; (4) did.py was never extracted into packages/ — PRISM is the retired lineage, so this is not canonical or active research code. Genuine latent bug, low impact: minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: iid bootstrap CIs for serial-dependence facts (leverage_effect, abs_autocorrelation, squared_return_acf) are null distributions, not confidence intervals
findings_verified.detail: As reported. Confirmed additionally that the flawed iid bootstrap was NOT carried into packages/stylized_facts (no _bootstrap_ci or equivalent iid-resampling in packages/), and that score_sign/score_magnitude use the DiD bootstrap CI from empirical/did.py, not FactResult.ci95. Impact is limited to any downstream reader of FactResult.ci95 / AggregatedFact.ci95 in the retired PRISM lineage.
findings_verified.evidence:
sample = data[rng.integers(0, n, size=n)]  # iid resampling
boot_stats[i] = stat_fn(sample)  # stat_fn measures serial dependence destroyed by the resampling
findings_verified.suggestion: Use a moving-block or stationary bootstrap for dependence statistics, or drop ci95 from these FactResults to avoid misinterpretation.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified at imported/PRISM/src/prism/facts/estimators.py:298-313: _bootstrap_ci draws sample = data[rng.integers(0, n, size=n)] — an iid resample of the raw returns — and applies stat_fn to it. For leverage_effect (line 127, stat = Corr(r_t, r_{t+1}^2)), abs_autocorrelation (line 199, lag-1 ACF of |r|), and squared_return_acf (line 245, lag-1 ACF of r^2), stat_fn measures serial dependence, which iid resampling destroys. The bootstrap distribution is therefore centered near 0 (approximately the permutation-null band, width ~2/sqrt(n)) regardless of the point estimate; for any series with genuine volatility clustering the reported ci95 excludes the estimate itself. This is the textbook failure that block/stationary bootstrap exists to avoid. Severity calibration also verified: ci95 is stored on FactResult and median-aggregated per-fact in pipeline.py:200-210, so it reaches AggregatedFacts, but scoring (scoring/scorer.py) uses only the DiD ground-truth ci95 (empirical/did.py:_bootstrap_did_ci, a separate stock-level bootstrap), so scores are unaffected. No copy of _bootstrap_ci exists under packages/ (grep found no matches), so the extracted canonical stylized_facts code is not contaminated, and PRISM is a retired lineage per the ADR. minor is the correct severity: a genuine statistical defect, but confined to a non-driving diagnostic field in retired code.

findings_verified.title: transaction_tax semantics are incoherent across adapters: ZI/SG convert a 0.1–0.3% tax into a 10–30x tick-size increase while CI applies ~1e-5 demand drag and FW applies rate-proportional drag
findings_verified.detail: ZI (zi.py:95-98) and SG (sg.py:109-112) map transaction_tax to tick_size = max(tick_size, rate * fundamental_value): with defaults (tick 0.01, price 100) a 0.3% tax becomes tick 0.3 — a 30x coarsening of the price grid, a huge structural shock. CI's tax is a verified no-op and FW's is a mild 1-rate demand drag. The same NER intervention therefore probes mechanically incommensurable channels per adapter, so cross-adapter 'transaction_tax' sign-match comparisons (the core PRISM divergence analysis) do not compare like with like. The tick-as-tax heuristic is documented in the ZI docstring but its magnitude mismatch with the other adapters is not. The ZI copy lives on unchanged in packages/abm_models/zero_intelligence/model.py:95-98.
findings_verified.evidence:
tax_rate = intervention.canonical_params.get("rate", 0.001)
effective_cost = tax_rate * self.params.fundamental_value
new_params.tick_size = max(self.params.tick_size, effective_cost)  # 0.001*100=0.1 → 10x default tick
findings_verified.suggestion: Define one canonical tax transduction (e.g. proportional demand drag 1-rate) or record per-adapter transduction strength so divergence analysis can condition on it.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all cited code. (1) ZI (imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/zi.py:95-98) and SG (sg.py:109-112) map transaction_tax to tick_size = max(tick_size, rate * fundamental_value); with defaults (tick 0.01, fundamental 100) rate=0.001 yields tick 0.1 (10x) and rate=0.003 yields 0.3 (30x) — a large structural coarsening of the price grid, not a trading cost. (2) CI (ci.py:119-121, 198-200) instead sets transaction_cost=rate and applies a demand shrink factor of cost/(|net_order|*mid) = rate/mid ≈ 1e-5 with mid≈100 — effectively negligible, matching the auditor's "~1e-5 drag". (3) FW (fw.py:115-117, 183-185) applies a multiplicative (1 - rate) demand drag. LM (lm.py:122-124) also uses transaction_cost=rate. So the same canonical "transaction_tax" intervention exercises mechanically incommensurable channels of wildly different magnitude per adapter, making cross-adapter sign-match/divergence comparisons (pipeline.py/scorer.py) not like-for-like. The identical tick-as-tax code also exists verbatim in packages/abm_models/abm_models/zero_intelligence/model.py:95-98 (the auditor's cited path omitted the inner abm_models directory, but the code is there). Mitigation check: no test or caller guards this; however, apply_intervention/transaction_tax is not referenced anywhere in experiments/ or tests/, and PRISM is a retired lineage, so the defect currently affects no active research output. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated: a genuine comparability defect (partly an intentional, docstring-documented proxy whose magnitude mismatch is undocumented) in retired/dormant code paths.

findings_verified.title: calibrate_baseline does not calibrate (achieved vol is orders of magnitude off target) and mutates self.params in place; packaged wrapper also mutates shared params
findings_verified.detail: calibrate_baseline (imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/ci.py:72-90) sets noise_scale=std(pre_returns) and hardcoded price_impact=0.5 but never validates achieved volatility: with target vol 2e-2, realized simulated vol is ~8e-4 (~25x low, verified by running the adapter over 5 seeds), matching the degenerate low-vol baseline independently noted at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/adapters.py:88-92. It also mutates self.params in place (pre adapter silently reconfigured; state leaks across reuse) and hardcodes CalibrationArtifact.seed=0. The packaged wrapper ChiarellaIori._adapter() (packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/__init__.py:23-26) likewise mutates the caller-owned CIParams via p.n_steps = self.n_steps. Minor: PRISM's calibration path is retired and not exercised by the canonical packages; the wrapper mutation is limited to n_steps and idempotent per wrapper instance.
findings_verified.evidence:
self.params.noise_scale = target_vol
self.params.price_impact = 0.5   # hardcoded, overwrites dataclass default; metadata={'target_vol': ...} never validated
findings_verified.suggestion: Return a new adapter instead of mutating; add a closed-loop check (simulate baseline, compare realized std to target within tolerance) and record the achieved value in the artifact.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three sub-claims verified against the code and by execution. (1) Calibration does not calibrate: imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/ci.py:72-90 sets noise_scale=target_vol and hardcodes price_impact=0.5, but never simulates to verify. I ran the CI adapter with noise_scale=0.02 (target vol 2e-2) over 5 seeds: realized return std is ~8.1e-4, i.e. ~25x (1.4 orders of magnitude) below target, because the noise passes through a_noise≈0.3, price_impact/depth attenuation, and tick rounding. The atlas comment at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/adapters.py:88-92 independently documents CI's "degenerate" low-vol baseline, corroborating the finding. metadata={'target_vol': ...} is recorded but never checked. (2) In-place mutation: lines 74-76 mutate self.params (noise_scale, price_impact, n_steps), silently reconfiguring the pre adapter; CalibrationArtifact.seed is hardcoded 0 (line 89). (3) Packaged wrapper mutation confirmed at packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/__init__.py:24-25 — `p = self.params or CIParams(); p.n_steps = self.n_steps` writes into the caller-owned CIParams instance. No test or caller guards any of this. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated: PRISM is a retired lineage, the calibration path is not part of the canonical packages (only simulate() is used via the ChiarellaIori wrapper), and the wrapper mutation only touches n_steps with a value that is re-applied consistently on every call. One correction to the detail: the vol shortfall is ~25x (realized ~8e-4 vs 2e-2), not "~1e-4" / multiple orders of magnitude.

findings_verified.title: Intervention tests only assert parameter assignment, never simulated effect — the CI tax no-op is invisible to a 294-test suite
findings_verified.detail: Transaction-tax intervention tests in imported/PRISM/tests/unit/test_ci_adapter.py (lines 76-92) and test_fw_adapter.py (lines 104-120) assert only parameter plumbing (transaction_cost == 0.002, other params unchanged), never any effect on simulated output. The sole behavioral pre/post intervention test in the repo (test_zi_adapter.py::test_tick_change_produces_different_returns) covers only tick_size on the ZI adapter. Workspace tests/test_prism_family_parity.py checks only n_paths=1 imported-vs-packaged output equality, faithfully preserving any defect. Note correction: simulate(n_paths>1) is exercised elsewhere (test_ci_adapter.py lines 44, 124-142 use n_paths=3/5), but never after an applied intervention; the tax's behavioral consequence is untested anywhere, so a bit-identical tax no-op passes the full suite.
findings_verified.evidence:
post_adapter = adapter.apply_intervention(calibration, intervention)
assert post_adapter.params.transaction_cost == 0.002  # only parameter plumbing is tested
findings_verified.suggestion: Add effect-level tests: assert post-intervention returns differ from baseline at the same seed, and that the direction of a volatility/spread proxy matches the documented mechanism.
findings_verified.verify_note: Read imported/PRISM/tests/unit/test_ci_adapter.py: TestCIIntervention's transaction-tax tests (lines 76-92) assert only that apply_intervention sets params.transaction_cost=0.002 and leaves behavioral params unchanged; no assertion on simulated returns. Identical pattern in test_fw_adapter.py (transaction_tax tests). Grepped all test suites: the only pre/post-intervention behavioral assertion anywhere is test_zi_adapter.py::TestZIAsSmuggleDetector::test_tick_change_produces_different_returns, which covers tick_size only, never transaction_tax, and only for the ZI adapter. Workspace tests/test_prism_family_parity.py compares imported vs packaged simulate(seed=42, n_paths=1) equality only (preserving any defect symmetrically), and test_registry_protocol only checks finiteness. So a transaction-tax no-op would pass every test — the gap is real. One inaccuracy in the original detail: simulate(n_paths>1) IS exercised (test_ci_adapter.py lines 44, 124-142 use n_paths=3/5), but never in combination with an applied intervention, so the substantive claim stands. Severity minor is calibrated: PRISM is a retired lineage, and this is a test-coverage gap rather than wrong results itself.

findings_verified.title: parse_decision matches the substring 'move' anywhere in the reply — regression from v1's first-token parse, biases decisions toward Move
findings_verified.detail: parse_decision in imported/agent-based-modeling/src/backends/base.py:72-78 classifies any reply containing the substring 'move' as Move, checking Move before defaulting to Stay. This regresses v1's parser (experiments/poc_llm_decision/run.py:107-114), which examined only the first whitespace token and checked 'stay' first. With max_tokens/num_predict=10 and no constrained decoding in either backend, multi-word replies are possible, and negated phrasings ('do not move', 'won't move') or prompt echoes ('Stay or Move') misparse as Move, biasing v2 segregation/happiness dynamics toward movement. Note: 'moving' does NOT trigger the bug ('move' is not a substring of 'moving'), so one of the original examples is wrong. Impact is limited to the LLM-Schelling PoC in the imported agent-based-modeling side project (no tests, no canonical-package consumers), hence minor rather than major.
findings_verified.suggestion: Parse the first alphabetic token and match it exactly against {'stay','move'} (as run.py v1 did), and count unparseable replies separately instead of defaulting them into either class.
findings_verified.verify_note: Code behaves exactly as claimed: base.py:76 returns 'Move' if 'move' appears anywhere in the lowercased reply, so negations ('I would rather not move') and echoes of the prompt's 'Stay or Move.' misparse as Move. Regression verified against v1 (experiments/poc_llm_decision/run.py:107-114), which parsed only the first token and checked 'stay' first. No mitigation exists: ollama_backend.py:71 and vllm_backend.py:80 both call parse_decision on raw text with max_tokens=10 and no constrained decoding/stop sequences, run_v2.py:151-176 uses the parsed decision directly to drive moves/happiness, and there are no tests covering the parser. One evidence example is incorrect ('Stay — moving is unnecessary' parses as Stay, since 'move' is not a substring of 'moving'), but the core defect stands. Severity downgraded: this lives in imported/agent-based-modeling, a proof-of-concept side project, not canonical packages/ or the active YH speculation-game research line; the prompt demands a single-word answer at temperature 0.2, so misparses require the model to elaborate, and saved results.json files record no raw responses to show it actually bit in past runs.

findings_verified.title: Backend errors are silently coerced to decision='Stay'; combined with moves==0 => 'Equilibrium', an LLM outage fabricates convergence
findings_verified.detail: Both backends (imported/agent-based-modeling/src/backends/ollama_backend.py:73-75 and vllm_backend.py:82-84) coerce every per-agent exception to decision="Stay". In run_v2.py, all-Stay yields moves==0 (line 157/163), which the run loop (lines 211-214) declares as equilibrium, and line 176 marks all agents happy. save_results() writes no error metric and discards raw_response, so a results.json produced by a total LLM outage is indistinguishable from a genuine step-1 convergence; the committed output_v2_ollama_5x5/results.json exhibits exactly this ambiguous signature. Mitigation is partial: errors are printed to the console during a live run but never persisted. Impact is limited: this is a proof-of-concept LLM-Schelling demo inside an imported side project, not imported by any canonical package, workspace experiment, or test, and unrelated to the active speculation-game research line — hence minor rather than major.
findings_verified.suggestion: Track an error count per step, persist it in history/results.json, and either retry failed calls or abort the run when the error rate exceeds a threshold instead of folding failures into the 'Stay' class.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three claimed links verified in code. (1) ollama_backend.py:73-75 and vllm_backend.py:82-84 catch bare Exception per agent and return DecisionResult(decision="Stay", raw_response="ERROR: ..."). (2) run_v2.py:157 selects movers by decision=="Move", so a full backend outage yields moves==0, which run_v2.py:211-214 treats as "*** Equilibrium at step s ***" and breaks; line 176 sets every agent happy=True, so history records happy_pct=1.0. (3) save_results() (run_v2.py:316-335) persists no error information: raw_response strings are dropped, error calls are counted inside total_llm_calls (line 152), and _record() stores only happy_pct/segregation. Therefore results.json from an all-error run is indistinguishable from a genuinely converged run. The committed output_v2_ollama_5x5/results.json (10 agents, terminated at step 1 of max_steps 3, happy_pct 1.0) matches the ambiguous signature the auditor described. Partial mitigation only: errors print to console during a live run, but nothing is persisted and no test or caller guards the path. Severity is miscalibrated, though: the code is in imported/agent-based-modeling/experiments/poc_llm_decision/, an explicitly labeled PoC in a retired/side project; it is not imported by packages/, workspace experiments/, or tests/, and is unrelated to the canonical SG/financial-ABM research line. Genuine but low-impact => minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: Candidate-cell scoring counts the moving agent as its own neighbor (self-similarity 1.0), biasing relocation toward cells adjacent to the current position
findings_verified.detail: In step(), when an unhappy agent evaluates candidate empty cells, temp_neighbors is gathered from self.grid while the agent still occupies its old cell. Any candidate within the Moore neighborhood of the agent's current position therefore includes the agent itself, and similarity_matrix[id][id] == 1.0 (embeddings are normalized) inflates that candidate's avg_sim. Unhappy agents are thus systematically drawn to cells next to where they already are — a spatial-persistence bias absent from the model description, which affects measured segregation dynamics. get_neighbors/compute_happiness are unaffected (they skip dx==dy==0 at the agent's own cell).
findings_verified.suggestion: Filter the agent itself out of temp_neighbors (if n is not None and n.agent_id != agent.agent_id), or vacate the grid cell before scoring candidates and restore it if no move is made.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is accurate. In step() (imported/agent-based-modeling/src/semantic_schelling.py lines 334-349), candidate empty cells are scored by scanning the Moore neighborhood via self.grid.get((nx,ny)) BEFORE the agent is removed from its old cell (self.grid[old_pos] = None only happens at line 360, after scoring). So if a candidate empty cell is adjacent to the agent's current position, the agent itself is collected into temp_neighbors, and since embeddings are normalized (line 167 normalize_embeddings=True; TF-IDF fallback also cosine; line 255 similarity_matrix = embeddings @ embeddings.T), similarity_matrix[agent_id][agent_id] == 1.0, inflating that candidate's avg_sim. This biases relocation toward cells near the current position. The comparison functions get_neighbors/compute_happiness correctly skip dx==dy==0 at the agent's own cell, so no mitigation exists; no test guards this. Severity minor is correct: the file lives in imported/agent-based-modeling (a related side project, not the canonical packages/ code or the active YH speculation-game line), the bias only affects at most a few of the 10 randomly sampled candidates per move, and it does not corrupt canonical research results.

findings_verified.title: Falsy-zero bug: plot_grid(step=0) saves the INITIAL grid as 'grid_step_final.png' — the committed output artifact is mislabeled
findings_verified.detail: filename = f"grid_step_{step or 'final'}.png" (and the title at line 419, f"Step {step or 'Final'}") treats step=0 as falsy. main() calls model.plot_grid(step=0) before the run, which creates grid_step_final.png containing the initial random layout titled 'Step Final', while the true final state is saved under its step number (output/grid_step_50.png). The committed output/ directory exhibits exactly this: grid_step_final.png is the step-0 state. Anyone reading the artifacts (or a figure built from them) gets initial and final states swapped.
findings_verified.evidence:
filename = f"grid_step_{step or 'final'}.png"
-- main() --
model.plot_grid(step=0)
...
model.plot_grid(step=model.history[-1]["step"])
findings_verified.suggestion: Use an explicit check: name = 'final' if step is None else f'{step:03d}', and pass step=None only for the true final plot. Regenerate or annotate the committed output/ images.
findings_verified.verify_note: In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/agent-based-modeling/src/semantic_schelling.py, line 444 uses `f"grid_step_{step or 'final'}.png"` and line 419 uses `f"Step {step or 'Final'}"`. Since `0 or 'final'` evaluates to 'final', the call `model.plot_grid(step=0)` at line 562 (initial state, before `model.run()`) saves the initial random grid as grid_step_final.png titled 'Step Final'. The actual final state (line 569, step=50) is saved as grid_step_50.png. The committed output/ directory contains both files, consistent with the swap. No other callers or tests mitigate this. Severity 'minor' is correct: it is a real, misleading artifact-labeling bug, but confined to a visualization in an imported side project (not the canonical SG research line) and does not affect simulation results or data.

findings_verified.title: v2 backends pass no seed to the LLM API while results.json records seed=42 — runs are not reproducible despite provenance implying they are
findings_verified.detail: OllamaBackend._ask_one sends only temperature=0.2 and num_predict; VLLMBackend likewise sends temperature/max_tokens with no seed. temperature > 0 with no seed makes every decision stochastic and unrepeatable, yet run_v2.py save_results() writes 'seed': self.seed into results.json config, implying the run is reproducible from that seed (the Python-side rng only controls placement/shuffles). The older src/semantic_schelling.py got this right for text generation (options={'temperature': 0.9, 'seed': self.config.seed + index}), so the v2 refactor dropped seeding. Given the sibling PROV-ABM repo's entire thesis is provenance honesty, this is a notable regression.
findings_verified.suggestion: Thread a per-request seed (e.g., simulation seed + step*n_agents + uid) into Ollama options['seed'] and the OpenAI-compatible 'seed' field for vLLM, or record in results.json that LLM decisions are unseeded/nondeterministic.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified as claimed. OllamaBackend._ask_one (imported/agent-based-modeling/src/backends/ollama_backend.py:62-65) sends only {"temperature": self.temperature, "num_predict": self.max_tokens} — no "seed" key — with default temperature=0.2 > 0, so Ollama sampling is nondeterministic. VLLMBackend (src/backends/vllm_backend.py:71-74) likewise sends only temperature/max_tokens with no seed. Yet run_v2.py (experiments/poc_llm_decision/run_v2.py:324) writes "seed": self.seed into results.json config; the Python RNG seeded at line 95 (random.Random(seed)) controls only placement/shuffles, not LLM decisions, so the recorded seed does not make the run reproducible. The older src/semantic_schelling.py:132 does pass options={"temperature": 0.9, "seed": self.config.seed + index}, confirming the v2 refactor dropped LLM-side seeding rather than it being intentionally omitted. No test or caller mitigates this. Severity minor is calibrated correctly: it is a genuine reproducibility/provenance-honesty regression, but in an imported side project (agent-based-modeling, an LLM-Schelling PoC), not the canonical SG research line, and it does not corrupt results — it only makes the recorded seed misleading.

findings_verified.title: Cancellation is uniform over occupied price levels, not quantity-weighted as the module docstring claims (and limit-offset k has min 2, mean offset_mean+1)
findings_verified.detail: The header (line 15) documents cancels as '占有 level から数量重みで 1 単位除去' (remove 1 unit weighted by quantity), matching Smith et al. 2003 per-order cancellation. The code picks a level uniformly among nonzero levels (nz[rng.integers(nz.size)]), so a level with 100 resting units is cancelled at the same rate as one with 1 unit. This changes the depth profile's stationary distribution — relevant because Finding 0002's own '既知の問題 1' flags a suspicious depth artifact (36 -> 356 at tick=2) that this mis-specified cancel dynamic plausibly feeds (thick levels created by tick rounding decay far too slowly relative to per-unit cancellation). Separately, k = 1 + rng.geometric(1.0/offset_mean) yields k >= 2 with mean offset_mean+1 = 6, while the docstring says 'k~geometric、k≥1' with mean offset 5.
findings_verified.evidence:
# docstring line 15: 取消: 占有 level から数量重みで 1 単位除去。
nz = np.nonzero(bid)[0]
if nz.size:
    lvl = nz[rng.integers(nz.size)]
    bid[lvl] -= 1
findings_verified.suggestion: Sample the cancelled level with probability proportional to resting quantity (e.g., rng.choice(nz, p=bid[nz]/bid[nz].sum())), and use k = rng.geometric(1.0/offset_mean) if k>=1 with mean offset_mean is intended. Re-measure the tick-vs-depth table in docs/findings/0002 afterwards.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/orderbook.py. (1) Cancel: docstring line 15 says '取消: 占有 level から数量重みで 1 単位除去' (quantity-weighted removal, i.e. per-unit cancellation as in Smith et al. 2003), but lines 136-144 do `nz = np.nonzero(bid)[0]; lvl = nz[rng.integers(nz.size)]; bid[lvl] -= 1` — a uniform choice over occupied price levels regardless of resting quantity. A level with 100 units is cancelled at the same per-event rate as a level with 1 unit, so deep levels decay far more slowly than per-unit cancellation would imply; this changes the stationary depth profile and is not Smith-2003-faithful. (2) Offset: line 118 `k = 1 + rng.geometric(1.0 / params.offset_mean)`; numpy's geometric is supported on {1,2,...} with mean 1/p = offset_mean, so k >= 2 with mean offset_mean+1 = 6, contradicting docstring line 13 'k~geometric、k≥1' and the offset_mean=5 parameter comment (line 32). No test or caller mitigates either: tests only exercise channel_correlation/decoupling behavior, and the probe's qualitative conclusion (corr(Δmid, net-flow) < 1) does not depend on these details, which is why severity stays minor. The code is self-consistent and reachable; the defect is documentation/model-spec drift in an imported toy probe, not in canonical packages/ or the active YH research line. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: sf3_hill returns the Hill statistic 1/alpha but the docstring claims 'tail index, smaller = heavier tail' — direction inverted
findings_verified.detail: sf3_hill returns the Hill statistic mean(log(top/thresh)) = estimate of 1/alpha (larger = heavier tail), but its docstring describes it as the tail index alpha with '小さいほど heavy tail' — direction inverted. Not used in CALIBRATION_SF (SF3 there is excess kurtosis); SF3_hill is a reported-only feature, so calibration and tests are unaffected. Risk is human misranking of tail heaviness when reading reported values per the docstring.
findings_verified.evidence:
return float(np.mean(np.log(top) - np.log(thresh)))
# docstring: """Hill estimator(top tail_frac の tail index、小さいほど heavy tail)。"""
findings_verified.suggestion: Either return 1.0/mean(...) and keep the docstring, or fix the docstring to '返り値は 1/α(大きいほど heavy tail)'.
findings_verified.verify_note: At imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/sf_battery.py:65 the function returns float(np.mean(np.log(top) - np.log(thresh))), the classic Hill statistic H = (1/k) Σ log(x_i/x_(k)) which estimates 1/alpha (the reciprocal of the tail index). For this quantity, LARGER values indicate heavier tails. The docstring on line 58 states 'Hill estimator(top tail_frac の tail index、小さいほど heavy tail)' — i.e., claims it is the tail index alpha with smaller = heavier tail — which is the exact inverse of the returned quantity's direction. There is no inversion (e.g., 1/H) before return, so the docstring misdescribes the output. Mitigation check: measure_sf_battery exposes it only as the reported key 'SF3_hill' (line 102); the calibration target key SF3 is excess kurtosis (line 101), and CALIBRATION_SF = ('SF1','SF2','SF3','SF4') excludes 'SF3_hill', so calibration, equivalence checks, and tests are unaffected — the impact is limited to misinterpretation by a human reading the docstring when comparing reported tail-heaviness. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated: genuine docs drift in an imported (non-canonical, non-package) repo with no effect on computed results.

findings_verified.title: Testing gap: no chance-level null test for the CNN, no test of the contract-mandated theta=0 identity for apply_masking, and orderbook.py (Finding 0002's basis) is entirely untested
findings_verified.detail: test_classifiers.py pins 'identical distribution -> ~50%' for LR (test_summary_identical_is_chance) and XGB, but not for the CNN — the one classifier whose implementation has an epoch-selection bias that a null test would immediately expose (a max-over-epochs estimator on ~16-sample folds drifts well above 0.5 under the null). Separately, docs/model_contract_v0.md §2.3 declares theta=0 identity ('intervene(θ=0) は no-op と bit 同一であること(property test 対象)') with toy/observation.py apply_masking as the reference implementation, yet no test in tests/ touches apply_masking at all (grep confirms zero hits). toy/orderbook.py, the sole implementation behind Finding 0002's corr=0.33 decoupling claim, likewise has no unit test pinning the correlation or book invariants (non-negative depth, bid < ask).

findings_verified.suggestion: Add a slow-marked CNN null test (identical AR(1) populations -> accuracy in [0.4, 0.6]); add a property test asserting np.array_equal(apply_masking(s, theta=0), s) for all four schemes; pin corr(Δmid,net-flow) < 0.6 and book invariants for simulate_lob at a fixed seed.
findings_verified.verify_note: All three sub-claims verified read-only in imported/PROV-ABM-atlas. (1) tests/unit/test_classifiers.py contains chance-level null tests for the summary/LR classifier (test_summary_identical_is_chance) and the IR/GBDT classifier (test_ir_identical_is_chance), but the only CNN test is test_cnn_separable_high_accuracy — no null test. The concern is real: toy/classifiers.py lines ~103-121 select best_state by max validation accuracy over epochs evaluated on the held-out fold itself (val computed on xe/ye, the same fold used for the reported accuracy), an optimistic max-over-epochs estimator that would drift above 0.5 under the null on ~16-sample folds — exactly the bias a null test would catch, and it is the one classifier without one. (2) docs/model_contract_v0.md line 56 mandates the theta=0 identity as a property-test target ("intervene(θ=0) は no-op と bit 同一であること（property test 対象）"), toy/observation.py:74 defines apply_masking, yet grep over tests/ finds zero references to apply_masking (toy/intervention.py is scaffold-only and defers to it). (3) toy/orderbook.py exists and no file under tests/ mentions "orderbook" (grep returns nothing). Severity "minor / testing" is correctly calibrated: PROV-ABM-atlas is an imported related project, not the canonical packages/ or the active speculation-game line, and the finding is a coverage gap rather than a direct wrong-result in canonical code (the underlying CNN selection bias itself was already the subject of another finding).

findings_verified.title: Kernel seeds the global legacy NumPy RNG; with NUMBA_DISABLE_JIT=1 (the CI configuration) this silently mutates np.random global state for the whole process
findings_verified.detail: _run_kernel calls np.random.seed(seed) / np.random.random(). Under JIT this touches only Numba's internal per-thread RNG, but toy/market.py documents that CI runs with NUMBA_DISABLE_JIT=1, in which case _run_kernel executes as plain Python and reseeds the process-global numpy legacy RNG. Any code (test, notebook, other library) relying on np.random state after a run_fast call gets a silently reseeded stream — a hidden-state/env-coupling hazard in the very repo whose ctx discipline exists to forbid untracked global RNG (capture.lint_ctx_purity flags exactly this pattern in agent code). Results are still deterministic per seed, but reproducibility of surrounding code depends on whether JIT was enabled.
findings_verified.suggestion: Pass the noise draws in as a pre-generated array from a local Generator outside the kernel, or save/restore np.random state around the no-JIT path and document the global-state side effect.
findings_verified.verify_note: imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/kernel.py lines 52 and 69 call np.random.seed(seed) and np.random.random() inside the @njit kernel. toy/market.py:36-55 provides a pass-through njit when numba is missing, and the repo's CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:46) sets NUMBA_DISABLE_JIT=1, so in CI (and any no-numba environment) _run_kernel executes as plain Python and reseeds/consumes the process-global numpy legacy RNG on every run_fast call. Nothing saves/restores the state and no test or caller mitigates it. Under JIT the seeding only affects Numba's internal RNG, so behavior (which global stream downstream code sees) is coupled to the JIT environment variable — exactly the untracked-global-RNG pattern the repo's own ctx discipline forbids. Outputs of run_fast itself remain deterministic per seed, and this is a non-canonical fast-path in a related (not the highest-priority) project, so severity 'minor' is calibrated correctly.

findings_verified.title: Partial per-ticker yfinance failure silently produces an empty (or truncated) frame that passes validate() and is cached for the whole day
findings_verified.detail: Partial per-ticker yfinance degradation silently truncates history for ALL tickers and caches the truncated frame for the day. yfinance_source.py:133 (df.dropna(subset=close_cols, how="any")) drops every row where any ticker's close is NaN; if one ticker returns NaN closes for a sub-period (e.g. its most recent days), the whole universe's history is silently shortened to that ticker's coverage. validate() (data/base.py:54) has no minimum-row or expected-date-coverage check, so the truncated frame passes; load_universe (data/__init__.py:48) caches it under today's key, and same-day reruns serve it from cache with no warning and no retry (the retry loop at yfinance_source.py:61-86 only catches raised exceptions, not degraded results). Empirically verified: a 10-row frame with 4 trailing NaN closes on one ticker yields a 6-row frame that validates, caches, and reloads silently. However, the original finding's worst case is wrong: a ticker that fails ENTIRELY (all-NaN) empties the frame, and cache.save() then crashes with IndexError (MarketDataFrame.date_range at base.py:40 does df.index[0] on the empty index) BEFORE the meta.json is written; cache.load requires both files and returns None for the orphaned parquet, so an empty frame is never cached or reused — that path fails loudly (albeit with an unhelpful IndexError instead of a validation message). Suggested fix: log per-ticker row coverage after dropna, and add a min-rows / expected-end-date check to validate(). Located in imported/market-dynamics (retired-adjacent own project), not canonical packages/ code.
findings_verified.evidence:
df = df.dropna(subset=close_cols, how="any")
...
# validate(): no n_rows check; close.isna().any() is vacuously False on an empty frame
findings_verified.suggestion: After dropna, assert a minimum row count and per-ticker non-NaN coverage vs. the requested range; raise (and retry) on all-NaN tickers before caching.
findings_verified.verify_note:
Tested both failure modes empirically with the real code (stub download_fn), so this is not speculative.

(1) The EMPTY-frame half of the finding is REFUTED by an accidental guard the auditor missed. When one ticker of a batch is entirely NaN, dropna(subset=close_cols, how='any') at imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/data/yfinance_source.py:133 does empty the frame, and validate() (data/base.py:54-83) does pass vacuously — both verified. But cache.save() (data/cache.py:64) computes mdf.date_range, and MarketDataFrame.date_range (data/base.py:40) does df.index[0] on an empty index, which raises IndexError. Verified: "save RAISED: IndexError". So load_universe (data/__init__.py:48) crashes loudly before completing the cache write; the orphaned .parquet without .meta.json is never served on subsequent runs (cache.load requires both files — verified it returns None). The claimed "empty frame cached for the whole day, all subsequent same-day runs reuse the bad data" does not happen.

(2) The TRUNCATION half is CONFIRMED exactly as claimed. When a ticker's close is NaN for only a sub-period (partial yfinance degradation, e.g. last N days missing), line 133 silently drops those rows for ALL tickers, validate() passes (no minimum-row or date-coverage check anywhere), and the truncated frame is cached under the day's key and served from cache thereafter. Verified: 10 rows in, 6 rows out (last date 2020-01-09 instead of 2020-01-15), cached and reloaded with n_rows=6, zero warnings logged. The retry loop (yfinance_source.py:61-86) only catches raised exceptions, so a degraded-but-non-raising download is never retried — also as claimed. No test covers partial per-ticker degradation (tests/test_data.py only tests a single mid-series NaN row).

Severity recalibration to minor: (a) the worst variant (empty/whole-ticker failure) fails loudly, not silently; (b) the surviving silent variant requires a specific runtime condition (yfinance returning partial NaN coverage for one ticker without raising); (c) the intersection-alignment dropna is partly documented design ("boundary rows are dropped, not filled", base.py:6); (d) this is imported/market-dynamics — a related own project, not the canonical packages/ code or the active speculation-game research line; (e) blast radius is one day (cache key includes today's date). Genuine defect worth a coverage/min-rows check and a log line, but not "likely to bite" at major level.

findings_verified.title: BetaVAEEmbedder seeds the GLOBAL torch/numpy RNG in __init__ and consumes global RNG during training
findings_verified.detail: torch.manual_seed(cfg.seed) / np.random.seed(cfg.seed) in the constructor are process-global side effects: (1) constructing an embedder reseeds every other torch consumer in the process (load() does this too, so loading a checkpoint silently resets global RNG state); (2) the reparameterization noise (torch.randn_like at line 68) draws from the global RNG, so training results change if any other torch RNG call happens between __init__ and fit() — e.g. constructing two embedders before fitting either would NOT reproduce the fit-immediately case. The DataLoader shuffle is correctly pinned to a local generator, but the weight init and eps-sampling are not.
findings_verified.evidence:
torch.manual_seed(cfg.seed)
np.random.seed(cfg.seed)
...
eps = torch.randn_like(std)  # global RNG
findings_verified.suggestion: Use a dedicated torch.Generator for init and eps sampling (torch.randn(..., generator=g)), and avoid reseeding globals in load().
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified against imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/embedding/vae.py. (1) Lines 85-86: torch.manual_seed(cfg.seed) and np.random.seed(cfg.seed) in BetaVAEEmbedder.__init__ are process-global side effects. (2) Line 190: load() calls cls(in_dim=..., cfg=cfg), so loading a checkpoint silently reseeds the global RNGs — exactly as claimed. (3) Line 68: eps = torch.randn_like(std) draws reparameterization noise from the global RNG, and weight init in _BetaVAEModule (nn.Linear defaults) also uses the global RNG; only the DataLoader shuffle is pinned to a local torch.Generator (lines 119-124). Nothing mitigates this: fit() does not reseed, so any torch RNG consumption between __init__ and fit() (e.g. constructing a second embedder does NOT reseed back since the second constructor reseeds to its own cfg.seed — but interleaving other torch.randn calls, or fitting two embedders whose constructions were interleaved) changes training results. Concrete failure: e1 = BetaVAEEmbedder(d, cfg); torch.randn(10); e1.fit(X) yields different weights/embeddings than construct-then-fit-immediately, breaking reproducibility claims. Severity: minor is correctly calibrated — this is in imported/market-dynamics (a related own project, not the canonical packages/ or the active YH speculation-game line), the results are not wrong per se, only fragile to reproduce, and in the common single-embedder construct-then-fit pattern the runs are reproducible.

findings_verified.title: density.kde_bandwidth config knob is dead — every call site hardcodes the 'scott' default
findings_verified.detail: DensityConfig.kde_bandwidth exists and is documented in config.yaml ('scott' | 'silverman' | float), but pipeline.run_atlas (pipeline.py:103), walkforward.single_split_backtest (walkforward.py:93), and universe_comparison.run_one_universe (universe_comparison.py:102) all call free_energy_with_basins(z, grid_size=cfg.density.grid_size) without passing bandwidth. All three pipeline call sites — state_atlas/pipeline.py:103, state_atlas/backtest/walkforward.py:93, state_atlas/experiments/universe_comparison.py:102 — call free_energy_with_basins(z, grid_size=cfg.density.grid_size) without a bandwidth argument, so free_energy.py:432's default "scott" always applies (kde = gaussian_kde(pts.T, bw_method=bandwidth) at free_energy.py:110). Only online/monitor.py:49 accepts a bandwidth parameter, and it is a function default, not wired to config. Editing the YAML value silently changes nothing. Severity minor is correct: it is a real config-drift defect, but it lives in a non-canonical imported project (imported/market-dynamics, not packages/ or the active YH speculation-game line), and the always-used default matches the YAML default, so no existing results are wrong.

findings_verified.title: _label_points duplicates label_points_on_grid, and both assign points to the lower-left cell, not the 'nearest grid cell' the docstrings claim
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as reported. _label_points (universe_comparison.py:58) duplicates label_points_on_grid (dynamics/latent_dynamics.py:45), and both use searchsorted-1 over grid-center arrays, mapping points to the lower-left cell rather than the documented nearest cell (half-cell systematic bias near basin boundaries).
findings_verified.evidence: ix = np.clip(np.searchsorted(grid.z1, z[:, 0]) - 1, 0, len(grid.z1) - 1)  # lower cell, not nearest
findings_verified.suggestion: Delete the copy and import label_points_on_grid; if 'nearest' is intended, use np.searchsorted(centers, x) on midpoints or round((x - z1[0]) / dz).
findings_verified.verify_note: (1) Duplication: universe_comparison.py:58-62 `_label_points` is a copy of `label_points_on_grid` in state_atlas/dynamics/latent_dynamics.py:45-51 (identical searchsorted logic, only `.astype(int)` and shape validation differ); pipeline.py in the same package imports from dynamics, so the import path exists. (2) Behavior vs docstring: FreeEnergyGrid.z1/z2 are documented as grid *centers* (density/free_energy.py:39, built via np.linspace). `np.clip(np.searchsorted(grid.z1, z)-1, 0, len-1)` returns the index of the center at or below the point — a floor operation with a systematic half-cell offset, not "nearest grid cell" as both docstrings claim. A point just above center i but nearer to center i+1 still gets label i. Severity is correctly minor: imported/ experiment code (not canonical packages/), bias is half a cell on an 80x80 grid and only flips labels for points near basin boundaries; the duplicate-code aspect is maintenance risk, not a correctness failure of active research results.

findings_verified.title: naive_always_carry baseline is byte-identical to buy_and_hold — the promised daily-rebalance tcost never accrues
findings_verified.detail: imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/online/backtest.py: naive_always_carry (lines 202-215) is functionally identical to buy_and_hold (lines 189-199) — both pass a constant weight-1.0 DataFrame to _simulate, whose cost term dw = w.diff().abs() (line 105) is zero after day 0. The docstrings (module lines 17-21 and function line 205) claim daily rebalancing makes tcost accrue, but the simulator never models weight drift between rebalances, so no cost can ever show up; the "fair tcost baseline" does not exist. The function is also uncalled anywhere in the repo. Fix: either drift weights within _simulate (w_drifted = w_prev*(1+r)/portfolio_growth) so daily rebalancing produces turnover, or delete naive_always_carry and correct the docstring.
findings_verified.evidence:
w = pd.DataFrame(0.0, index=asset_returns.index, columns=[asset])
