w[asset] = 1.0  # constant => dw == 0 => zero daily cost
findings_verified.suggestion: Either model weight drift (w_pre = w_prev*(1+r)/(1+r_p)) so daily rebalancing incurs turnover, or delete the redundant baseline and fix the docstring.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is accurate. naive_always_carry (backtest.py:202-215) builds exactly the same constant weight frame as buy_and_hold (lines 189-199): `w = pd.DataFrame(0.0, ...); w[asset] = 1.0`. In _simulate, cost is charged only on weight changes: `dw = w.diff().abs().sum(axis=1).fillna(w.iloc[0].abs().sum())` (line 105), which is nonzero only on day 0 for a constant weight series. Since _simulate never models weight drift from returns between rebalances, a "rebalanced daily" baseline with target weight 1.0 genuinely incurs zero simulated turnover — so the docstring's promise (lines 19-21: "rebalanced to 100% SVXY every step (so tcost shows up...)") is impossible under this cost model, and the two baselines return identical metrics. Both the module docstring and the function docstring ("fair tcost comparison") misdescribe the behavior. Mitigating factors keeping this minor: grep shows no callers of naive_always_carry anywhere in the repo (only its own definition and docstring), the code lives in imported/market-dynamics (a related side project, not the canonical packages/ or the active YH speculation-game line), and the defect only makes a null baseline redundant rather than corrupting strategy results.

findings_verified.title: The money-simulation layer (walk_forward_strategy, _simulate, cost model) has zero test coverage
findings_verified.detail: tests/test_online.py covers monitor, state machine, and strategy weight functions, but nothing imports state_atlas.online.backtest: no test exercises _simulate's return/cost alignment (w_t x r_{t+1}, cost on dw), the overlapping-window dedup logic, the counter-reset behavior (finding #2), or the baselines. online/__init__.py doesn't even export the backtest functions. For the only component that converts states into P&L numbers, an alignment off-by-one here would silently inflate every reported Sharpe, and nothing would catch it.
findings_verified.evidence: grep walk_forward|_simulate|online.backtest tests/ -> no matches
findings_verified.suggestion: Add tests with hand-computable 3-day weight/return fixtures asserting exact daily returns and costs, plus a leakage canary (corrupt returns after t must not change weights at <= t).
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified by direct inspection: (1) imported/market-dynamics/tests/test_online.py imports only state_atlas.online.monitor, .state_machine, and .strategy — nothing from state_atlas.online.backtest; (2) the workspace tests/ suite has zero matches for backtest/walk_forward/_simulate; (3) tests/test_backtest.py in the imported repo tests the Phase 7 module state_atlas.backtest (single_split_backtest), which is a different file from state_atlas/online/backtest.py (Phase 8 walk-forward); (4) the CLI `backtest` command (state_atlas/cli.py:188-203) also dispatches to the Phase 7 module, so no smoke test touches the online walk-forward path either; (5) state_atlas/online/__init__.py mentions backtest only in its docstring and exports nothing from it. No caller or test anywhere in the repo exercises walk_forward_strategy/_simulate, so its return/cost alignment and dedup/counter-reset logic are entirely unverified. The finding is accurate. Severity minor is calibrated: it is a test-coverage gap (not a demonstrated wrong result) in an imported related project, not in the canonical packages/ or the active speculation-game research line.

findings_verified.title: end=None cache key freezes whatever intraday partial bar was fetched first that day; cache_dir is CWD-relative
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as written. cache_key (imported/market-dynamics/state_atlas/data/cache.py:28) resolves end=None to local dt.date.today(); config.yaml sets end: null by default, so intraday first runs cache a still-moving partial bar that later same-day runs silently reuse, making results depend on the time of the first fetch and irreproducible after close. load() has no staleness/TTL check; only the manual --force-refresh flag bypasses it. cache_dir defaults to relative "artifacts/data" (state_atlas/config.py:23), so cache location and hence network behavior depend on the CWD of CLI invocation.
findings_verified.evidence: end_resolved = end if end is not None else dt.date.today().isoformat()
findings_verified.suggestion: Drop the last (possibly partial) bar when end is None, or key by the last complete trading session; resolve cache_dir against the config file location.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim reads the code correctly and the path is the default configuration. cache.py:28 (`end_resolved = end if end is not None else dt.date.today().isoformat()`) hashes the local-timezone calendar date into the cache key. config.yaml explicitly sets `end: null` ("null = today, yfinance resolves at fetch time"), so this is the default execution path via state_atlas/data/__init__.py:40, where the cached parquet is loaded unless --force-refresh is passed (cli.py:36). Therefore the first fetch of a trading day during market hours caches a partial last bar, and all subsequent same-day runs silently reuse it; results depend on the wall-clock time of the first run and are not reproducible after the close (a fresh cache the next day yields a different final bar). No guard exists: save() never records fetch time in the key, load() does no staleness check, and the module docstring says old files are never deleted. Tests (test_data.py) use mocked fetchers with fixed start/end and tmp_path cache dirs, so they neither exercise nor mitigate the end=None path. The cache_dir claim is also accurate: DataConfig.cache_dir defaults to the relative string "artifacts/data" (config.py:23, config.yaml:11) and cache_paths/save just do Path(cache_dir), so cache hits and network fetches depend on the invoking CWD. Severity: minor is correctly calibrated — this is in imported/market-dynamics (a related side project, not the canonical packages/ or the active YH speculation-game line), the error affects at most the last partial bar of a multi-year daily series, and --force-refresh provides a manual workaround.

findings_verified.title: Walk-forward loop silently skips the final partial test window (up to step_days-1 most recent dates get no weights); test_end=min(...) is dead code
findings_verified.detail: The loop condition `while start + bt_cfg.test_days <= len(idx)` requires a full 252-day test window, yet inside the loop `test_end = min(start + bt_cfg.test_days, len(idx))` implies partial windows were intended — the min() can never bind under the loop condition. Consequently the most recent tail of the data (up to step_days-1 = 62 trading days) receives no weights and is dropped from the equity curve by the index intersection in _simulate, i.e. the backtest quietly ends months before the data does — the most decision-relevant period for a live monitor.
findings_verified.suggestion: Loosen the condition to `while start < len(idx)` (train window still full-length) so the final partial test window is emitted, or document the truncation.
findings_verified.verify_note: Line 144's loop condition requires a full test window, so line 146's min(start + test_days, len(idx)) can never bind — dead code that shows partial windows were intended. Because step_days=63 < test_days=252, when the last full window no longer fits, the remaining tail (up to step_days-1 = 62 trading days) is never assigned weights; _simulate's index intersection (line 94) then drops those dates from the equity curve, so the backtest ends before the data does. No caller or test mitigates this. Severity minor is appropriate: it is in imported/market-dynamics (a related but non-canonical project), does not corrupt results within the covered range, and only truncates the most recent ≤62 days of a paper backtest.

findings_verified.title: clear_uniform tie-break accumulates pairwise midpoints: 3+ tied max-volume prices yield a price outside the documented 'midpoint' rule
findings_verified.detail: book.py line 53-54 accumulates pairwise midpoints on ties: with 3+ tied max-volume candidate prices the returned clearing price is order-dependent and outside the documented tie-range midpoint (reproduced: prices 99/100/101 tied at volume 5 → returns 100.25 instead of 100). Unreached by engine.py (which implements batch clearing independently); only caller is tests/test_clearing.py, which tests no tie case — so the D5c 'independent verification' layer silently carries a wrong tie rule. continuous_match and the spec'd OrderBook/Fill API (data-model.md:26) are unused/unimplemented (docs drift).
findings_verified.evidence: elif abs(vol - best_vol) <= 1e-12 and best_p is not None and vol > 0:\n    best_p = (best_p + p) / 2.0  # tie: midpoint
findings_verified.suggestion: Track (lo, hi) of the tied max-volume range and return (lo+hi)/2; add a 3-way-tie test. Update or prune the data-model OrderBook section to match the functional implementation.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified by reading imported/ABM-Microstructure/src/microstructure/book.py:48-54 and by executing the function in isolation. The tie branch (line 53-54: `best_p = (best_p + p) / 2.0`) folds each new tied price into a pairwise midpoint iteratively rather than taking the midpoint of the tied range. Concrete reproduction: bids [BUY 101 x5, BUY 100 x0] vs asks [SELL 99 x5] produces three candidate prices 99/100/101 all with matched volume 5; clear_uniform returns (100.25, 5) — ((99+100)/2 + 101)/2 — while the tied-range midpoint (and the docstring's 'tie 時は中点' promise) is 100.0. With only two tied prices it correctly returns 100.0, so the defect manifests only with 3+ tied max-volume prices. Reachability/impact checks match the auditor's claims: clear_uniform is called only from tests/test_clearing.py, whose tie coverage is absent (test_clearing_handcalc has a unique maximum at p=100); engine.py never imports book.py's clearing (it implements batch clearing itself), so no simulation results are wrong. The function is billed (module docstring and test file header) as the engine-independent D5c verification of the clearing layer, so the bug weakens the anchor battery but corrupts no research output. The docs-drift side note also checks out: continuous_match is defined but never called anywhere, and the OrderBook/match_continuous/Fill API in specs/001-exp-a-clob-harness/data-model.md:26 was never implemented. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated: genuine bug, contained to an imported (non-canonical) repo's test-only verification helper.

findings_verified.title: Metrics field comment defines participation_margin as mm_net_pnl - opp_cost*T, contradicting the spec and the actual computation
findings_verified.detail: imported/ABM-Microstructure/src/microstructure/metrics.py:14 documents participation_margin as "mm_net_pnl - opp_cost*T（US3, D9）", i.e. including noise spread revenue. The actual computation (engine.py _assemble: participation_margin = fees - extraction - cfg.opp_cost * cfg.horizon, comment "spread 収入は含めない") and the spec/CLAUDE.md invariant ("f·noise量 − sniping − c") deliberately exclude spread income. The comment overstates the margin by noise_pnl; computed values are correct, so this is documentation drift only.
findings_verified.evidence: participation_margin: float  # mm_net_pnl - opp_cost*T (US3, D9)  [metrics.py:14] vs engine.py:134: participation_margin = fees - extraction - cfg.opp_cost * cfg.horizon
findings_verified.suggestion: Fix the comment to 'fees - extraction - opp_cost*T (excludes spread income; see engine._assemble / FR-008)'.
findings_verified.verify_note: metrics.py:14 annotates participation_margin as "mm_net_pnl - opp_cost*T", but the actual computation in engine.py (_assemble) is participation_margin = fees - extraction - cfg.opp_cost * cfg.horizon, with an inline comment explicitly stating spread income is NOT included. Since mm_net_pnl = (noise_pnl - extraction) + fees, the field comment's formula differs from the real one by +noise_pnl. The spec ground truth agrees with the code: imported/ABM-Microstructure/CLAUDE.md M1 invariants define the margin as "f·noise量 − sniping − c". So the dataclass comment is genuinely wrong documentation; the computed values themselves are correct, making this docs-drift only. Severity minor is calibrated: no runtime code consumes the comment, the imported repo is not the canonical active research line, and anyone reading engine.py or the spec gets the right formula — but a downstream re-derivation from the Metrics docstring alone would inflate the margin by noise_pnl and misplace exit regions.

findings_verified.title: BudgetLedger writes the snapshot before appending to the journal, so a crash inside charge() silently drops the event from the 'primary' append-only ledger
findings_verified.detail: The 0002b fix declares the journal the ground truth and rebuild_spent() folds it. But charge(), refund(), and reconcile() all mutate self.data, call _save() (snapshot write), and only then _journal_append(). A process killed between the two writes leaves the snapshot ahead of the journal: verify() will flag the mismatch, but rebuild_spent() - the designated recovery path - resolves it by UNDER-counting spent, i.e., the budget cap can be silently exceeded by exactly the periods of the lost charge event. Journal-first ordering makes the failure mode conservative (journal >= snapshot) and is what an append-only WAL discipline implies.
findings_verified.evidence: self.data["spent"][tier] = spent + int(periods)\nself._save()\nself._journal_append({"op": "charge", ...})  # (designmap.py:191-193; same order in refund:198-200 and reconcile:213-216)
findings_verified.suggestion: Append to the journal before writing the snapshot in all mutating ops (and fsync the journal if crash-hardness matters); document that verify()==False should be resolved journal-first only when journal >= snapshot.
findings_verified.verify_note: The cited code behaves exactly as claimed. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/ABM-Microstructure/src/microstructure/designmap.py, charge() does `self.data["spent"][tier] = spent + int(periods)` then `self._save()` (snapshot write, line 192) and only afterwards `self._journal_append({"op": "charge", ...})` (line 193); refund() (lines 198-200) and reconcile() (lines 213-216) use the same snapshot-first order, as does the refusal branch (185-187) and audit() (223-224). The design doc docs/findings/0002b-incident-ledger-snapshot.md explicitly promotes the journal to the ledger ("台帳とは追記ログのことである") and demotes the snapshot to a cache, with rebuild_spent() (lines 159-172) as the fold used for recovery and the regression test covering "snapshot 破損→journal 復元". Nothing guards the write window: _save() is a plain write_text and _journal_append a plain append; a process killed between lines 192 and 193 leaves a charge in the snapshot but not the journal. verify() (line 176) would detect the divergence, but the designated recovery (rebuild_spent) would then under-count spent by the lost charge's periods, allowing later charges to exceed the cap — the exact conservative-direction violation the 0002b fix was meant to prevent (journal-first would instead over-count, which is safe for a budget cap). No caller or test enforces journal-first ordering or fsync. Severity: minor is correctly calibrated — the window is a few milliseconds per event, the ledger tracks compute budget (not scientific results), and this lives in an imported side project, not the canonical SG line; verify() at least surfaces the inconsistency even though recovery resolves it unsafely.

findings_verified.title: Hardcoded exact p-values are printed unconditionally, even when the test conditions (complete separation / all-positive diffs) do not hold
findings_verified.detail: The script prints 'exact Mann-Whitney two-sided p = 2/252' and 'sign test exact two-sided p = 2/2**5' regardless of whether sep (complete separation of 5+5) or all(d > 0) is actually True. These p-values are only valid for the extreme configurations; if a future recalculation (different calibration or seed count) yields sep=False, the log still displays a significant p next to it. Since this console output is the artifact backing the finding-0002 direction claim, a misleading line could propagate into the paper.
findings_verified.evidence: print(f"complete separation: {sep}  (exact Mann-Whitney two-sided p = 2/252 = 0.0079)")\n...\nprint(f"all paired diffs positive: {all(d > 0 for d in diffs)}  (sign test exact two-sided p = {2 / 2**5:.4f})")
findings_verified.suggestion: Compute the U statistic and exact p from the observed data (comb-based helper or scipy mannwhitneyu(method='exact')) and print p conditionally on the observed statistic; derive the sign-test p from the observed count.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves exactly as claimed. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/ABM-Microstructure/scripts/bcs_per_seed.py lines 46-49, the strings "exact Mann-Whitney two-sided p = 2/252 ≈ 0.0079" and "sign test exact two-sided p = 0.0625" (2/2**5) are hardcoded into f-strings and printed unconditionally, regardless of the computed booleans `sep = max(c) < min(v)` (line 44) and `all(d > 0 for d in diffs)` (line 48). These p-values are only valid for the extreme configurations (complete separation of 5+5 ranks; all 5 paired signs positive). If a recalculation under a changed calibration yields sep=False, the log would still juxtapose a significant p-value with the result. No test or caller guards this (the script is a standalone __main__ entry; nothing else in the repo imports it). Mitigating factors that keep severity low: (1) the actual boolean (True/False) is printed on the same line immediately before the p-value, so a False outcome is visible to a careful reader; (2) the script is deterministic (bit-identical re-runs per its docstring, D-B12), so under the current fixed calibration/seeds the printed booleans are True and the p-values are correct for the artifact actually backing finding-0002; (3) it lives in imported/ABM-Microstructure, not the canonical packages/ or the highest-priority speculation-game line. The defect only bites under a hypothetical future recalibration, and even then the False flag is co-printed. This is a genuine but low-impact hygiene issue: minor is the correct severity (arguably borderline info, but the console output is the stated evidentiary artifact for a research direction claim, which keeps it at minor).

findings_verified.title: compute_hill=False imputes HILL_ALPHA_CAP=20.0, fabricating a 'maximally thin tail' reading for MG/GCMG
findings_verified.detail: fingerprint.py:157 imputes HILL_ALPHA_CAP=20.0 for compute_hill=False series (MG/GCMG attendance excess), making "Hill imputed because inapplicable" bit-identical in the feature vector to "Hill measured and capped" (chiarella_iori) — confirmed by identical standardized hill centroids of 1.558 for minority_game/gcmg/chiarella_iori in notebooks/atlas_v4/gate.json ((20.0−8.604)/7.3147=1.558). The imputation itself is semantically defensible (bounded attendance series genuinely lack power-law tails, matching the cap's documented "thin tail verdict" role), and the suggested NaN+nansum alternative carries its own bias (unnormalized dimension dropping shrinks all MG/GCMG pair distances). The real, low-impact issues are: (a) capped-measured vs imputed readings are indistinguishable in all NN/novelty/inverse-ABM queries; (b) 24 imputed point-mass values (of 126 runs) shift the population mean/std used to standardize the hill feature for every model, mildly distorting the atlas geometry and separation statistics. hill_raw=NaN is stored as a diagnostic (populate.py:78) but never enters the distance computation. Affects only the exploratory fingerprint_atlas pipeline, not the canonical SG line; other feature axes still separate MG/GCMG from CI strongly.
findings_verified.evidence: hill = hill_tail_index(r) if compute_hill else HILL_ALPHA_CAP  (fingerprint.py:157); notebooks/atlas_v4/gate.json centroids: chiarella_iori hill=1.558, gcmg hill=1.558, minority_game hill=1.558 (identical)
findings_verified.suggestion: Return NaN for the hill feature when compute_hill=False (distance_matrix handles NaN via nansum), or add a per-run feature mask; then re-run the gate to see whether MG/GCMG separation survives without the fabricated axis.
findings_verified.verify_note: The cited behavior is real and reachable: fingerprint.py:157 substitutes HILL_ALPHA_CAP=20.0 whenever compute_hill=False, and every atlas populator (populate.py:74, propose_cli.py:239) passes compute_hill=(kind=="returns") with adapters.py routing MG/GCMG through kind="attendance_excess". The committed notebooks/atlas_v4/gate.json confirms the consequence exactly as the auditor described: minority_game, gcmg, and chiarella_iori all sit at standardized hill 1.558, which is precisely (20.0−8.604)/7.3147 — imputed values and genuinely-capped values are indistinguishable, and nothing downstream (distance_matrix, standardize, propose.py kNN, preference.py novelty) distinguishes them; hill_raw is stored as a diagnostic only (populate.py:75-78) and never enters the geometry. So the finding cannot be refuted. But the severity is miscalibrated. (1) The "fabricated maximally thin tail" framing overstates the error: attendance excess is a bounded series, and "no power-law tail" is the mathematically correct verdict for bounded support, consistent with the cap's documented semantics ("anything above this is a thin-tail verdict", lines 51-54) — the imputation is a defensible, deliberate encoding, not a silent bug. (2) The proposed remedy (NaN + np.nansum) is itself biased: nansum drops the dimension without renormalizing, shrinking MG/GCMG distances to ALL models, including fat-tailed ones, so it is not clearly "less biased". (3) The genuine residual defects are narrower: measured-at-cap vs imputed are conflated, and 24 imputed point-mass values out of 126 runs distort the population mean/std used to z-score every other model's hill feature. (4) Impact is confined to the exploratory fingerprint_atlas pipeline (atlas/novelty/inverse-ABM geometry), not the canonical SG research line, and MG/GCMG remain strongly separated from chiarella_iori on other axes (e.g. gcmg volatility z=+2.17 vs CI −0.49), so 1-NN outcomes are not driven by the hill axis alone. Genuine, low-impact, partially-by-design: minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: 00_sanity_check.jpynb is a 0-byte file with a misspelled extension, referenced nowhere
findings_verified.detail: The file is empty (0 bytes) — not valid notebook JSON — has the extension .jpynb instead of .ipynb (so Jupyter/nbconvert/CI notebook globs would never pick it up even if populated), and nothing in the repo references it (grep for 'sanity_check' and 'jpynb' across the tree returns nothing). It has been an empty placeholder since first_commit. Its presence implies a sanity-check notebook exists when none does.
findings_verified.evidence: -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Jun 26 10:50 00_sanity_check.jpynb; grep -r 'sanity_check|jpynb' -> no matches
findings_verified.suggestion: Delete it, or replace it with a real 00_sanity_check.ipynb that imports the packages and runs the fast REGISTRY smoke path.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified directly: /home/user/financial-abm-lab/notebooks/00_sanity_check.jpynb is 0 bytes (ls -la shows size 0), carries the misspelled extension .jpynb (Jupyter/nbconvert/*.ipynb globs would not match it), and no project file references 'sanity_check' or 'jpynb' (grep across .py/.md/.toml/.yml/.json excluding vendored .venv returns nothing; the only raw-grep hits are binary/vendored site-packages false positives). git log --follow shows it has existed unchanged since first_commit. All factual claims in the finding hold. It is a pure hygiene issue — a dead empty placeholder that misleadingly implies a sanity-check notebook exists — with no runtime or correctness impact, so 'minor' severity is correctly calibrated (arguably could be 'info').

findings_verified.title: Stale pre-monorepo path ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db in populate.py usage docs and CI PR-body instructions
findings_verified.detail: Stale pre-monorepo DB path `../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db` survives in populate.py's usage docstring (lines 11-13), in db.py's module docstring (line 1), and in the PR body template of propose.yml (line 102), while CI actually uses `data/abm_knowhow.db`. Every weekly proposal PR therefore tells the reviewer to run the execute command against a nonexistent path. Docs-drift only; runtime code takes --db explicitly and is unaffected.
findings_verified.evidence: populate.py:11-13: 'uv run python -m fingerprint_atlas.populate --db ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db'; propose.yml:101-103: '--db ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db execute <id>'
findings_verified.suggestion: Update both to the monorepo-canonical DB location (data/abm_knowhow.db) and consider centralizing the default in one constant.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified both cited locations. packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/populate.py:11-13 documents the canonical invocation as `--db ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db`, and .github/workflows/propose.yml:101-102 embeds the same path in the reviewer-facing PR body ("To run an approved proposal end-to-end ... --db ../test/knowhow/abm_knowhow.db execute <id>"). No `test/` (or `../test/knowhow`) directory exists anywhere in the tree; the workflows themselves use DB_PATH=data/abm_knowhow.db (propose.yml:31, ingest_arxiv.yml:34). So the docstring and every weekly proposal PR instruct running against a nonexistent path. The same stale path also appears in packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/db.py:1. Impact is limited to docs/instructions — the CLI takes --db explicitly and CI passes the correct path — so minor is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: No test exercises the gate against a diverged/outlier population
findings_verified.detail: tests/test_fingerprint_atlas.py (per commit f014e57) verifies the gate 'recognises a separable toy population' — a positive-path test only. The failure mode that actually occurred in production (one exploded run collapsing a feature's standardization while the gate still returns ok:true, as in atlas_v3) has no regression test, so the gate can fail open again after future feature additions. A test injecting one run with a 1e10-scale feature into an otherwise separable population and asserting the gate flags it (once the guard exists) would pin this down.
findings_verified.evidence: validation_gate ok criterion (atlas.py:86) passed on the atlas_v3 population containing a volatility-5e8-scale run; test suite only checks a clean separable toy population
findings_verified.suggestion: Add an adversarial gate test with a single diverged run and assert either ok:false or an explicit per-feature warning in the gate dict.
findings_verified.verify_note: The claim is a testing-gap finding and it is accurate. (1) tests/test_fingerprint_atlas.py contains exactly one gate test, test_validation_gate_recognises_separation (lines 68-85), which builds a clean separable toy population and asserts ok is True — no negative-path/outlier test exists anywhere (grep for validation_gate hits only this test and the package itself). (2) The code path has no defense against the described failure mode: collect_population (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/db.py:475-489) only drops non-finite fingerprints, so a finite 1e10-scale feature passes through; standardize (fingerprint.py:178-190) z-scores with population std, so one exploded run inflates that feature's sd and collapses it to near-zero for every other run; the ok criterion at atlas.py:86 ("np.isfinite(sil) and sil > 0 and inter > intra") can still hold because the remaining features may keep the models separated, so the gate fails open exactly as described. I could not locate the atlas_v3 artifact in the repo to verify the specific production incident (the "volatility 5e8" evidence), but the code mechanism it describes is real and untested, which is the substance of the finding. Severity minor is correctly calibrated: it is a test-coverage gap in a non-canonical analysis package, not wrong results in active research code.

findings_verified.title: Round-trip dG in the LOB port is marked at submit-time cognitive price (mid(t-1) image), excluding own-step impact — undocumented divergence from YH005/Java
findings_verified.detail: YH005 and the Java reference record entry_price and settle at the cognitive price P AFTER the current step's price update, so a round trip's dG includes the market impact of the agent's own opening and closing orders (Sample.java calls openPrices()/update() after cognitivePrice += move-2; reference.py sets entry_price = market.P after step 4). YH006 instead sets entry_price_cog = P_now and close_price_cog = P_now at submission time, where SharedHistoryState.advance_to has only incorporated Δmid(t-1) = P_mid(t-1) − P_mid(t-2); the fill then happens during step t. So dG = entry_action * (close_price_cog − entry_price_cog) spans a window shifted ~one step earlier and systematically excludes the adverse own-impact of the close order that YH005/Java include. This changes the ΔG-vs-horizon statistics and the wealth-feedback loop that the 2×2 aggregate-vs-LOB comparison is designed to measure, yet neither speculation_agent.py's docstring nor the adapter/parity docs mention the shift (history_broadcast.py documents the μ lag as equivalent, which is correct, but the entry/exit marking convention is a separate divergence).
findings_verified.evidence:
self.entry_action = rec
self.entry_price_cog = P_now
self.entry_step = t
... (close path) ...
self.close_price_cog = P_now  # vs YH005: entry_price = market.P AFTER step-4 P += h
findings_verified.suggestion: Either evaluate entry/close cognitive prices at reconcile time (next step's advanced P, which includes the execution step's move, matching YH005's inclusion of own-step impact), or document the one-step-early marking convention explicitly wherever aggregate-vs-LOB ΔG/round-trip statistics are compared.
findings_verified.verify_note: Code behaves exactly as claimed. YH006 speculation_agent.py sets entry_price_cog = P_now (line 264) and close_price_cog = P_now (line 289) at submit time, where SharedHistoryState.advance_to (history_broadcast.py:69-83) has only accumulated quantized diffs through Δmid(t-1)−Δmid(t-2); dG is computed from these submit-time prices at next-step reconcile (line 345). YH005 simulate.py by contrast does P += h (line 121, h quantizing the CURRENT step's aggregate move including the agent's own order) before settling closes (line 130) and marking opens (line 151), so YH005/Java dG includes own-step impact of both legs while YH006 excludes it — a genuinely shifted window. The divergence is undocumented: YH006 SPEC.md §3.1/3.2 only says "open で entry_price 固定 / ΔG は cognitive price ベース"; history_broadcast.py documents only the μ lag; nothing in the adapter or docs/backbone_parity.md covers the marking convention (and backbone_parity.md makes no YH006 parity claim, so no stronger claim is falsified). Severity minor is correct: YH006 is an explicit LOB translation with already-accepted sources of numeric divergence (partial fills, self-cancels, lagged shared history), the effect is a one-step boundary shift per round trip (own impact still enters P with lag and affects subsequent trades), and no document asserts YH005≡YH006 numerics — so it is an undocumented convention divergence that risks misinterpreting ΔG-vs-horizon comparisons, not wrong results in canonical code.

findings_verified.title: PRISM 'sg' adapter is a Franke-Westerhoff-type model misattributed to Katahira et al. (2019) in docstring, README, and complexity metadata — confirmed not imported as the real SG anywhere canonical
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed: the file implements a fundamentalist/chartist/noise weighted-demand model with softmax (discrete-choice) strategy switching — structurally FW/Brock-Hommes, sharing nothing with the Speculation Game (no strategies over base-5 history, no round-trip wealth, no bankruptcy). Yet the docstring calls it "Speculation Game (SG) adapter — Katahira et al. (2019) variant", describe_complexity() repeats "(Katahira et al. 2019)" (line 144), and imported/PRISM/README.md:100 lists `sg` as "Speculation Game | Katahira et al. (2019)". Import audit: SGAdapter is only used by PRISM's own pipeline.py/tests and by imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/models/sg.py (a byte-identical copy differing only in the types import, verified by diff); packages/fingerprint_atlas builds "speculation_game" from abm_models.REGISTRY (the true YH005-derived SG), packages/abm_models/sg/__init__.py explicitly warns about the name clash, and ADR 0001 documents it. So no canonical code mistakes it for the real SG — the residual risk is citation drift: PRISM's README/metadata would propagate a false Katahira attribution if PRISM results are ever quoted, and the arXiv/LLM fingerprint_atlas literature pipeline could ingest the mislabeled description.
findings_verified.evidence:
"""Speculation Game (SG) adapter — Katahira et al. (2019) variant.

A minimal heterogeneous-agent model with fundamentalists, chartists,
and noise traders..."""  (+ README.md:100: '| `sg` | Speculation Game | Katahira et al. (2019) | 7 | ...')
findings_verified.suggestion: Even under a no-touch policy for imported/, add a one-line correction to the repo-level docs (ADR 0001 already exists) noting that PRISM README/describe_complexity carry the false attribution; if imported/PRISM is ever edited, rename the adapter's docstring/reference to Franke-Westerhoff-variant.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all three claims. (1) The code at imported/PRISM/src/prism/adapters/sg.py:149-202 implements aggregate fundamentalist/chartist/noise weighted demand (lines 164-180) with softmax/discrete-choice strategy-fraction switching (lines 194-200) — structurally Franke-Westerhoff/Brock-Hommes. It has none of the real Speculation Game's mechanics (no base-5 history strategies, no per-agent round-trip wealth accounting, no bankruptcy/entry). (2) The misattribution is real: docstring line 1 ("Speculation Game (SG) adapter — Katahira et al. (2019) variant"), describe_complexity() line 144 ("(Katahira et al. 2019)"), README.md:100 and CHANGELOG.md:9 all cite Katahira 2019. (3) The mitigation claims also check out: packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/__init__.py:7 explicitly warns the PRISM/PROV-ABM "sg.py" is a different model, and docs/adr/0001-canonical-sg-is-yh005.md documents the name misuse and byte-identity of the PRISM/PROV-ABM copies; SGAdapter is only referenced within PRISM and the PROV-ABM-atlas copy, never by packages/ or experiments/. Notably PRISM's own docs/AUDIT_REPORT.md:96 already flags the gap, but README/docstring/metadata inside PRISM remain misattributed, so the citation-drift risk stands. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated: PRISM is a retired lineage, no canonical code or results depend on the mislabel, and the canonical layer already documents the clash — this is pure docs-drift within an imported repo.

findings_verified.title: Paired condition differences aligned by array position, not by seed — misaligned pairs whenever trials are missing or NaN
findings_verified.detail: Latent seed-misalignment bug, confirmed in code but verified not to have affected any produced result. lob_diff_bootstrap (aggregate_full_summary.py:148-154), compute_interactions (:304-316), and compute_a1_interactions/compute_shrinkage (aggregate_ablation_summary.py) build paired per-trial differences positionally from per-condition `dropna()` arrays truncated to `min(...)`, never using the df's seed column; upstream aggregate_ensemble.py:220-222 silently skips missing trial parquets. Any missing trial or metric-level NaN in one condition would silently pair seed s with seed s' != s, corrupting the paired interaction estimates and bootstrap CIs. Verified against the on-disk data, though: ensemble_summary.parquet has exactly 100 rows per condition (C0u/C0p/C2/C3/C2_A1/C3_A1), seeds exactly 1000-1099 in order, zero NaNs in all five interaction metrics, and all trial parquets present — so positional pairing matched seed pairing for every published YH006_1 result. The defect only bites on future reruns/extensions with a failed trial or degenerate NaN (0/600 trials so far). Recommended fix: pivot on seed (e.g. df.pivot(index='seed', columns='cond', values=metric).dropna()) or assert per-condition seed lists are identical before differencing.
findings_verified.evidence:
n = min(c2.size, c3.size)
    diffs = c3[:n] - c2[:n]
...
agg_diff = c0p[:n] - c0u[:n]
        lob_diff = c3[:n] - c2[:n]
        inter = lob_diff - agg_diff
findings_verified.suggestion: Pivot the summary df on seed (e.g. df.pivot(index='seed', columns='cond', values=metric)), compute differences on the joined rows, and assert no NaN/missing seeds before bootstrapping. Fail loudly if the per-condition seed sets differ.
findings_verified.verify_note:
The code behaves exactly as claimed. In /home/user/financial-abm-lab/imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/aggregate_full_summary.py, lob_diff_bootstrap (lines 148-153: `c2 = df[df.cond=='C2'][metric].dropna()...; n = min(c2.size, c3.size); diffs = c3[:n] - c2[:n]`) and compute_interactions (lines 304-316), plus compute_a1_interactions and compute_shrinkage in aggregate_ablation_summary.py (same `dropna()` + `min(...)` + positional-slice pattern), pair trials purely by array position. Upstream, aggregate_ensemble.py's aggregate_ensemble_summaries (lines 215-224) silently `continue`s past missing trial parquets (logged but not fatal), and per-metric `dropna()` removes rows independently per condition — so any single missing trial or metric NaN in one condition would silently shift all subsequent pairings to mismatched seeds. The df carries a `seed` column that is never used for alignment, and no test or assertion guards row-count/seed parity. So the defect is real and not mitigated in code.

However, I verified the actual data the published results were computed from: data/ensemble_summary.parquet contains exactly 100 rows per condition (C0u, C0p, C2, C3, C2_A1, C3_A1), each with seeds exactly 1000-1099 in sorted order, and zero NaNs in all five INTERACTION_METRICS (rho_pearson, rho_spearman, tau_kendall, bin_var_slope, q90_q10_slope_diff), and all four main conditions have all 100 trial parquets on disk. Positional pairing therefore coincided with seed pairing for every produced result — the YH006_1 headline interaction estimates and CIs currently in the README/tables are NOT corrupted. The bug is latent: it fires only on a future rerun/extension with a failed trial or a degenerate-trial NaN (0 occurrences across 600 trials so far). Given the defect exists but demonstrably did not bite the recorded results, and the failure precondition has never occurred in practice, "major" overstates it; this is a genuine but currently non-impactful robustness defect in active research code, so minor (with the caveat that a fix — merge on seed or assert seed alignment — is cheap and worthwhile).

findings_verified.title: CI/ZI/FW canonical cores keep PRISM's `default_rng(seed + i)` consecutive-seed path streams plus cross-path return averaging — latent ensemble trap
findings_verified.detail: simulate(seed, n_paths) in chiarella_iori/model.py:131, franke_westerhoff/model.py:127, and zero_intelligence/model.py:108 seeds path i with default_rng(seed+i) and then returns np.mean(all_returns, axis=0). Two problems: (1) consecutive-integer seeds mean simulate(seed=42) and simulate(seed=43) share n_paths-1 identical path streams, so any sweep over adjacent base seeds produces almost entirely overlapping ensembles; (2) averaging return paths across independent runs is CLT smoothing that destroys the fat tails and volatility clustering these models exist to produce. Currently harmless in this monorepo because the ABMModel wrapper (chiarella_iori/__init__.py:28-30) calls simulate(seed=seed, n_paths=1), but the flawed multi-path API is the public surface of the canonical package and will silently bite the first caller who passes n_paths>1.
findings_verified.evidence:
for i in range(n_paths):
            rng_i = np.random.default_rng(seed + i)
            returns = self._simulate_one_path(rng_i)
            all_returns.append(returns)
        avg_returns = np.mean(all_returns, axis=0)
findings_verified.suggestion: Use np.random.SeedSequence(seed).spawn(n_paths) for path streams, and either return the stack of paths or concatenate them; never average return paths. At minimum document that n_paths>1 output is not a return series.
findings_verified.verify_note: Verified all three cited sites: packages/abm_models/abm_models/chiarella_iori/model.py:130-135, franke_westerhoff/model.py:126ff, and zero_intelligence/model.py:107ff each seed path i with np.random.default_rng(seed + i) and return np.mean(all_returns, axis=0). Both claimed problems are real: (1) simulate(seed=42, n_paths=N) and simulate(seed=43, n_paths=N) share N-1 identical path streams (consecutive-seed overlap), and (2) element-wise averaging of independent return paths is CLT smoothing that suppresses the fat tails / volatility clustering these stylized-facts models are meant to exhibit — the correct semantics would be to return per-path returns or concatenate, not average. The mitigation claim also checks out: the ABMModel wrappers (chiarella_iori/__init__.py:29, franke_westerhoff/__init__.py:29, zero_intelligence/__init__.py:35) always call simulate(seed=seed, n_paths=1), and the only in-repo n_paths>1 callers are inside the retired imported/PRISM lineage (its own tests/docs), not the canonical packages/experiments. So no current canonical result is wrong, but the flawed multi-path API is the public surface of the canonical package and would silently corrupt the first n_paths>1 analysis. Severity minor is correctly calibrated (latent, no active caller hits it).

findings_verified.title: Hardcoded default_rng(0) fallback re-instantiated per call reuses identical bootstrap resample matrices across metrics and conditions
findings_verified.detail: Confirmed as reported, with one nuance: at the loop-based call sites (aggregate_full_summary.py:333/477, aggregate_ensemble.py:322/426, aggregate_ablation_summary.py:150/188) a single default_rng(0) is reused across the loop so resample matrices differ within that loop; the perfect bit-identical duplication occurs at the per-call sites (subsample_aggregate.py:318-319, survival_extension.py:261, survival_analysis.py:331, and any bare bootstrap_ci/bootstrap_interaction_ci call with rng=None) and across the first draws of every function/script that restarts at seed 0. YH008's shared-bseed pattern (run_all.py:199-203, run_p0.py:72-80, finalize_faithfulness.py:71-73) is confirmed as well.
findings_verified.evidence:
if rng is None:
        rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
    ...
    boot = rng.choice(arr, size=(n_resample, arr.size), replace=True).mean(axis=1)
findings_verified.suggestion: Create one seeded Generator (or SeedSequence.spawn children) at the top of each aggregation script and thread it through every bootstrap call, instead of a shared default seed 0 constant.
findings_verified.verify_note: stats.py:22-23 and 47-48 do fall back to a fresh np.random.default_rng(0) per call. subsample_aggregate.py:313-319 (trial_level_ci), survival_extension.py:261, and survival_analysis.py:331 instantiate default_rng(0) on every call, so bootstrap resample index matrices are bit-identical across metrics/conditions whenever input arrays have the same length (100 trials/condition). The remaining cited sites (aggregate_full_summary.py:147/301/333/477, aggregate_ablation_summary.py:150/188, aggregate_ensemble.py:322/426) share one generator within a loop (stream advances there) but every function restarts at seed 0, so cross-function/script bootstrap noise is still correlated. YH008 claim also verified: run_all.py:199-203 (and run_p0.py:72-80, finalize_faithfulness.py:71-73) pass the same bseed into marginal_disposition, paired_disposition, and paired_ath_asymmetry, making the first bootstrap stream inside each identical. No guard or test decorrelates these. Severity minor is correct: each CI is individually deterministic and unbiased; only shared MC noise across CIs is affected.

findings_verified.title: The provenance core records no seed, git commit, or config hash — cannot reproduce the runs it documents
findings_verified.detail: emit_prov (packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py:23-59), the sole public API of the provenance package, emits PROV-O records with no seed, git_commit, config_hash, or environment fields — fields the repo's own conventions require (README.md:57; PROV-ABM-atlas L2 minimum). The entity_in/entity_out dicts are free-form so callers could embed these, but nothing enforces or documents that, and the docstring pins the schema to backward compatibility with capital-allocation. In-repo the function is only called from tests; fingerprint_atlas records git_commit/host/seed independently. A design/convention gap in the canonical package rather than a correctness bug: records it produces cannot re-seed the runs they document unless callers volunteer that data.
findings_verified.suggestion: Extend emit_prov (backward-compatibly) with optional seed, git_commit, config_hash, and env fields, and have experiments/ wrappers pass them; align with the L2 provenance schema already specified in PROV-ABM-atlas.
findings_verified.verify_note: The factual claim is accurate. packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py:23-59 (emit_prov, the module's entire public API per __all__) builds a record containing only activity id, startedAtTime, entity_in/entity_out dicts, used/wasAssociatedWith/wasGeneratedBy links, and agent name. There is no field, parameter, or docstring guidance for RNG seed, git commit, config hash, or environment. README.md:55-57 indeed demands run tables with run_id/timestamp/git_commit/config_path/seed, and fingerprint_atlas's populate.py does record git_commit/host/seed on its own, confirming the canonical package is weaker than its satellite consumer. Within this repo emit_prov is only exercised by tests (tests/test_provenance.py), and the docstring says the record structure is deliberately backward-compatible with capital-allocation's prov_record.py — so it is a conscious design carryover, not an accidental bug, and callers could in principle stuff seed/config into the free-form entity_in dict. That mitigation is possible but not enforced or documented, so the structural gap stands. It causes no wrong results in canonical research code (models run fine; other components self-record), so "minor" is the right calibration.

findings_verified.title: Run seeds and LHS parameter draws depend on which models are selected and their registry order — same (model, sample) is not reproducible across invocations
findings_verified.detail: populate() assigns each run seed = seed_base + n_total where n_total counts across all chosen models, and draws each model's LHS parameter samples from one shared Generator sequentially (line 56 rng = default_rng(rng_seed); line 66 samples = sample_params_lhs(name, n_per_model, rng)). Consequently: running `--models sg` alone gives sg seeds 1000-1011 and the first LHS draws, while a full-registry run gives sg different seeds and different parameter samples depending on its position in REGISTRY; adding a model to REGISTRY re-randomizes every later model's rows. Individual DB rows do record their seed and params, so stored results remain auditable, but the population procedure itself is not reproducible per model, and re-running a subset to 'append' produces rows that cannot match a prior full run. populate_refs.py:44-54 has the identical structure.
findings_verified.suggestion: Derive per-model streams and seeds deterministically from (rng_seed, model_name), e.g. default_rng(SeedSequence([rng_seed, hash(name)])) and seed = f(seed_base, name, i), so a model's rows are invariant to subset selection and registry growth.
findings_verified.verify_note: Read populate.py in full: line 54 builds `chosen` from --models or full REGISTRY order; line 56 creates a single shared np.random.default_rng(rng_seed); line 66 draws each model's LHS samples sequentially from that shared stream, so a model's parameter draws depend on which models precede it; line 68 assigns seed = seed_base + n_total with n_total counting globally across models (incremented at line 108 even on errors), so seeds also depend on subset/order. populate_refs.py:44-54 replicates the pattern (shared rng at 44, sequential LHS at 52, seed = seed_base + n_total at 54). No caller or test pins the model subset/order or resets the RNG per model, so the defect is not mitigated. The auditor's caveat is accurate: insert_run records each row's seed and params, so stored rows remain auditable/replayable; only the population procedure lacks per-model reproducibility (subset re-runs or REGISTRY additions cannot reproduce a prior full run's rows for later models). Severity 'minor' is calibrated: genuine reproducibility weakness in an exploratory atlas-population script, but no wrong results and rows are self-describing.

findings_verified.title: Registry fingerprint smoke test accepts up to 5 of 9 features being NaN/inf
findings_verified.detail: FEATURE_NAMES has 9 entries (v4 layout), but the acceptance criterion is np.isfinite(fp).sum() >= 4. A model whose returns silently break kurtosis, hill_tail_index, leverage, acf_absret_decay and agg_kurt_decay (5 features) still passes, so a regression in series_for_fingerprint or in a model's output scaling that NaNs out most of the fingerprint would not be caught before the long populate run — the exact failure mode the docstring says this test exists to catch. The threshold looks like it dates from a 6-D fingerprint (the docstring still says '6-vector').
findings_verified.evidence:
"""Goal: every REGISTRY model produces a finite 6-vector..."""
if not (np.isfinite(fp).sum() >= 4 and len(fp) == len(FEATURE_NAMES)):
findings_verified.suggestion: Tighten per-model: require all 9 features finite for returns-kind models, and explicitly whitelist the features that are legitimately NaN for attendance-kind series (e.g. compute_hill=False paths). Update the stale '6-vector' docstring.
findings_verified.verify_note: tests/test_fingerprint_atlas.py:40 checks np.isfinite(fp).sum() >= 4 while FEATURE_NAMES (packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint.py:19-32) has 9 entries in the v4 layout; the docstring (line 3) still says "6-vector". So a model/series regression that NaNs out 5 of 9 fingerprint features (e.g. kurtosis, hill_tail_index, leverage, and both v4 decay features) still passes the smoke test, defeating its stated purpose of catching series_for_fingerprint/MODEL_BOUNDS regressions before the long populate run. No other test or caller enforces stricter finiteness. Mitigating context: some features (Hill/decay fits) can be legitimately NaN on the tiny smoke-length series, so a slack threshold is deliberate, but it was evidently calibrated for the old 6-D fingerprint and never rescaled. Test-strength issue only; no production code misbehaves. Severity minor is correct.

findings_verified.title: ingest() orchestration (weekly scheduled CI job) has zero test coverage and no injection seam; also O(n^2) DB reload per paper
findings_verified.detail: test_arxiv_ingest.py covers extract_paper_structured (dry-run), coercions and DB round-trips, but the ingest() loop that the ingest_arxiv.yml cron job actually executes — new/skip/drop counting, already-extracted detection, error accumulation, min_relevance_to_keep gating — is untested because query_arxiv() is called unconditionally and there is no way to inject a papers list. The skip/drop logic reads the entire literature table via load_literature(db_path) inside the per-paper loop (lines 231, 246-249), which is O(n_papers * n_db) and will degrade as the weekly snapshot accumulates. A regression here only surfaces as a broken Sunday cron run.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add a `papers: list[dict] | None = None` parameter (dry-run seam mirroring dry_run_response) so tests can drive ingest() end-to-end against a temp DB, asserting the summary counters and that low-relevance papers keep metadata but skip extraction. Hoist a single load_literature() (or an arxiv_id -> extracted map via one SQL query) out of the loop.
findings_verified.verify_note: All claims check out against the code. (1) Coverage gap: tests/test_arxiv_ingest.py contains 9 tests (schema idempotency, upsert idempotency, extract_paper_structured dry-run, coercions, extraction round-trips, load filters, corpus summary) and none call ingest(); grep for "ingest(" in the test file returns nothing. (2) ingest() at packages/fingerprint_atlas/fingerprint_atlas/arxiv_ingest.py:222 calls query_arxiv(query, ...) unconditionally with no papers-list parameter, so the new/skip/drop counting (lines 226-252), error accumulation (254-260), and min_relevance_to_keep gating (263-269) can only be exercised via network or monkeypatching, which no test does. (3) O(n_papers * n_db) is real: load_literature(db_path) is called inside the per-paper loop at line 231, and the resulting full-table list is linearly scanned twice (line 232 and lines 246-249) per paper. (4) The path is live and scheduled: .github/workflows/ingest_arxiv.yml runs `... ingest $ARGS` (line 106) as the weekly cron job, so regressions surface only as broken scheduled runs. One nitpick: "no injection seam" is slightly overstated — a test could monkeypatch fingerprint_atlas.arxiv_ingest.query_arxiv — but that doesn't change the finding: coverage is absent today. Severity "minor" is correctly calibrated: it's a testing/performance-hygiene gap in the literature-pipeline tooling, not in canonical research code, and the O(n^2) reload is per-paper full-table reads on a slowly growing SQLite table (correctness is unaffected; upsert keeps re-runs safe).

findings_verified.title: Provenance test writes into the repository working directory instead of tmp_path; cleanup only on success
findings_verified.detail: test_emit_prov_record_structure_backward_compat passes prov_dir="prov_test_tmp" (a cwd-relative path), creating a directory inside the repo checkout when pytest runs from the root, and removes it with shutil.rmtree only after all assertions pass. Any assertion failure leaves prov_test_tmp/ debris in the working tree (and in a read-only or parallel-execution environment the test itself can fail spuriously). The sibling test in the same file already uses the tmp_path fixture correctly. Related hygiene in the same suite family: many fingerprint_atlas tests create NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) DBs that are never deleted, and both YH006 invariant tests blanket-suppress UserWarning at import time.
findings_verified.evidence:
rec = emit_prov("act", {"id": "e_in"}, {"id": "e_out"}, prov_dir="prov_test_tmp")
...
import shutil
shutil.rmtree("prov_test_tmp", ignore_errors=True)
findings_verified.suggestion: Use the tmp_path fixture (prov_dir=str(tmp_path)) as the first test in the file already does; drop the manual rmtree. Consider a shared _tmpdb fixture with finalizer for the delete=False temp DBs.
findings_verified.verify_note: tests/test_provenance.py:28 passes prov_dir="prov_test_tmp" to emit_prov, which does os.makedirs(prov_dir) and writes a JSON file relative to cwd (packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py:36,55-56). Cleanup via shutil.rmtree happens only at line 40-41, after all assertions, so any assertion failure leaves prov_test_tmp/ debris in the repo root; there is no conftest chdir/autouse fixture and no .gitignore entry for prov_test_tmp. The sibling test (line 14) correctly uses tmp_path, confirming the intended pattern. Severity minor is correct: test hygiene only, no impact on research results.

findings_verified.title: MMFCN-artifact gate verdict (H_artifact_negated / H_artifact_mmfcn) decided from n=2 seeds with zero uncertainty quantification
findings_verified.detail: classify_mmfcn_artifact in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH006_1/code/aggregate_mmfcn_sensitivity.py issues a categorical MMFCN-artifact gate verdict (ε≤0.3 "negated strong" / ε≥0.7 "refactor") from n=2 seeds (DEFAULT_SEEDS=[1000,1001], line 45) with no uncertainty quantification on the elasticity itself — only n_rt min/max are aggregated. The estimate is threshold-proximate: the run's ε(4x)=0.254 gates the verdict while the code's own ε(2x)=0.313 sits just above the 0.3 cut, so the "strong" label is fragile. Impact is limited in practice: the sweep is seed-paired across settings, and per-seed paired elasticities (0.2530 vs 0.2541) are nearly identical, so paired seed noise on ε is empirically small (the large 0.08–0.43 range only arises from unpaired cross-seed ratios the design never uses); n=2 was a documented design tradeoff in stage_S5.6_plan.md; all per-trial values are surfaced in S5.6_summary_for_diff.json and stage_S5.6_diff.md; and the plan makes the S6-GO decision an explicit human (Yuito) judgment integrating S5.5. Recommended fix: report per-seed paired elasticities and a min/max or jackknife band on ε alongside the verdict, and/or add seeds before citing "H_artifact_negated_strong" in any publication-facing claim.
findings_verified.evidence:
DEFAULT_SEEDS = [1000, 1001]
...
elasticity_4x = float(np.log(ratio_4x) / np.log(4.0)) ...
if elasticity_4x <= 0.3: return ("H_artifact_negated_strong", ...)
findings_verified.suggestion: Run >= 10 seeds per setting (LOB T=1500 trials are cheap relative to the aggregate runs), report a bootstrap or per-seed range for ε, and require the whole interval to clear the threshold before the verdict; otherwise return 'inconclusive'.
findings_verified.verify_note: Code behaves as claimed: aggregate_mmfcn_sensitivity.py line 45 has DEFAULT_SEEDS=[1000,1001] (mmfcn_sensitivity.py defaults --seeds "1000,1001"), and classify_mmfcn_artifact (lines 214-284) issues a categorical gate verdict (H_artifact_negated_strong at ε≤0.3 / H_artifact_mmfcn at ε≥0.7) from two-trial means with no CI, no dispersion of ε itself, and only n_rt min/max reported. The actual run (logs/S5.6_summary_for_diff.json) produced ε(4x)=0.254 → "H_artifact_negated_strong ... S6 進行可", and S6 did proceed. Threshold proximity is real: the code's own ε(2x)=0.313 lands just above the same 0.3 cut, so the categorical "strong" label is fragile. However, severity is miscalibrated. The auditor's failure scenario (two-seed mean ratio moving ε across the threshold) is weaker than claimed because seeds are paired across settings (common random numbers): the per-seed paired elasticities are 0.2530 and 0.2541 — nearly identical — so paired seed noise on ε is empirically tiny; the 0.08–0.43 spread only arises from cross-seed mixing that the paired design never does. Additionally, n=2 was a documented deliberate design choice (stage_S5.6_plan.md: "2 trial で min/max を取って range を出す... 判定誤りリスク" mitigation), all per-trial values are surfaced in the summary JSON and stage_S5.6_diff.md table, and the plan makes the final S6-GO/refactor decision a human (Yuito) judgment integrating S5.5 — the script's verdict is advisory, not an unattended gate. This is a one-off stage-gating script in the imported research line, not canonical package code. Genuine methodological weakness, low practical impact given the observed data and human-in-the-loop process: minor, not major.

findings_verified.title: kurtosis_windowed at window=640 uses only ~78 aggregated observations — a sample-kurtosis estimate that is essentially noise, reported without any SE
findings_verified.detail: Same defect, corrected arithmetic: all real callers use the SG baseline T=20000 (not 50000), giving only 31 block sums at window=640 and 78 at window=256. Gaussian-case SE of excess kurtosis at n=31 is sqrt(24/31) ≈ 0.88, so the recorded single-seed kurt_640 = -0.403 (tests/test_sg_parity.py) is statistically indistinguishable from 0 — the aggregational-Gaussianity reading at the largest windows is noise-dominated. The summary dict (core.py:121-128) reports these floats with no n_blocks or SE. The fixed-seed 5% parity test is a valid deterministic regression check and unaffected.
findings_verified.evidence:
kurt_windows: tuple[int, ...] = (1, 16, 64, 256, 640),
...
"kurt": {w: kurtosis_windowed(returns, w) for w in kurt_windows},  # n_blocks = 50000//640 = 78
findings_verified.suggestion: Report n_blocks per window in the summary dict, and either cap windows so n_blocks >= 500 or add a block-bootstrap SE; for cross-model tables prefer windows <= 100 at T=50k.
findings_verified.verify_note: The code behaves as claimed: stylized_facts_summary (packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py:112-128) defaults to kurt_windows=(1,16,64,256,640) and kurtosis_windowed (lines 95-109) computes a plain sample excess kurtosis on the non-overlapping block sums with no n_blocks or SE reported anywhere in the returned dict. However, the auditor's arithmetic is wrong in a way that strengthens, not weakens, the finding: the canonical SG baseline uses T=20000 (BASELINE_PARAMS in packages/abm_models/abm_models/sg/__init__.py:23, and every actual caller — experiments/speculation_game/baseline.py, tests/test_sg_parity.py, imported YH005 baseline — runs T=20000, not 50000). That yields 20000//640 = 31 blocks at window=640 and 78 at window=256, so the Gaussian-case SE at the largest window is sqrt(24/31) ≈ 0.88, worse than the ~0.55 claimed. The recorded finding kurt_640 = -0.40342 (tests/test_sg_parity.py:26) is therefore within one SE of zero even under Gaussianity, and a single-seed value is essentially noise for the aggregational-Gaussianity claim. Mitigations checked: the parity test compares against a pinned value at the same fixed seed (deterministic regression check, so it is not flaky and does not launder the statistical weakness into a wrong test), and no caller averages across seeds or attaches SE/n. So the defect is real but limited: it under-powers an interpretive claim rather than corrupting canonical simulation results — severity minor is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: CCDF normalization divides by std(|r|), not std(r), while figures label the axis |r|/σ — a silent scale inconsistency
findings_verified.detail: ccdf() computes ax = |x| then s = ax.std() — the standard deviation of the absolute values, not of the returns. For a Gaussian, std(|r|) ≈ 0.60·std(r), and the ratio varies with the distribution's shape, so the rescaling differs across models by a shape-dependent factor. compare_three_models.py:155 labels the x-axis '|r|/σ', which any reader interprets as return volatility σ. The docstring acknowledges a deliberate 'scale-only' choice but does not note that the scale is std(|r|). Tail-shape comparisons are unaffected (pure horizontal shift on log axes), but any quantitative reading of 'events beyond kσ' from these figures is wrong by the model-dependent factor std(r)/std(|r|).
findings_verified.suggestion: Use s = x.std() (std of signed returns) for the normalization, or relabel the axis |r|/std(|r|).
findings_verified.verify_note: packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py lines 65-69: ccdf() computes ax = np.abs(x) then s = ax.std(), i.e. the standard deviation of |r|, not of r. For mean-zero returns std(|r|) = sqrt(E[r^2] - E[|r|]^2), which is ~0.603*std(r) for a Gaussian and varies with kurtosis, so each model's CCDF is rescaled by a different shape-dependent factor. The consumer imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/compare_three_models.py:147-157 calls ccdf(r, normalize=True) and labels the axes "$|r| / \sigma$" and "P(|r|/\sigma >= x)", which any reader interprets as return volatility sigma. The docstring (lines 60-61) says only "scale-only" normalization and never states the scale is std(|r|). Identical code exists in imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/analysis.py and playground/assets/sf.py; no caller or test corrects or guards the discrepancy. Severity is correctly minor: on log-log axes this is a pure horizontal shift, so tail-exponent/shape comparisons (the function's stated purpose) are unaffected; only quantitative axis readings of "events beyond k sigma" are biased by a model-dependent factor of roughly 1.2-1.7x.

findings_verified.title: provenance package is a prov_record stub, but spec declares the provenance core complete and promises PROV-O + L2 ctx integration
findings_verified.detail: Docs-drift confirmed: specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md's 進捗サマリ declares 正準コア完成 for packages/provenance, but the spec's own definition of that package (target structure comment, mapping table lines 89-90, task T1) is "PROV-O(PRISM) + L2 ctx(PROV-ABM) + prov_record(cap-alloc)", and only the third component was extracted — the package is the single emit_prov function (packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py, __all__ = ["emit_prov"]). imported/PRISM/src/prism/provenance/tracker.py and imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/provabm/{ctx,capture,reach,validator,provenance}.py remain unextracted. tests/test_provenance.py exercises only emit_prov write/read + key-structure compat, so the spec's "round-trip + 後方互換 GREEN" claim is literally true but does not support "完成". Additionally, the pyproject description and docstring "W3C PROV-O 記録の共有実装" overstate compliance: the emitted record is ad-hoc JSON with prov:-prefixed keys (flat "prov:used"/"prov:wasGeneratedBy" dicts, no @context, no qualified terms), not a valid PROV-JSON serialization. Severity reduced to minor: pure documentation inconsistency with no runtime misbehavior; T1 is still listed as an open task in the same spec, limiting the chance the researcher permanently skips the integration. Suggested fix: qualify the 進捗サマリ line (e.g. "packages/provenance (prov_record のみ / T1 の PROV-O + L2 ctx は未了)") and soften the pyproject/docstring wording, or complete T1.
findings_verified.evidence: packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py: `__all__ = ["emit_prov"]` — the entire package is one function. spec 001: 'packages/provenance/  # PROV-O(PRISM) + L2 ctx(PROV-ABM) + prov_record(cap-alloc)' vs 進捗サマリ '正準コア完成: …packages/provenance'. Unextracted sources: imported/PRISM/src/prism/provenance/tracker.py, imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/provabm/ctx.py.
findings_verified.suggestion: Either re-scope the progress summary to 'provenance = prov_record shim only; PROV-O + L2 ctx pending T1/intervention_atlas spec', or extract the two remaining components. Rename the misleading 'W3C PROV-O' description until the record format actually conforms.
findings_verified.verify_note:
Every factual element of the finding checks out on inspection. (1) packages/provenance/provenance/__init__.py is exactly one 60-line function `emit_prov` with `__all__ = ["emit_prov"]` (line 20) — the cap-alloc prov_record promotion only. (2) specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md's 進捗サマリ (line ~14-15) states 正準コア完成 including `packages/provenance`, while the same spec's target-structure comment ("PROV-O(PRISM) + L2 ctx(PROV-ABM) + prov_record(cap-alloc)"), the mapping table (lines 89-90: PROV-ABM-atlas → packages/provenance/(L2 ctx), PRISM → packages/provenance/(PROV-O)), and task T1 all define the package as a three-component integration. Two of the three components exist unextracted: imported/PRISM/src/prism/provenance/tracker.py ("W3C PROV-O minimal implementation") and imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/provabm/{ctx,capture,reach,validator,provenance}.py. (3) tests/test_provenance.py only round-trips emit_prov JSON and asserts key structure — so the spec's "provenance round-trip + 後方互換" parity claim is itself accurate, but the "完成" claim is not. (4) The pyproject description and module docstring ("W3C PROV-O 記録の共有実装") do overstate: the emitted record is ad-hoc JSON with prov:-prefixed keys (e.g. "prov:used" as a flat {activity, entity} dict), not valid PROV-JSON (no @context, no bundle/qualified-term structure), matching the finding's critique.

Refutation angles considered and rejected: the spec header says "Stage B 進行中" and T1 remains listed as a task, which could be read as acknowledging incompleteness — but the 進捗サマリ explicitly and unambiguously lists packages/provenance under 正準コア完成, which contradicts the spec's own definition of that package. No test or caller mitigates the drift.

Severity calibration: this is documentation drift only — no code misbehaves, no results are wrong, and the one implemented function is correct and tested. The impact is that the ground-truth spec could mislead the researcher into believing T1 (PROV-O + L2 ctx integration) is done, and the PROV-O compliance claim overstates interoperability of records shared with the capital-allocation repo. That is genuine but low-impact for a solo researcher with T1 still visible in the task list, so "minor" fits better than "major" (which is defined as a real defect likely to bite).

findings_verified.title: README declares Stage B '未着手' (not started) although Stage B is merged to main with 4 packages, 8 models and core-importing experiments
findings_verified.detail: README.md line 19 declares Stage B '未着手' (not started), but Stage B is merged to main (merge commit 8711417, PR #2 stage-b-sg-backbone): packages/{abm_models (8 models + base protocol), stylized_facts, provenance, fingerprint_atlas} exist and experiments/{classical,speculation_game}/baseline.py import the cores. This contradicts specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md line 3 ('Stage B 進行中'). Related staleness: spec line 13 still says the Stage B branch is un-pushed ('GitHub 未 push=トークン要') though it is merged; README line 12 describes speculation-game-info as 'YH001-006_1' while the imported tree contains YH007 and YH008. Pure docs drift — no runtime impact, and the spec (the README's own declared ground truth) is mostly correct — hence minor rather than major.
findings_verified.evidence: README.md: '**Stage B — core 抽出と再編(未着手)**: `imported/` の中身を以下へ再配置する。' vs `git log`: '8711417 Merge pull request #2 from yuitokyouni/stage-b-sg-backbone' and existing packages/abm_models/abm_models/{sg,cont_bouchaud,…,franke_westerhoff}/.
findings_verified.suggestion: Update README status to mirror spec 001's progress summary (Stage B partially complete: 8 models canonical, parity GREEN, market/regimes/intervention_atlas/llm_abm pending), and refresh the stale '未 push' line in the spec.
findings_verified.verify_note: Every factual element of the finding checks out. (1) README.md line 19 reads '**Stage B — core 抽出と再編(未着手)**' (verified by reading the file). (2) Stage B is in fact merged: `git log --merges` shows commit 8711417 'Merge pull request #2 from yuitokyouni/stage-b-sg-backbone', which is an ancestor of the current history. (3) packages/ contains abm_models (with 8 model subpackages: sg, cont_bouchaud, chiarella_iori, franke_westerhoff, gcmg, lux_marchesi, minority_game, zero_intelligence, plus base.py), stylized_facts, provenance, and fingerprint_atlas. (4) experiments/classical/baseline.py and experiments/speculation_game/baseline.py exist. (5) specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md line 3 says 'Stage B 進行中', directly contradicting the README, and its line 13 still claims the stage-b branch is 'GitHub 未 push=トークン要' although the merge is on main. (6) imported/speculation-game-info/experiments contains YH007 and YH008 while the README table (line 12) still says 'YH001-006_1'. So the README, designated entry document, contradicts the spec (the declared ground truth), the git history, and the actual tree. However, the severity is miscalibrated: this is pure documentation staleness in a solo-researcher repo — it produces no wrong results, corrupts no data, and the spec (which the README itself points to as ground truth) carries the correct status. Per the given definitions (major = real defect likely to bite), a stale status line that any reader can falsify with `ls packages/` is genuine but low-impact: minor.

findings_verified.title: AC6's promised verification (`git log --follow`) returns nothing on imported files; history is only reachable through the subtree merge parent
findings_verified.detail: AC6: 'THE SYSTEM SHALL 移植元の git コミット系譜を `git log --follow` で辿れる状態にする'. Empirically, `git log --follow --oneline -- imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/model.py` prints nothing at all (and the non---follow form shows only the subtree-add merge commit 29c649a). The upstream lineage IS preserved in the DAG (the merge parent aad2c74 carries the original commits; `git log --oneline --all` shows the imported repos' PR history), so the substantive provenance requirement holds, but the acceptance criterion's literal check fails because subtree merges keep upstream paths (experiments/YH005/model.py) that --follow cannot connect across the prefix rename. Anyone re-verifying AC6 as written will conclude history was lost.
findings_verified.evidence: $ git log --follow --oneline -- imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/model.py → (empty); $ git log --oneline -- <same path> → '29c649a Add imported/speculation-game-info/ from commit aad2c745…' only.
findings_verified.suggestion: Amend AC6 to the working recipe (e.g. `git log --oneline <subtree-merge-parent> -- experiments/YH005/model.py`, or `git log --all --oneline -- <old path>`), and note in README that --follow does not cross the subtree prefix.
findings_verified.verify_note: Reproduced exactly as claimed. specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md line ~169 (AC6) states history must be traceable via `git log --follow`. Running `git log --follow --oneline -- imported/speculation-game-info/experiments/YH005/model.py` returns empty output; the non-follow form shows only the subtree-add merge 29c649a. Meanwhile the upstream lineage IS intact: `git log 29c649a^2 -- experiments/YH005/model.py` reaches the original commits (e.g. ef77e7f), because subtree merges keep the original un-prefixed paths, which --follow cannot connect across the prefix. So the substantive provenance requirement is satisfied but the acceptance criterion's literal verification method fails, and anyone re-checking AC6 as written would wrongly conclude history was lost. This is documentation drift in the spec (the check should reference the merge parent, `git log --all`, or `git log -m --follow`), not a data-loss defect. Severity minor is correctly calibrated.

findings_verified.title: stylized_facts is the YH005 analysis promotion only, not the promised 'SF battery 統一 (PRISM + PROV-ABM の定義を統合)'
findings_verified.detail: spec §3.1/§4 define packages/stylized_facts as the unification of PRISM's and PROV-ABM's SF batteries ('SF battery 統一 (PRISM + PROV-ABM の定義を統合)'), and the progress summary counts it among the completed 正準コア. The actual package docstring says 'YH005: Stylized facts 計算関数' and contains exactly the 5 Cont-(2001) items YH005 needed plus YH005_1 plotting helpers; PRISM's SF estimators (which the future intervention_atlas experiments per spec T5 will need for fact-delta scoring) remain only in imported/PRISM. This is defensible as T0.3 ('最小 SF 抽出') but the unqualified '完成' claim and the package description '統一実装' overstate what exists — a reader would assume PRISM SF definitions are already reconciled with YH005's (they use different anchors, which R1 flags as a parity risk).
findings_verified.evidence: packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py line 1: '"""YH005: Stylized facts 計算関数.' vs spec §4: 'stylized_facts/  # SF battery 統一 (PRISM + PROV-ABM の定義を統合)' and pyproject description 'Cont (2001) stylized facts 計算 (統一実装)'.
findings_verified.suggestion: Qualify the progress summary ('YH005 SF subset; PRISM SF merge deferred to intervention_atlas spec') or track the SF-definition reconciliation as an explicit open task, since anchor differences are a known parity risk (spec R1).
findings_verified.verify_note: All cited evidence checks out. (1) packages/stylized_facts/stylized_facts/core.py line 1 docstring is '"""YH005: Stylized facts 計算関数.' and the package contains only the 5 Cont-(2001) items YH005 needed (log_returns_from_prices, ACF helpers, ccdf/Hill, windowed kurtosis) — the package dir has just core.py and __init__.py. (2) specs/001-monorepo-consolidation.md line 119 describes the package as 'SF battery 統一 (PRISM + PROV-ABM の定義を統合)' and line 14 counts packages/stylized_facts among the '正準コア完成'; pyproject.toml line 4 says '(統一実装)'. (3) The PRISM SF battery still lives only at imported/PRISM/src/prism/facts/estimators.py and PROV-ABM's at imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/toy/sf_battery.py — neither is imported by nor reconciled into the package (only experiments/ and tests import stylized_facts is false: nothing imports the imported SF code from packages). The spec itself scopes the delivered work as T0.3 '最小 SF 抽出' (line 183), so this is genuinely docs-drift/overclaim, not a code defect: no computation is wrong, and the parity claims that were tested (SG rel.err, classical bit-identical) do not depend on the missing unification. Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated — it can mislead a future reader (especially for the planned intervention_atlas fact-delta scoring), but produces no wrong results today.

findings_verified.title: imported/ trees carry no frozen/canonical-pointer signage although YH001-005 code is now duplicated in packages/ and imported CLAUDE.md files still instruct active workflows
findings_verified.detail: AC7's 'README に統合先を明記' was satisfied on the OLD GitHub repos (verified: descriptions read '[Consolidated→financial-abm-lab] imported/<name>', archived=True for all 5). But inside the monorepo, the imported/ copies read as live standalone projects: imported/speculation-game-info/README.md presents YH001-005 as the implementations (their model.py files are now byte-duplicated in packages/abm_models with the canonical status declared only in root-level docs); imported/ABM-Microstructure/CLAUDE.md still instructs an active spec→plan→tasks workflow and its speckit skills are loaded into this monorepo's skill list, steering any agent working under that directory by stale ground truth ('spec: specs/…', '計画パッケージ: src/microstructure…コードは未実装'); imported/PROV-ABM-atlas/CLAUDE.md likewise. Since YH006-008 development intentionally continues inside imported/speculation-game-info (spec scope decision), the risk of editing a duplicated YH001-005/model.py there instead of the canonical copy is real. Mitigation exists — tests/test_classical_parity.py and test_prism_family_parity.py dynamically import the imported/ files, so divergence for the covered models would fail the suite — but that guard is undocumented as a freeze contract, and the SG reference (YH005 model.py/simulate.py) is guarded only by the slow-excluded findings test.
findings_verified.evidence: imported/speculation-game-info/README.md has no mention of packages/ or 正準; imported/ABM-Microstructure/CLAUDE.md: 'spec → plan → tasks → implement の順(/speckit-*)。spec に無いものを勝手に作らない。' — active-development instructions in a consolidated tree.
findings_verified.suggestion: Add a one-line banner to each imported/<repo>/README.md and CLAUDE.md: 'Consolidated snapshot — canonical YH001-005/CI/ZI/FW implementations live in packages/abm_models; parity tests pin this copy; only YH006+ is actively developed here.'
findings_verified.verify_note: All factual claims verified read-only.
